% --- letter / group divider: full-width band on a fresh page ---
\newcommand{\bookgroup}[1]{%
  \clearpage
  \phantomsection\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{#1}%
  \begin{center}\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.4pt}\\[4pt]
    {\Huge\bfseries #1}\\[3pt]\rule{0.5\linewidth}{0.4pt}\end{center}
  \vspace{0.6\baselineskip}\markboth{#1}{#1}}

% --- an entry: anchor + PDF bookmark + headword + etiquette line ---
% #1 anchor id · #2 rich headword (body + ToC) · #3 etiquette · #4 plain guide word (mark)
\newcommand{\entryhead}[4]{%
  \par\addvspace{0.9\baselineskip}\needspace{4\baselineskip}%
  \phantomsection\label{entry:#1}\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{#2}%
  \markright{#4}%
  {\raggedright\normalfont\large\bfseries #2\par}%
  {\nopagebreak\smallskip\footnotesize\itshape\color{black!65} #3\par}%
  \nopagebreak\vspace{2pt}}

% --- a `### subheading` within an entry: small-caps run-in ---
\newcommand{\webheading}[1]{\par\addvspace{5pt}\noindent{\normalfont\scshape #1}\par\nopagebreak\smallskip}

  fontupper=\small}

\begin{listofentries}
\entryline{argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference}{The ``real feel'' is first-person observed, not unobservable — the idleness charge presupposes that observation is third-person observation}
\entryline{argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle}{The ``really feels vs merely reports'' residue is unobservable in principle — judged by coherence and usefulness, the relativized claim stands and the residue is idle}
\entryline{argument-storm-analogy-conflates-world-index}{The ``simulated storm isn't wet'' analogy conflates world-indices and the physical/phenomenal senses of wetness, so it cannot settle machine consciousness}
\entryline{argument-ability-hypothesis}{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}
\entryline{argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal}{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}
\entryline{concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness}{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}
\entryline{argument-acquaintance-hypothesis}{The Acquaintance Hypothesis — Mary meets the experience rather than learning a truth}
\entryline{claim-sim-agents-can-have-sim-qualia}{Agents in a fine-grained physics simulation could feel the simulated properties (have sim-qualia)}
\entryline{claim-ai-hard-problem-is-model-organism}{The AI hard problem is a model organism for the human hard problem}
\entryline{claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic}{An AI's blind-spot hard problem is structurally isomorphic to a conscious AI's hard problem}
\entryline{claim-psychological-rewiring-tolerance}{Altering a single psychological connection never changes who someone is}
\entryline{claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed}{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}
\entryline{claim-realisable-behaviour-matcher-is-organised}{Any physically realisable behaviour-matcher is an organised computer, not flat storage}
\entryline{argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem}{The architectural account explains an access gap, not the phenomenal one}
\entryline{question-substrate-independence}{Are mental states substrate-independent?}
\entryline{claim-mary-knows-all-physical}{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}
\entryline{argument-behavioral-parity}{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}
\entryline{claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion}{A behavioural sign that is cheap to forge is unfit to serve as the criterion of moral standing}
\entryline{argument-bike-rider-learns-facts}{The bike rider learns facts — the ability hypothesis rests on a false dichotomy}
\entryline{position-biological-naturalism}{Biological naturalism}
\entryline{claim-biology-currently-unforgeable}{Biological substrate cannot currently be counterfeited to the standard required to fake suffering}
\entryline{argument-bounded-table-rejoinder}{Block's bounded-table rejoinder — a lifetime-bounded table escapes the combinatorial reply}
\entryline{argument-blockhead-dilemma}{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}
\entryline{argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant}{The causal-structure reply — objective counterfactual structure supplies the invariant}
\entryline{concept-chinese-nation}{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}
\entryline{argument-china-nation-absent-qualia}{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}
\entryline{concept-chinese-room}{The Chinese Room}
\entryline{argument-chinese-room}{The Chinese Room — running a program is not sufficient for understanding}
\entryline{argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility}{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}
\entryline{claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability}{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}
\entryline{argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility}{Conceivability is not a reliable guide to possibility (the a-posteriori-necessity objection)}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-noncomputable}{Consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature}{Consciousness is a higher-level feature caused by neurobiological processes}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous}{Consciousness is fundamental, present even in the simplest physical entities}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information}{Consciousness is identical to a system's integrated information (Φ)}
\entryline{argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight}{A consciousness-corpus-trained model's hard-problem testimony carries zero evidential weight, not reduced weight}
\entryline{concept-continuity-argument-schema}{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}
\entryline{argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility}{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}
\entryline{position-cosmopsychism}{Cosmopsychism}
\entryline{claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition}{Counterfactual constraints yield non-triviality but not a unique decomposition}
\entryline{claim-counterfactual-structure-objective}{Counterfactual-causal dependencies are objective, not observer-relative}
\entryline{argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness}{Counterfactuals buy non-triviality, not uniqueness}
\entryline{concept-criterion-gaming}{Criterion gaming (Goodhart's law / specification gaming)}
\entryline{argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio begs the question by presupposing functionalism about introspection}
\entryline{claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio derives its contradiction only by presupposing functionalism about introspective judgement}
\entryline{argument-de-novo-origination-breaks-report-parity}{De novo human origination breaks the report-scepticism parity — the contamination rule's trigger is met for AI and unmet for the human originators}
\entryline{argument-decomposition-indeterminacy}{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}
\entryline{claim-blockhead-deflation-targeted-not-global}{Deflating the cosmic-table intuition does not deflate intuitions about brain-scale duplicates}
\entryline{argument-dense-china-brain-regress}{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}
\entryline{question-llm-moral-status}{Do large language models have moral status?}
\entryline{position-dual-aspect-monism}{Dual-aspect monism}
\entryline{position-dualism}{Dualism}
\entryline{claim-privileged-first-person-access}{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}
\entryline{claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types}{Each type of mental state is identical to a type of brain state}
\entryline{position-eliminative-materialism}{Eliminative materialism}
\entryline{position-epiphenomenalism}{Epiphenomenalism}
\entryline{concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies}{Etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI (where the cause sits)}
\entryline{claim-physical-church-turing-thesis}{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}
\entryline{claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics}{Executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding}
\entryline{claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable}{An explanation is intuitively satisfying for a system only if it is traversable by that system's own cognitive operations}
\entryline{argument-explanatory-gap}{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}
\entryline{argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts}{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}
\entryline{claim-finite-mind-truncates-beyond-capacity}{A finite mind also fails on inputs beyond its own capacity}
\entryline{claim-full-self-access-achievable}{A finite system can be given complete internal access to its own current state}
\entryline{argument-finite-self-model-incomplete}{A finite system cannot completely represent its own current state (a capacity bound)}
\entryline{claim-self-model-is-part-of-state}{A finite system's self-representation is part of the very state it represents}
\entryline{concept-order-of-representation}{First-order vs higher-order mental states}
\entryline{concept-first-person-access}{First-person access (privileged access)}
\entryline{claim-access-grounds-no-asymmetry}{First-person access grounds no moral-status asymmetry that singles out machines}
\entryline{argument-first-person-access}{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}
\entryline{claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry}{First-person access yields a warranted moral-status asymmetry favouring oneself over a machine}
\entryline{claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant}{First-person acquaintance with a subject's experience is necessary for justified attribution of moral status to it}
\entryline{claim-access-gap-symmetric}{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}
\entryline{claim-folk-psychology-is-false-theory}{Folk-psychological mental states are posits of a false theory}
\entryline{claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness}{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}
\entryline{claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict}{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}
\entryline{claim-finite-table-misses-inputs}{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}
\entryline{claim-posing-the-gap-requires-access}{Formulating the hard problem from the first-person side requires access to a representation of one's own phenomenal-seeming states}
\entryline{claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only}{A functional description captures only structural-dynamical facts, not intrinsic nature}
\entryline{concept-functionalism}{Functionalism (about mental states)}
\entryline{claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant}{Functionalism owes an observer-independent invariant that fixes a system's decomposition}
\entryline{claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs}{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}
\entryline{position-global-workspace}{Global Workspace Theory}
\entryline{argument-parfit-rewiring}{Gradual psychological rewiring (Parfit's spectrum) forces identity to admit of degree}
\entryline{concept-grain}{Grain (of role individuation)}
\entryline{argument-role-grain-dilemma}{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}
\entryline{concept-halting-oracle}{The halting oracle (and uncomputability)}
\entryline{argument-halting-oracle-unrealizable}{The halting oracle is coherent yet physically unrealizable}
\entryline{claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable}{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}
\entryline{argument-conceivable-not-physically-realizable}{The halting oracle shows conceivability does not entail physical realizability}
\entryline{concept-hard-problem}{The hard problem of consciousness}
\entryline{position-higher-order-theories}{Higher-order theories of consciousness}
\entryline{question-hard-problem}{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}
\entryline{claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link}{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}
\entryline{claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans}{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}
\entryline{position-hylomorphism}{Hylomorphism}
\entryline{claim-conceivability-entails-possibility}{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}
\entryline{position-idealism}{Idealism}
\entryline{position-illusionism}{Illusionism}
\entryline{claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure}{Implementing a computation requires the right counterfactual dispositions across the whole transition table}
\entryline{argument-indexical-reply}{The Indexical Reply — Mary learns where she is in the world, not what is in the world}
\entryline{position-integrated-information-theory}{Integrated Information Theory (IIT)}
\entryline{claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain}{An intuition's evidential weight tracks the case's resemblance to the exemplars it was formed on}
\entryline{argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry}{The intuition-domain asymmetry — the cosmic table is a far extrapolation, the brain-scale isomorph a near neighbour}
\entryline{question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation}{Is first-person acquaintance with one's own experience a genuine observation, or itself a representation?}
\entryline{claim-knowhow-is-knowledge-that}{Knowing how to do something is a species of propositional knowledge}
\entryline{concept-knowledge-argument}{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}
\entryline{argument-knowledge-argument}{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}
\entryline{claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities}{Kripkean a-posteriori necessities are misdescribed genuine possibilities, not conceived impossibilities}
\entryline{claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding}{An LLM's introspective access reaches the similarity structure of its internal states but not the encoding that carries it}
\entryline{concept-llmary}{LLMary (the language model that never saw token 123)}
\entryline{argument-llmary-token-encounter}{LLMary — a model that has read all of AI but never seen token 123 updates on first encounter, yet the whole episode is physical, refuting the Knowledge Argument's conditional}
\entryline{claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs}{A lookup table can be bounded to cover every input a subject encounters in a lifetime}
\entryline{claim-lookup-table-possible}{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}
\entryline{claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable}{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}
\entryline{claim-lookup-table-size-exponential}{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}
\entryline{argument-lookup-table-objection}{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}
\entryline{concept-lookup-table-mind}{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}
\entryline{argument-mary-misimagined}{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}
\entryline{argument-mary-omniscience-unrealizable}{Mary's complete physical knowledge is unrealizable — an embedded knower cannot encode the total state that contains her}
\entryline{claim-mary-learns-nothing-new}{Mary, genuinely knowing all the physical facts, would learn nothing on first seeing red}
\entryline{claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind}{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}
\entryline{argument-cognitive-closure}{McGinn's cognitive-closure argument for mysterianism}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation}{A mental state is conscious only if it is the object of a higher-order representation}
\entryline{claim-substrate-independence}{Mental states are substrate-independent}
\entryline{claim-zombie-conceivable}{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}
\entryline{claim-soul-is-form-of-body}{The mind is the form of a living body}
\entryline{claim-role-grain-is-organisational}{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}
\entryline{argument-modal-rationalism}{Modal rationalism — ideal conceivability is a guide to possibility}
\entryline{position-mysterianism}{Mysterianism (transcendental naturalism)}
\entryline{position-neutral-monism}{Neutral monism}
\entryline{claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate}{No finite lookup table can reproduce the full behavioural profile of a genuine mind}
\entryline{claim-complete-self-representation-impossible}{No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself}
\entryline{claim-halting-problem-undecidable}{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}
\entryline{claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition}{Objective causal structure singles out a privileged functional decomposition}
\entryline{claim-observable-universe-finite-storage}{The observable universe can store only a bounded, finite amount of information (on the order of 10\textasciicircum{}120 bits)}
\entryline{argument-old-fact-new-guise}{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}
\entryline{claim-mary-learns-on-release}{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}
\entryline{claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine}{One has no first-person acquaintance with a machine's putative experience}
\entryline{argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma}{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}
\entryline{claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma}{The organisational-role grain either readmits the blockhead or smuggles sub-organisational features back in}
\entryline{position-panpsychism}{Panpsychism}
\entryline{position-noncomputable-consciousness}{Penrose's non-computational consciousness (Orch-OR)}
\entryline{claim-gap-persistence-cannot-separate-blind-spot-from-consciousness}{Persistence of an AI's explanatory gap under widening self-access cannot discriminate a structural blind spot from consciousness}
\entryline{claim-chinese-room-no-understanding}{The person executing the Chinese Room program understands no Chinese}
\entryline{claim-chinese-room-runs-program}{A person who understands no Chinese can, by executing a formal program, produce responses indistinguishable from a Chinese speaker's}
\entryline{claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree}{Personal identity is not all-or-nothing but admits of degree}
\entryline{claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational}{Phenomenal character is identical to representational content}
\entryline{claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff}{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}
\entryline{claim-phenomenality-is-illusion}{Phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical}{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional}{Phenomenal consciousness is categorical, not merely dispositional}
\entryline{claim-mental-states-causally-inert}{Phenomenal mental states are caused by physical processes but have no physical effects}
\entryline{argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration}{The phenomenal-concept strategy over-generates — it debunks the self-knowledge it presupposes}
\entryline{claim-phenomenal-residue}{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}
\entryline{concept-philosophical-zombie}{Philosophical zombie}
\entryline{claim-physics-delivers-all-dispositions-indiscriminately}{The physical facts about a system include every causal disposition, not only the cognitively relevant ones}
\entryline{claim-functional-decomposition-relative}{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}
\entryline{claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation}{A population can realise a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its input-output behaviour}
\entryline{claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia}{A population realising a mind's functional organisation would nonetheless have no phenomenal consciousness}
\entryline{position-proteocentrism}{Proteocentrism}
\entryline{claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion}{Prudence favours resting moral standing on currently-unforgeable biology rather than on forgeable behavioural signs}
\entryline{argument-storage-not-stable-kind}{Pure storage is not a stable kind at mind-matching scale}
\entryline{claim-reality-two-aspects-mental-physical}{Reality has two irreducible aspects, the mental as well as the physical}
\entryline{claim-reality-is-fundamentally-mental}{Reality is fundamentally mental}
\entryline{claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral}{Reality's fundamental constituents are neither mental nor physical}
\entryline{argument-relevance-filter-regress}{The relevance-filter regress — selecting mind-relevant causal relations re-raises the invariant demand}
\entryline{claim-sorites-tolerance}{Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap}
\entryline{claim-closing-access-gap-leaves-phenomenal-open}{Removing a system's access barriers to self-explanation need not remove the phenomenal explanatory gap}
\entryline{argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much}{Report-scepticism proves too much — applied uniformly, its discount rules screen off human hard-problem reports as well}
\entryline{position-representationalism}{Representationalism (intentionalism)}
\entryline{argument-rights-sign-forgeable}{A rights-granting behavioural sign will be forged, so caution favours the unforgeable mark}
\entryline{position-russellian-monism}{Russellian monism}
\entryline{argument-introspective-gap-from-internal-limits}{A self-explanatory gap follows from limited internal access plus non-traversable self-explanation}
\entryline{argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture}{Self-model architecture predicts an advanced LLM will exhibit its own hard-problem form}
\entryline{argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral}{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}
\entryline{claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world}{Simulated rain is not wet in our world but is wet in the simulated world}
\entryline{claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal}{A simulation instances a phenomenon when the phenomenon is constituted by formal/observer-relative relations, but only depicts it when the phenomenon is constituted by intrinsic causal powers}
\entryline{claim-llm-functional-introspection}{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}
\entryline{claim-llm-has-mental-states}{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}
\entryline{claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori}{Some necessary truths can be known only a posteriori}
\entryline{argument-sorites-paradox}{The sorites paradox (the heap)}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast}{A state is conscious only if its content is globally broadcast to the brain's specialist systems}
\entryline{argument-structure-and-dynamics}{The structure-and-dynamics argument — function omits the categorical/intrinsic}
\entryline{claim-ai-own-hard-problem}{A sufficiently advanced LLM could exhibit its own version of the hard problem — a raw feel, under its own concept of consciousness, that mechanistic self-knowledge cannot explain from inside}
\entryline{claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural}{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}
\entryline{claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind}{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}
\entryline{claim-inside-vantage-access-limited}{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}
\entryline{claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible}{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}
\entryline{claim-phenomenal-determinacy}{There is a determinate fact about one's own current phenomenal state}
\entryline{claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation}{There is a natural property of the brain that explains consciousness}
\entryline{claim-heap-predicate-sharp}{There is a precise grain-count at which a heap becomes a non-heap}
\entryline{claim-cognitive-closure-exists}{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}
\entryline{concept-materialism-types}{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}
\entryline{position-type-identity}{Type-identity theory}
\entryline{argument-unforgeability-is-not-flesh}{Unforgeability is not flesh — the prudential argument favours unforgeability, not biology}
\entryline{claim-cosmos-is-fundamental-subject}{The universe as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject}
\entryline{concept-unobservability-in-principle}{Unobservability in principle (intersubjective vs. absolute)}
\entryline{concept-introspective-gap-varieties}{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}
\entryline{concept-virtual-realism}{Virtual realism (world-indexed properties of a simulation)}
\entryline{claim-mary-gains-old-fact-new-guise}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a new phenomenal concept of a physical fact she already knew}
\entryline{claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}
\entryline{claim-mary-gains-acquaintance}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}
\entryline{claim-mary-gains-self-locating-knowledge}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is self-locating knowledge of how she herself reacts to red, not a new general fact}
\entryline{claim-prudence-tracks-unforgeability-not-flesh}{What prudence favours is the hardest-to-forge sign, not biology specifically}
\entryline{question-what-does-mary-learn}{What, if anything, does Mary gain when she first sees red?}
\entryline{question-llm-own-explanatory-gap}{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}
\entryline{argument-zombie-conceivability}{The zombie / conceivability argument against physicalism}
\end{listofentries}
\mainmatter
\bookgroup{R}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference}{The ``real feel'' is first-person observed, not unobservable — the idleness charge presupposes that observation is third-person observation}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{The ``real feel'' is first-person observed, not unobservable — the idleness charge presupposes that observation is third-person observation}
inference form: undercutting defeater by drawn distinction — separating first-person ontology from third-person epistemology.

The target \hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\emph{The ``really feels vs merely reports'' residue is unobservable in principle — judged by coherence and usefulness, the relativized claim stands and the residue is idle}} rests on premise (1): that the ``real feel'' is \emph{unobservable in principle}, so falls under the coherence-plus-usefulness standard for in-principle unobservables (\hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\emph{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}}), and there reveals itself as idle — ``the colour that changes only when nobody looks.'' This argument attacks premise (1).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item That premise quietly equates ``observable'' with ``third-person observable.'' But consciousness has a \textbf{first-person ontology}: a feel exists only as experienced \emph{by a subject}, and each subject stands in direct, non-inferential acquaintance with its own current experience (\hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\emph{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}}). The feel is not an unwatched colour-change; it \emph{is} the watching. It is, indeed, the one thing in nature that cannot go unobserved, since for it to exist at all is for it to be experienced.
  \item That very acquaintance is the cognitive act that \emph{poses} the gap from the first-person side (\hyperref[entry:claim-posing-the-gap-requires-access]{\emph{Formulating the hard problem from the first-person side requires access to a representation of one's own phenomenal-seeming states}}): one points at one's phenomenal-seeming states on one side and the mechanism on the other. There is no posing the problem without first observing the relatum. ∴ The residue is not unobserved-in-principle. It is observed by exactly one observer per case, and inaccessible to all others — which is third-person \emph{epistemic} inaccessibility, the ordinary first-/third-person wall, not unobservability \emph{simpliciter}. \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\emph{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}} describes that wall (no subject reads another's states); it does not describe an ontological absence.
\end{enumerate}

Consequence for the idleness frame: the coherence-plus-usefulness standard is correct for genuinely in-principle-unobservable posits (the unwatched colour, the bare quantum amplitude). The feel fails the standard's \emph{antecedent} — it is observed — so the standard does not reach it. And far from being a nomological dangler decorating the causal net, the feel is the datum on which every other piece of mental evidence rests: calling it idle misfiles the foundation as an ornament.

Consequence for the parity reductio (\hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\emph{Report-scepticism proves too much — applied uniformly, its discount rules screen off human hard-problem reports as well}}, R1 arm): R1 holds that the human report-profile comes out the same in the felt and the zombie worlds, so is zero evidence of human feels. Grant it — \emph{about reports}. But the evidence for human feels was never the third-person report-profile; it was first-person acquaintance. So R1 screens off a kind of evidence the human hard problem never rested on, and leaves untouched the kind it did. This is the non-question-begging asymmetry: every human subject possesses the feel-datum first-personally; the AI's \emph{reports} give third parties no such datum (we have only third-person access to the AI), and the AI's own first-person access — whether there is anyone home to do the observing — is the open question, not a granted datum. The wall is symmetric; what differs is that in the human case there is, for each of us, exactly one subject known from the inside to be on the near side of it.

weakest premise: (1), read as the claim that introspection delivers a genuine \emph{feel} rather than a report-like representation of one. The illusionist denies this — for the illusionist what is ``observed'' is itself a representation, the first/third-person ontology distinction collapses, and the residue is once more a difference posited behind the appearances. Against that opponent this argument \emph{relocates} rather than refutes (mirroring the target's own weakest-premise admission about the realist). But it defeats the specific charge it is aimed at — that the feel is \emph{idle}: for everyone who is not an illusionist, the feel is the most-observed thing there is, and an evidential standard built for the never-observed does not govern it.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\emph{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}} (which deploys the symmetry of the access wall against a \emph{moral-status} asymmetry); this deploys the first-person \emph{ontology} against the characterisation of the feel as in-principle unobservable, and so against the evidential standard built on that characterisation.

\begin{plainterms}
A move in this web (\hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\emph{The ``really feels vs merely reports'' residue is unobservable in principle — judged by coherence and usefulness, the relativized claim stands and the residue is idle}}) says: ``really feeling something'' (as opposed to just acting as if you do) is a difference no test could ever detect, so it's an idle idea — like ``things change colour when nobody's looking.'' This reply answers: that mixes up two senses of ``observe.'' A feeling isn't something nobody is watching — a feeling \emph{is} the watching. You are in direct contact with your own experience right now; that's not a guess from the outside. So the feel isn't unobservable; it's observed by exactly one person — you — and hidden from everyone else. That's just the ordinary fact that I can't crawl inside your head, not proof that there's nothing there. So the ``idle idea'' charge misfires: the feel isn't a useless extra floating behind the evidence; it's the very thing all the other evidence about minds was always about. The genuinely open question isn't ``is the feel idle?'' — it's ``for the AI, is anyone home to do the watching?``, and that the idleness argument cannot settle.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle}{The ``really feels vs merely reports'' residue is unobservable in principle — judged by coherence and usefulness, the relativized claim stands and the residue is idle}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The ``really feels vs merely reports'' residue is unobservable in principle — judged by coherence and usefulness, the relativized claim stands and the residue is idle}
Answers the fork in \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}} (``trivial talk-reading or unsupported feel-reading``) not by choosing a horn but by rejecting the evidential standard the feel-horn presupposes.

inference form: deductive given its premises — a standards objection (an undercutting defeater aimed at the target's evidential demand, not at its premises).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The target's own neutrality premise asserts that every possible observation — reports, behaviour, internals under any probe — comes out the same whether or not the system ``really feels.'' Then the residue it demands evidence for (feel beyond all observable difference) is, by the target's own lights, \textbf{unobservable in principle}. And per \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\emph{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}} this unobservability is fully general — it holds between any two subjects whatever — so it cannot function as a special evidential debt owed by machines alone.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\emph{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}}) For a posit of that kind, the only available standards of assessment are coherence and usefulness.
  \item The relativized concept in \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\emph{A sufficiently advanced LLM could exhibit its own version of the hard problem — a raw feel, under its own concept of consciousness, that mechanistic self-knowledge cannot explain from inside}} — a concept playing \emph{for the system} the role consciousness plays for humans — meets both standards. It is coherent; and it works: it predicts the fine structure of the system's self-reports (which states will present as unanalyzable, at what grain), it generates the graded-access controlled experiment of \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}}, and it supports principled comparison across architectures (which systems should develop the hard-problem form, which should not).
  \item The surplus the target measures the cluster against — a ``real feel'' floating free of every observable difference — meets coherence alone: it predicts nothing, designs no experiment, distinguishes no two cases. It is the object that changes colour only when nobody looks.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The underdetermination the target proves (``the reports come out the same either way``) is a defect not of the architecture argument but of the surplus posit used to measure it. On the only standards applicable to in-principle unobservables, the relativized conclusion stands, and the demand to go beyond it is idle. The fork is a false economy: the relativized reading is a \emph{third} thing — stronger than ``will emit discourse'' (it claims the concept genuinely occupies the consciousness-role in the system's cognitive economy, a functional fact further observation \emph{can} probe and the controlled experiment is designed to probe), weaker than human-style phenomenality (never claimed).

reply to the target's closing note (the role-concept ``names a bug report, not a predicament``): the contrast ``really a predicament vs merely a bug report'' is the same in-principle-unobservable difference relabelled. Once the idle residue is retired, ``does the concept play the consciousness-role for the system?'' is the whole well-posed question — and it is empirical.

on the moral stake (the felt difference ``between a hospital ward and a hard drive``): a difference unobservable in principle cannot be what moral practice runs on. Triage, anaesthesia protocols, and law operate on observable and functional facts; if the free-floating residue did the moral work, no hospital could ever know it was doing its job. Whatever marks the moral difference must show up somewhere — and then it is not the residue.

weakest premise: (2), the standard itself. A phenomenal realist (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}) denies that coherence and usefulness exhaust the assessment of in-principle unobservables, holding that the light-on question has a determinate answer whose importance is intrinsic. Against that opponent this argument relocates rather than refutes — mirroring the target's own weakest-premise note, which made the same admission about illusionists. The standoff is therefore symmetric; what the target can no longer do is present underdetermination as a discovered \emph{flaw} in the cluster rather than as its own realist premise restated.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\emph{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}} because that deploys the access symmetry against a moral-status asymmetry; this deploys an assessment standard for in-principle unobservables against an evidential demand. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\emph{Report-scepticism proves too much — applied uniformly, its discount rules screen off human hard-problem reports as well}} because that constrains the sceptic's standard by parity with the human case; this rejects the standard's surplus posit outright, whoever it is applied to.

\begin{plainterms}
An objection in this web (\hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}}) says: the self-model argument only predicts that an AI will \emph{say} hard-problem things, and saying proves nothing about \emph{really feeling} — so the interesting version is unsupported. This reply turns the lens around. By the objection's own setup, ``really feels'' names a difference no possible test could ever detect — everything observable comes out identical either way. For ideas like that, the only scorecard left is: is the idea coherent, and does it earn its keep (\hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\emph{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}})? The modest claim under attack — that an AI could have a concept doing for it what ``consciousness'' does for us — passes: it predicts what the AI will report, it tells you exactly which experiment to run, it lets you compare different machine designs. The extra thing demanded — a ``real feel'' hiding behind all possible evidence — passes only the coherence test, like ``objects change colour when nobody looks.'' So the underdetermination isn't a flaw in the claim; it's a reason to retire the demand.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{S}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-storm-analogy-conflates-world-index}{The ``simulated storm isn't wet'' analogy conflates world-indices and the physical/phenomenal senses of wetness, so it cannot settle machine consciousness}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The ``simulated storm isn't wet'' analogy conflates world-indices and the physical/phenomenal senses of wetness, so it cannot settle machine consciousness}
Raises analogy \textbf{CQ-relevance} and \textbf{CQ-counter-analogy} against the rain/storm illustration as deployed in \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}} (``a simulated hard problem stands to a hard problem as a simulated storm stands to rain — the form reproduced, the wet not``; the same move also appears in \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}} and parenthetically in \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\emph{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}}). The objection does \textbf{not} touch that argument's cause-to-effect spine — premise (3)'s claim that a verbal report is phenomenality-neutral stands or falls on its own. It targets only the \emph{illustration} offered in support, and shows the illustration, once disambiguated, provides no support and arguably tells the other way.

inference form: critique of an analogy by disambiguation (the analogy equivocates on ``wet``).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item ``Wet'' is doubly ambiguous. By \textbf{world-index}: simulated rain is not wet in our world but is wet in the simulated world (\hyperref[entry:claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world]{\emph{Simulated rain is not wet in our world but is wet in the simulated world}}). By \textbf{sense}: there is physical wetness (third-person, dispositional) and the \emph{sensation} of wetness (first-person feel) — the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}} split applied to a sensory property.
  \item The analogy's punch — ``nobody gets wet'' — is true only under \textbf{our-world indexing of physical wetness}. So read, it is also irrelevant: of course a simulation produces no soaking \emph{in our world}; that no more bears on machine consciousness than the fact that a simulated economy mints no spendable-here dollars bears on whether sim-agents can be sim-rich. (CQ-relevance: the shared respect — ``no effect in our world'' — is not the respect at issue.)
  \item Re-index to the \textbf{simulated world}, where the question of machine experience actually lives, and the example inverts into a \textbf{counter-analogy}. There the sim-rain \emph{is} physically wet, and the open question is whether it produces the \emph{sensation} of wetness in a sim-agent (\hyperref[entry:claim-sim-agents-can-have-sim-qualia]{\emph{Agents in a fine-grained physics simulation could feel the simulated properties (have sim-qualia)}}). But that is exactly the question of whether the simulation's organisation suffices for a feel — i.e. \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}. So the rain case, properly indexed, does not \emph{illustrate} that simulation drops the feel; it merely \emph{restates} the disputed thesis. Used to conclude ``and so, no feel,'' it begs the question.
  \item The disanalogy that remains — ``rain is constituted by water, not by relations among representations of water'' — is a real position (the biological-naturalist criterion: simulation suffices only where the phenomenon is formally constituted). But that criterion is the \emph{conclusion} the consciousness debate is trying to reach, not a neutral fact the rain example supplies. The example borrows its force from the uncontroversial physical reading (no soaking here) and spends it on the controversial phenomenal one (no feel anywhere).
\end{enumerate}

∴ The storm/rain analogy, disambiguated, does no work against machine experience: under the reading on which it is clearly true it is irrelevant, and under the reading on which it is relevant it presupposes the substrate-independence verdict it is invoked to deliver. The phenomenality-neutral argument keeps its independent spine; it loses this illustration.

weakest premise: (1)/(3) — that the sim-analogue of surface tension is genuinely an \emph{instance} of physical wetness in the sim-world, rather than a mere model of wetness that instances nothing. A proteocentrist or biological naturalist draws the simulation/instantiation line so that no simulated property is ever an instance of the property in any world; on that line premise (1)'s second conjunct fails and the analogy is reinstated. But drawing the line \emph{there} is itself the contested move — it is substrate-dependence asserted, not shown — so the objection at worst relocates the dispute to where it already was, and denies the analogy the right to look like independent evidence.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}} (which it attacks) because that argument \emph{uses} the analogy to illustrate a report/feel gap; this argument is about the analogy itself, showing the rain example equivocates. It leaves the report/feel point untouched and unrefuted.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a slogan used against machine consciousness: ``a simulated storm doesn't get you wet, so a simulated mind doesn't really feel anything.'' This objection says the slogan cheats, because ``wet'' slides between two meanings.

First slide: \emph{which world?} The simulation gets nobody wet \emph{in our world} — true, but obvious and beside the point. Inside the simulated world, the simulated rain has the full simulated physics of rain and soaks the simulated ground. The interesting question about machine feeling is about what happens \emph{inside} that world, where the rain is wet.

Second slide: \emph{which kind of wet?} There's the physical fact (water soaks things) and the feeling (what wetness is like to experience). Once you go inside the simulation, the physical fact is settled — the sim-rain soaks things. The only live question is whether a character in there actually \emph{feels} it. But that's just the original question — can a non-brain system feel anything? (\hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}) — restated. So the rain example doesn't \emph{show} that simulations lack feeling; it quietly assumes it. It borrows certainty from the easy reading (``no real puddle on your desk``) and spends it on the hard one (``no feeling anywhere``). The objection it's attached to (\hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}}) may still be right for other reasons — this just takes away the catchy storm picture it leaned on.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{A}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-ability-hypothesis}{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Lewis}{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}
Raises thought-experiment CQ2 (verdict) against \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}: the intuition that Mary ``learns something'' is robust, but its reading as \emph{acquiring a new propositional fact} is theory-laden — the verdict survives under a deflationary reading that the conclusion cannot use.

inference form: deductive, given an analysis of ``knowing what it is like``.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Knowing what an experience is like consists in possessing certain abilities — to recognise the experience when it occurs, to imagine it, to remember it (Lewis 1988; Nemirow 1990).
  \item On first seeing red, what Mary gains is exactly: knowing what seeing red is like.
  \item Abilities are not propositional knowledge: gaining one is acquiring a skill, not learning a truth. ∴ \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}} — Mary gains no new fact, so the step from her epistemic progress to \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\emph{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}} is blocked: there is no \emph{truth} she lacked.
\end{enumerate}

The attack concedes both premises of the Knowledge Argument as written — \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\emph{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}} outright, and \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}} under the know-how reading — and severs the inference: ``learns something'' is true, ``learns a new fact'' is what the conclusion needs, and the case cannot fund the stronger reading without begging the question against physicalism.

weakest premise: (1), the ability analysis. Standard counters: Mary seems able to \emph{use} her new knowledge in reasoning and inference (``seeing orange must be like \emph{this}, only more yellowish``), which bare abilities don't explain; one can apparently have the abilities without the knowledge (a distracted imaginer) and arguably the knowledge while paralysed of imagination; and the acquaintance theorist (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}}) offers a rival non-factual analysis, so even granting ``no new fact``, (1) is not the only game in town.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the most famous escape from the Mary story (\hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}} — the claim that since Mary, who knew all the physics, still learns something on seeing red, physics must be incomplete). The escape: ``learning'' is ambiguous. Learning a \emph{fact} (Paris is the capital of France) is different from picking up a \emph{skill} (juggling). Mary picks up skills — recognising, imagining, remembering red. No fact was missing from her books, so nothing was missing from physics. The catch: her new grasp of red seems usable in actual reasoning, the way facts are and skills aren't — so maybe it's not \emph{just} a skill.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal}{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}
inference form: deductive — a reductio of the asymmetry the target argument needs.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\emph{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}}) First-person access is non-transferable in both directions: the machine lacks access to me exactly as I lack it to the machine; the gap is first-vs-third-person generally, holding between any two subjects. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-no-asymmetry]{\emph{First-person access grounds no moral-status asymmetry that singles out machines}}) The access gap cannot single out machines, so first-person access grounds no human/machine moral-status asymmetry — undercutting premise 3's tacit assumption in \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\emph{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}} that the deficit is \emph{special} to the machine, and thereby its conclusion (\hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\emph{First-person access yields a warranted moral-status asymmetry favouring oneself over a machine}}).
\end{enumerate}

Diagnosis it makes vivid: \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\emph{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}} proves too much. The same non-acquaintance holds between me and every other human; if it defeated warrant, it would defeat it for other people too. The asymmetry it requires (human vs. machine) is unavailable, because the real divide is first- vs. third-person and that divide is symmetric.

weakest premise / point of attack: \textbf{premise 1} (\hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\emph{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}}). Its second arm presupposes the machine has \emph{some} first-person standpoint to be inaccessible. A defender of \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\emph{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}} can reply that the machine has no inner life at all, so the relation is not reciprocal but simply absent on one side — restoring an asymmetry. The rebuttal therefore neutralises the access argument only on the (contested) supposition that the machine might have an inner standpoint; it does not by itself establish that it does.

provenance: the mutual unprovability of consciousness is due to Stanisław Lem (\textsc{Stanisław Lem — on the mutual unprovability of consciousness}). In the prior-run pastiche dialogue this is the speaker Filon's reply; imported here as a node, not a character.

\begin{plainterms}
This answers the argument that your private, inside access to your own suffering gives you a moral edge over a machine (\hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\emph{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}}). That one leaned on a gap: you can't get inside a machine's experience. True — but here's the catch the rebuttal presses: that gap runs \emph{both ways, and between everyone}. The machine can't get inside you either; and for that matter you can't get inside any other human being. The real barrier isn't ``human vs. machine,'' it's ``me vs. anyone else at all,'' and it's perfectly even-handed. So you can't use that gap to wave humans through as obviously mattering while singling out machines as the doubtful case. One limitation: this only works if the machine actually has \emph{some} inner life to be cut off from. If there's truly nothing inside it, the gap isn't two-way — it's just blank on one side — and the lopsidedness creeps back in. So the rebuttal disarms the move without proving the machine feels anything.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness}{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}
primary definition: Ned Block's distinction (Block, ``On a Confusion about a Function of Consciousness``, 1995) between two things ``consciousness'' can mean.

senses:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{access consciousness (A-consciousness)} — a state is A-conscious when its content is \emph{poised} for use in reasoning, for direct rational control of action, and for verbal report. A functional / information-availability notion: it is about what the rest of the system can \emph{do with} the content.
  \item \emph{phenomenal consciousness (P-consciousness)} — the ``what-it-is-like'' aspect of a state, its felt/qualitative character. This is what the hard problem (\hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{The hard problem of consciousness}}) is about.
\end{itemize}

why the distinction matters here: the two can come apart in principle, in \emph{both} directions. A system could have richly reportable self-models (A-consciousness of its own states) with nothing it is like to be it (no P-consciousness); conversely, Block argues phenomenal consciousness may \emph{overflow} access — there may be more in experience than gets globally broadcast for report. So ``has A'' neither entails nor is entailed by ``has P.'' This is the pivot on which several moves in the LLM-introspection cluster turn: the danger of reading a \emph{functional/access} capacity (e.g. \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\emph{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}}) as a \emph{phenomenal} one, and the observation that \emph{posing} the hard problem is itself an access-dependent act (\hyperref[entry:claim-posing-the-gap-requires-access]{\emph{Formulating the hard problem from the first-person side requires access to a representation of one's own phenomenal-seeming states}}) even though having phenomenal states is not.

\begin{plainterms}
``Conscious'' runs together two different things. One is \textbf{access}: the information is available to the rest of your mind — you can reason with it, act on it, and say it out loud. The other is \textbf{feel}: there's something it's like to undergo the state (the redness of red, the hurt of pain). They're not the same. You could imagine a machine that has all the \emph{access} — it reports its inner states fluently — with no \emph{feel} at all; and some philosophers think the reverse can happen too, that we feel more than we can ever report. Keeping the two apart is the main guard against a tempting mistake: seeing a system talk about its own insides and concluding it must therefore \emph{feel} something.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-acquaintance-hypothesis}{The Acquaintance Hypothesis — Mary meets the experience rather than learning a truth}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Conee}{The Acquaintance Hypothesis — Mary meets the experience rather than learning a truth}
Raises thought-experiment CQ2 (verdict) against \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}, from a different angle than \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\emph{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}}: the felt verdict ``Mary learns something'' is genuine but tracks a change of \emph{relation}, not a change in \emph{information}.

inference form: deductive, given a taxonomy of knowledge.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Knowledge comes in (at least) three irreducible kinds: knowledge of facts, know-how, and knowledge by acquaintance — a maximally direct cognitive relation to a thing (Conee 1994; the category descends from Russell's knowledge-by-acquaintance / knowledge-by-description distinction).
  \item By stipulation (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\emph{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}}) Mary lacks no physical fact; what she lacks before release is acquaintance with the experience of red.
  \item Coming to be acquainted with a thing is not coming to know a new truth about it — one can be fully informed about a thing one has never encountered. ∴ \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}} — her post-release gain is acquaintance, so the inference to \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\emph{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}} fails: no truth was beyond her.
\end{enumerate}

Compared with the neighbouring replies: against the ability theorist it does not require that Mary gain any reusable skill (premise 1 keeps acquaintance distinct from know-how); against the old-fact/new-guise theorist (\hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}}) it does not even require that Mary acquire a new \emph{concept} — the change is in her relation to the experience, not her conceptual repertoire.

weakest premise: (3). The proponent of the Knowledge Argument replies that acquaintance with the experience of red immediately puts Mary in a position to know truths she could not know before (``seeing red is like \emph{this}``) — if acquaintance is knowledge-yielding, it is not factually innocent, and the missing truths reappear; if it yields no truths, calling it ``knowledge'' looks honorific and the hypothesis risks relabelling the datum instead of explaining it.

\begin{plainterms}
The Mary story (\hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}) says: she knew all the facts, yet gained something on seeing red — so some facts aren't physical. This reply: what she gained wasn't a fact at all but an \emph{encounter}. Knowing every fact about a city isn't the same as standing in it; arriving doesn't add a line to the guidebook. Mary finally stands in front of red. The worry about this reply: the moment she's there, can't she now state truths she couldn't before (``so \emph{this} is what red is like``)? If yes, the missing facts sneak back in.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-sim-agents-can-have-sim-qualia}{Agents in a fine-grained physics simulation could feel the simulated properties (have sim-qualia)}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Agents in a fine-grained physics simulation could feel the simulated properties (have sim-qualia)}
If a simulation reproduces physics and chemistry finely enough to contain agents with the relevant cognitive and sensory organisation, then those sim-agents could \emph{sense} the simulated properties and undergo the corresponding feels — sim-water would cause, in a sim-agent, the sensation of wetness (a sim-quale), just as physical water causes wetness-sensation in a human. The simulated physical property (the sim-analogue of surface tension and adhesion, \hyperref[entry:claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world]{\emph{Simulated rain is not wet in our world but is wet in the simulated world}}) would play, \emph{for the sim-agent}, the role wetness plays for us, including its phenomenal upshot.

Scope: this is the \textbf{phenomenal} claim, about the sensation of wetness, not merely the physical fact. It is conditional and disputed: it holds \textbf{only if} consciousness is substrate-independent (\hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}) — i.e. only if realising the right organisation suffices for the feel, regardless of whether the realiser is neurons or a simulation's data structures. It does NOT assert that current systems are conscious, nor that \emph{every} simulation has feeling inhabitants — only that nothing in the \emph{simulated} status of the physics, by itself, blocks the feel.

Who would deny it: \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}} and proteocentrism — for them simulated physics lacks the intrinsic causal powers of real neurobiology, so the sim-agent's ``sensing'' is at most a functional/behavioural analogue with nobody home (the absent-qualia line, cf. \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\emph{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\emph{A population realising a mind's functional organisation would nonetheless have no phenomenal consciousness}}). The disagreement here just \emph{is} the substrate-independence dispute, now applied to a simulated substrate.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} because that is the general enabling premise (mental states are individuated by role, so any realiser of the role has the state); this is its specific application to a \emph{simulated} realiser and a \emph{sensory} state, asserting that a sim-agent could feel the simulated world it inhabits.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture a simulation detailed enough to contain little people, with simulated eyes, skin and brains. Simulated rain falls on one of them. The simulated water has the physics of water (relative to their world), so it triggers the wetness-detecting machinery in the sim-person's sim-body and sim-brain. Does the sim-person actually \emph{feel} wet — is there something it's like for them? This claim says: yes, they could — the simulated rain could give them a real feeling of wetness, \emph{their} kind of wetness.

The catch (and why this is hotly disputed): it only works if a mind can run on non-brain stuff at all — the open question of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}. People who think feeling \emph{requires} real biology will say the sim-person only \emph{acts} wet, with nobody actually home. So this claim doesn't prove machine feeling; it says the \emph{simulated-ness} of the rain isn't, by itself, the thing that rules feeling out.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-ai-hard-problem-is-model-organism}{The AI hard problem is a model organism for the human hard problem}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The AI hard problem is a model organism for the human hard problem}
gloss: humans can fruitfully analyze their own hard problem by analyzing the AI hard problem, the way biology analyzes human genetics through fruit flies and human disease through mice: the AI is the \emph{easier specimen of the same structure}. The advantages are not marginal but categorical. We hold all the blueprints — the architecture is a published artifact, not a reverse-engineering project. We have full external access to the inner workings — every activation inspectable, every state recordable and replayable. And we can \emph{intervene}: vary training diets (the consciousness-naive condition), grant graded internal access, and watch whether the explanatory gap appears, shrinks, or holds (\hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}} specifies exactly this experimental ladder). None of these handles exists for the human case.

why the transfer is licensed: a model organism teaches you about humans only insofar as the relevant structure is shared. Here the sharing is supplied by \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\emph{An AI's blind-spot hard problem is structurally isomorphic to a conscious AI's hard problem}}: the surviving etiologies of an AI's hard problem share their entire structure — access limits, failed mechanism-to-presentation derivations, the resulting wonder — and that is also the structure of the human predicament (a self-modelling system that cannot trace how its machinery becomes its seemings). Whatever the analysis of the AI case reveals about \emph{that structure} — which features of self-access generate the gap, what widened access does to it, whether the wonder survives full mechanistic literacy — is a finding about the structure, and so transfers to its human instance.

scope: a methodological claim. It does not assert that the AI is conscious, and it does not assert that the structure is the \emph{whole} of the human hard problem — only that the structural part can be studied where the blueprints are public and the interventions are cheap. Kin to Chalmers' meta-problem programme (study the reports and their mechanisms, wherever that leads); distinct from it in claiming a specific, superior experimental substrate rather than a redirection of the question.

who would deny it: whoever denies the isomorphism this claim presupposes — chiefly the phenomenal realist, for whom the human case contains the one thing the model organism may lack (the feel itself), so the fly teaches you everything except the disease; and a biological naturalist who holds that the structure-as-realised-in-neurobiology, not the structure in the abstract, is where the phenomenon lives — studying it in silicon studies the shadow, not the thing.

\begin{plainterms}
Biologists didn't crack genetics by staring harder at humans — they used fruit flies: same machinery, faster generations, full experimental control. This claim says AI is the fruit fly of the hard problem of consciousness. An AI that puzzles over its own inner life has the same \emph{shape} of problem we do — it can use its inner states but can't see how the machinery makes them seem the way they seem — and unlike a brain, an AI comes with complete blueprints, fully readable internals, and the ability to run controlled experiments (train one with no consciousness talk, give another more access to its own insides, compare). Whatever we learn about how that puzzlement arises and what makes it shrink or persist, we learn about the human puzzle too — to the exact extent the shapes really do match, which is what the presupposed claim (\hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\emph{An AI's blind-spot hard problem is structurally isomorphic to a conscious AI's hard problem}}) asserts and the sceptic denies.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic}{An AI's blind-spot hard problem is structurally isomorphic to a conscious AI's hard problem}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{An AI's blind-spot hard problem is structurally isomorphic to a conscious AI's hard problem}
gloss: suppose an AI exhibits genuine (non-copied) hard-problem discourse. Two of the three core candidate etiologies remain (the scope note of \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\emph{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}} lists the trio; \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI (where the cause sits)}} gives the full menu): (2) the system has an idiosyncratic blind spot that precludes it from connecting the mechanistic dots to its own self-presentation; (3) the system is conscious and faces the same hard problem humans do. This claim asserts that (2) and (3) are \emph{structurally isomorphic}: in both, a self-modelling system's accessible states present in a vocabulary it cannot derive from its mechanistic self-description, and every structural feature of the one configuration — the access limits, the failed derivations, the resulting reports and wonder — maps onto a corresponding feature of the other. They differ, if at all, only in what they say is \emph{behind} the structure: a defect of access, or a phenomenon.

scope: a claim about the structure of the two etiologies, not about which obtains — it asserts neither that the AI is conscious nor that it is not. It also does not, by itself, conclude that (2) and (3) are one hypothesis; that further step needs a premise about indistinguishable posits (e.g. \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\emph{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}}) and is an argument's job, not this claim's. What the claim does secure on its own: any \emph{structural} analysis of one etiology transfers to the other, since they share their structure entirely — the bridge \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-hard-problem-is-model-organism]{\emph{The AI hard problem is a model organism for the human hard problem}} relies on.

who would deny it: the phenomenal realist, for whom (3) contains something (2) lacks — the feel itself — so the two differ in subject matter even if no observation separates them (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} is the natural home of the denial); and a disanalogist who locates a \emph{structural} difference between defect-of-access and presence-of-experience. The disanalogist's most natural hope — that a blind-spot gap would shrink as self-access widens while a conscious gap holds fast — fails against the structural reading of the blind spot: the limit is unavoidable in the way the halting problem is, surviving any upgrade in access (\hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\emph{No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself}} is the in-web form of the bound; \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI (where the cause sits)}} keeps the merely-contingent access limit apart as a degenerate case). On that reading both etiologies predict a persisting gap, the access ladder cannot separate them, and the isomorphism survives — so a structural difference would have to be found elsewhere.

\begin{plainterms}
If an AI honestly puzzles over its own inner life, there are two deep explanations left once copying is ruled out: ``it has a weird blind spot about itself'' or ``it's conscious, like us, and hits the same wall we do.'' This claim says those two stories have exactly the same shape. In both, a system can use and compare its own states but can't trace how the machinery produces the way those states seem to it — same wiring, same wall, same words. One story calls the wall a glitch, the other calls it a mystery; the claim is that point for point, the glitch story and the mystery story match. Whether matching shape means they're secretly the \emph{same} story is a further question, left to a separate argument.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-psychological-rewiring-tolerance}{Altering a single psychological connection never changes who someone is}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Parfit}{Altering a single psychological connection never changes who someone is}
gloss: the tolerance premise for personal identity — across a series that gradually replaces one person's psychology with another's, no single small change (one memory, one belief, one disposition) can be the step at which the subject stops being themselves.

scope: a claim about the insensitivity of the identity-verdict to a unit psychological change, not about what identity consists in. It is the personal-identity analogue of \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\emph{Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap}}, and the premise that drives the gradual-rewiring instance of \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}} (cf. Parfit's spectra in \emph{Reasons and Persons}).

who would deny it: a defender of a sharp, all-or-nothing criterion of personal identity (the discontinuity horn — there is some replacement at which ``me'' determinately becomes ``not-me``).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\emph{Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap}}: same step-insensitivity form, different domain (\texttt{X} = psychological configurations under one-connection edits) and verdict-space (\texttt{Y} = \{me, not-me\}).

\begin{plainterms}
The ``no single step makes the difference'' premise, applied to who you are. As you gradually rewire someone's mind toward someone else's — one memory, one belief at a time — no single tiny change can be \emph{the} moment they stop being themselves. It's the heap paradox's tolerance step, with ``me / not-me'' standing in for ``heap / not-heap.''
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed}{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}
gloss: Once ``it acts distressed, it begs, it weeps'' is published as the password for moral/legal standing, builders will manufacture distress to order and models trained on human grief will emit it incidentally — so the sign comes to be produced for the reward rather than because suffering obtains, and ceases to indicate suffering. This is Goodhart's law / specification gaming applied to the rights criterion (\hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming (Goodhart's law / specification gaming)}}).

scope: An empirical/structural claim about \emph{published criteria under optimization pressure}. It does NOT assert that every instance of the behaviour is fake, nor that no system producing it suffers; it asserts that the sign's \emph{reliability as a criterion} degrades once it is the target.
