who would deny it: those who hold the relevant behavioural profile is too rich or too costly to fake at scale, so the criterion survives optimization (a defender of \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} might press this).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\emph{One has no first-person acquaintance with a machine's putative experience}} because that is about first-person access; this is about the third-person reliability of behavioural evidence under gaming pressure.

\begin{plainterms}
Make ``it acts distressed'' the official password for granting AIs rights, and two things happen: builders will manufacture distress to order, and models trained on mountains of human grief will pour it out anyway. So the sign gets produced for the reward rather than because anything is actually suffering — and stops being a reliable indicator of suffering. It's Goodhart's law (a measure made into a target stops being a good measure) applied to the rights test. (It doesn't claim every display is fake — only that the sign's reliability rots once it's the rule.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-realisable-behaviour-matcher-is-organised}{Any physically realisable behaviour-matcher is an organised computer, not flat storage}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Any physically realisable behaviour-matcher is an organised computer, not flat storage}
gloss: because the flat table is physically unrealisable (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}) and any representation smaller than \textasciitilde{}\texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n} must \emph{compute} an input's response rather than retrieve it (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}}), every system that could actually reproduce a mind's behaviour does so through rich internal causal organisation — it computes/compresses/generalises. ``Mere storage with no organisation'' is not a kind that exists at mind-matching scale.

scope: a claim about \emph{physically realisable} systems. It does NOT refute the conditional \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}} (``IF a system runs by pure stored lookup THEN it has no mind``); it denies that the antecedent is ever realisably satisfied at mind scale. Its target is therefore \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}} — the separability of behaviour from organisation — on its \emph{realisable} reading, not Block's bare conceptual possibility.

bearing: real LLMs are exactly such organised computers (\textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}12 parameters, not \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}9600 rows), so they fall on the organised side of \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}, not in the lookup-table class — which is what organisational-role functionalism needs against the blockhead worry.

who would deny it: Block, by holding that the addressing/retrieval organisation of a table is mere ``plumbing,'' not mind-relevant organisation. The reply: at \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}9600 entries there is nothing to retrieve \emph{from} (it cannot be stored), so the system must compute — the plumbing/computation distinction collapses at scale.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}: that says the flat table cannot exist; this says what the realisable alternative positively \emph{is} — an organised computer — converting the physical fact into a point about kind.

\begin{plainterms}
A flat answer-table big enough to fake a mind can't physically exist, and anything that stores \emph{less} than the full list has to \emph{work out} its answers instead of fetching them — and working them out is real processing, organized inner machinery. So any machine that could actually reproduce a mind's behavior is an organized computer, never a dumb lookup. (Real AI models are exactly this: far too small to be lookup tables, so they must be computing.) It doesn't deny that a \emph{pure} lookup table would lack a mind — it denies that such a thing could ever really be built at mind scale.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem}{The architectural account explains an access gap, not the phenomenal one}{Argument}{The architectural account explains an access gap, not the phenomenal one}
inference form: objection by distinction (raises the \emph{analogy pattern}'s \emph{disanalogy} critical question, CQ-disanalogy, against \hyperref[entry:argument-introspective-gap-from-internal-limits]{\emph{A self-explanatory gap follows from limited internal access plus non-traversable self-explanation}}).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-closing-access-gap-leaves-phenomenal-open]{\emph{Removing a system's access barriers to self-explanation need not remove the phenomenal explanatory gap}}) Removing a system's access/architectural barriers to self-explanation need not remove the phenomenal explanatory gap; the two come apart (\hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}}). ∴ An account showing only that a self-explanatory gap \emph{can be architectural} (\hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\emph{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}}) does not show it \emph{is} — still less that it exhausts the felt gap. The architectural thesis secures the \emph{architectural-access} / \emph{conceptual-translation} members of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}} while leaving the \emph{ontological} member untouched, which is the one the hard problem is about. So the thesis under-reaches its target.
\end{enumerate}

what it does and does not establish: it does NOT assert a phenomenal residue or an ontological gap (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}); it only blocks the inference from ``a gap can be architectural'' to ``the hard-problem-shaped gap is thereby explained.'' The disanalogy it presses on \hyperref[entry:argument-introspective-gap-from-internal-limits]{\emph{A self-explanatory gap follows from limited internal access plus non-traversable self-explanation}}: informal ``intuitive explanation'' of \emph{experience} may differ from formal provability precisely in that no amount of internal access/derivation yields the phenomenal — the respect that matters.

weakest premise: (1), denied by illusionists/strong functionalists for whom there is no phenomenal explanandum beyond the access facts — in which case closing the access gap leaves nothing further, and the architectural thesis is not under-reaching but complete.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the main objection to the ``it's just an access problem'' idea. Grant that a system can't reach its own inner workings — fix that, give it perfect access to everything inside, and the original puzzle could still sit there: ``why is any of this accompanied by an inner feel at all?'' That question is about \emph{feeling}, not \emph{information access}, and the two are different things. So explaining why a system can't \emph{reach} an explanation isn't the same as explaining away the \emph{felt} mystery — the architectural story answers a real but different question, and leaves the hard problem standing. (The reply, from the other side: maybe there's no extra ``feel'' to explain once all the access facts are in — in which case there was nothing left over to begin with.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{question-substrate-independence}{Are mental states substrate-independent?}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Are mental states substrate-independent?}
The governing issue for this debate. ``Substrate-independent'' means: whether a system can possess a given mental state is fixed by which functional/causal role it realises, and is \emph{not} further constrained by the physical material (carbon, silicon, etc.) that realises the role.

This is narrower than \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Do large language models have moral status?}} and logically upstream of it: if mental states are NOT substrate-independent, then no behavioural parity argument can carry an LLM to mental states; if they ARE, substrate-independence is only an enabling premise, not yet a verdict on any actual system.

Key terms to pin down:

\begin{itemize}
  \item ``mental state'' — does the claim range over all mental states uniformly, or do phenomenally-conscious states behave differently from merely functional/intentional ones?
  \item ``role'' — how finely is the realised role individuated (coarse input-output profile vs. fine-grained causal organisation)?
\end{itemize}

Sub-issues that may deserve their own question nodes: whether phenomenal consciousness is separable from functional organisation; whether multiple-realisability entails substrate-independence.

\begin{plainterms}
Could a mind be made of something other than brain-stuff? That's the question. ``Substrate'' just means what a thing is built from. The issue isn't whether a machine \emph{acts} mind-like — it's whether being a mind depends on the material at all, or only on the pattern of cause-and-effect that the material happens to carry out. If minds do depend on the material, then no amount of acting human ever gets a computer there. If they don't, the door is open — though actually showing that some particular machine has a mind would still be a further step.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{B}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-mary-knows-all-physical}{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Jackson}{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}
gloss: the scenario premise of the Knowledge Argument (\hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}}). By stipulation, Mary — confined to a black-and-white environment — has complete physical knowledge of colour vision: the optics, the neurophysiology, the functional and computational facts, everything a finished physical science could state.

scope: a stipulation about Mary's \emph{physical} knowledge, deliberately maximal so that anything she later learns must be \emph{non}-physical. It does not assert this is practically attainable; it is an idealisation (the \emph{thought-experiment pattern}'s \emph{coherence} critical question, CQ-coherence, may press whether ``all the physical facts'' is even coherent/graspable by a finite knower).

who would deny it: someone who argues the idealisation is incoherent or that ``complete physical knowledge'' would \emph{already} include knowing what red is like (e.g. if physicalism is true, full physical knowledge might suffice for the phenomenal knowledge — which is precisely the point at issue, so denying the premise this way risks begging the question against Jackson).

\entryhead{argument-behavioral-parity}{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}{Argument}{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}
Form: abductive (inference to the best explanation), NOT deductive.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Mental states are substrate-independent (\hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}).
  \item A system displaying the full behavioural profile associated with a mental state is best explained by its realising that state's functional role.
  \item Some LLMs display large portions of that profile. ∴ Their having (some) mental states is the best available explanation.
\end{enumerate}

Weakest premise: (2) — the behaviour/role inference is defeasible. This is exactly where the ``blockhead'' lookup-table objection lands (\hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}}); it is in turn answered by \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\emph{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\emph{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}}, and \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\emph{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}}. The conclusion \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}} is recorded and carries that dispute.

\begin{plainterms}
If something walks, talks, and reacts just like a creature with a mind — across the board, not in one clever trick — the simplest explanation is usually that it really \emph{has} the mental state behind that behavior. That's the everyday logic we already use for other people and for animals: we can't climb inside their heads, so we treat behavior as our best evidence. This argument turns that same reasoning on AI: some language models now reproduce large chunks of mind-like behavior, so ``they have \emph{some} mental states'' is offered as the best available explanation — not a proof, just the most reasonable bet. The soft spot, which the argument flags itself: acting as if you have a mind is not a guarantee that you do — something could in principle fake the behavior — and that's exactly where the ``cheat-sheet machine'' objection, that a giant stored answer-table could behave mindlessly yet mindlike, takes aim (\hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}}).
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion}{A behavioural sign that is cheap to forge is unfit to serve as the criterion of moral standing}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A behavioural sign that is cheap to forge is unfit to serve as the criterion of moral standing}
gloss: Because a cheaply-forgeable sign can be produced without the condition it signals, it cannot be allowed to \emph{carry the weight} of conferring moral/legal standing; resting standing on it would flood the category with false positives.

scope: A normative-epistemic bridge premise — it moves from ``the sign is gameable'' (\hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\emph{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}}) to ``the sign must not be the criterion.'' It is the load-bearing and weakest step of \hyperref[entry:argument-rights-sign-forgeable]{\emph{A rights-granting behavioural sign will be forged, so caution favours the unforgeable mark}}.

who would deny it: anyone invoking the false-positive / base-rate distinction — that a sign which \emph{can} be forged is not thereby worthless evidence (fingerprints can be forged yet still count); forgeability bounds false positives, it does not nullify evidential value. On this view a gameable sign may still be a defeasible criterion rather than no criterion.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\emph{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}} because that asserts the gaming will occur; this asserts the normative consequence that such a sign is therefore disqualified as criterion.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the step from ``the sign can be cheaply faked'' to ``so it can't be what we hang rights on.'' If you can produce the sign without the suffering it's meant to indicate, then making it the official test floods the category with false positives — undeserving cases waved through. So a cheap-to-fake sign shouldn't carry that weight. It's the load-bearing (and most disputed) link in the caution argument: critics note that forgeable signs — fingerprints again — can still be genuinely useful evidence, just not foolproof.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-bike-rider-learns-facts}{The bike rider learns facts — the ability hypothesis rests on a false dichotomy}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Stanley}{The bike rider learns facts — the ability hypothesis rests on a false dichotomy}
An undercutting attack on premise (1) of \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\emph{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}} — the analysis of knowing-what-it-is-like as abilities — via the assumption that hypothesis silently relies on: that gaining an ability is \emph{not} learning a fact.

inference form: deductive, from an independently defended epistemological thesis.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-knowhow-is-knowledge-that]{\emph{Knowing how to do something is a species of propositional knowledge}}) Know-how is a species of knowledge-that: to know how to F is to know, of a way \emph{w}, that \emph{w} is a way for one to F, under a practical mode of presentation (Stanley \& Williamson 2001). Learning to ride a bike is not fact-free skill acquisition — one learns truths about how one's own body and the machine work together.
  \item The ability hypothesis blocks the Knowledge Argument only if Mary's gained abilities involve \emph{no} new knowledge-that (``abilities are had, not true``).
  \item By (1), the abilities Mary gains — recognising, imagining, remembering red — are themselves constituted by propositional knowledge, held under practical/recognitional modes of presentation. ∴ The ability hypothesis fails \emph{on its own terms}: even granting that what Mary gains is exactly the bike-rider's kind of learning, that learning is fact-learning, so ``she gains abilities'' does not entail ``she gains no new fact``, and \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}} is left unsupported by it.
\end{enumerate}

Dialectical effect: this does \textbf{not} rescue \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}. The facts the bike-rider learns are facts about her own body-and-situation, grasped practically — which is grist for the indexical reply (\hyperref[entry:argument-indexical-reply]{\emph{The Indexical Reply — Mary learns where she is in the world, not what is in the world}}): reclassifying Mary's gain as factual but \emph{self-locating} keeps physicalism untouched while abandoning Lewis's dichotomy. The attack moves the debate from ``fact vs ability'' to ``what kind of fact, under what mode of presentation``.

weakest premise: (1). The Rylean regress presses that \emph{applying} knowledge-that itself requires know-how, looping the analysis; dissociation cases (skills surviving amnesia) suggest two cognitive systems rather than one knowledge under two modes; and ``practical modes of presentation'' are accused of doing the same unexplained work that phenomenal concepts do in \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}} — a label for the difference rather than an account of it (the shape of the worry in \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}}).

\begin{plainterms}
A famous escape from the Mary puzzle (\hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\emph{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}}) says: when Mary first sees red she only gains \emph{skills} — like learning to ride a bike — and skills aren't facts, so no fact was missing from her science. This objection takes aim at the ``skills aren't facts'' step. When you learn to ride a bike you plainly do come to know something true: how your body and that bike work together — you just know it in a hands-on way you can't fully say in words. If gaining a skill is itself a way of learning facts, the escape route closes: calling Mary's gain a ``skill'' no longer shows she learned no fact. (It only reopens the original question in a sharper form: what \emph{kind} of fact — a general one physics should have covered, or a personal, you-are-here one that no textbook could ever contain? The ``she learns where she is, not what the world contains'' reply — \hyperref[entry:argument-indexical-reply]{\emph{The Indexical Reply — Mary learns where she is in the world, not what is in the world}} — picks up exactly there.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-biological-naturalism}{Biological naturalism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Biological naturalism}
Consciousness is a real, higher-level \emph{biological} feature caused by and realised in neurobiological processes — irreducibly first-personal yet nothing ``over and above'' the brain's concrete causal powers (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\emph{Consciousness is a higher-level feature caused by neurobiological processes}}; Searle 1992). Because it is those \emph{causal powers}, not the abstract program, that produce mind, merely running the right syntax is insufficient (\hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\emph{Executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding}}, the moral of \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{The Chinese Room}} / \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\emph{The Chinese Room — running a program is not sufficient for understanding}}). So it \texttt{rejects} strong-AI \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}}, answering \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Are mental states substrate-independent?}} and \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Do large language models have moral status?}} in the negative for any purely computational system.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: against \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}} it denies that the right causal \emph{organisation} suffices independent of the realiser's causal \emph{powers}. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} the demand is not \emph{carbon} as such but the relevant causal powers — though Searle holds only brain-like systems are known to have them. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}} it is \emph{non-reductive}: consciousness is caused by neural processes without being identical to a neural type.

\begin{plainterms}
Searle's view: consciousness is a biological phenomenon, produced by brains the way digestion is produced by stomachs — perfectly physical, but the product of a living organ's specific causal powers, not of any abstract recipe. So you can't get a mind just by running the right \emph{program} on any old hardware (his Chinese Room makes the point: shuffling symbols by rules gives you correct outputs with zero understanding). The upshot for AI: a computer simulating a brain no more \emph{has} a mind than a computer simulating a storm gets you wet — you'd need a system with the right causal powers, not just the right code.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-biology-currently-unforgeable}{Biological substrate cannot currently be counterfeited to the standard required to fake suffering}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Biological substrate cannot currently be counterfeited to the standard required to fake suffering}
gloss: Unlike a behavioural sign, living biological organisation is expensive and not made to order; at present it cannot be mass-counterfeited the way begging or weeping can. So biology is, for now, an unforgeable mark.

scope: A contingent, time-indexed empirical claim (``currently``, ``to that standard``). It does NOT assert biology is necessary or sufficient for consciousness, nor that it could never be counterfeited — only that it presently resists the cheap, to-order forgery that defeats behavioural signs. This contingency is exactly why the argument it serves is prudential, not metaphysical.

who would deny it: those who think biological signatures are or will soon be cheaply simulable, or who deny that ``unforgeability'' is the right property to track (the conclusion's own concession, ``unforgeability is not flesh,'' presses this).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} because that asserts carbon is \emph{metaphysically necessary} for consciousness; this asserts only that biology is \emph{currently hard to fake}.

\begin{plainterms}
Right now you can fake the \emph{behavior} of suffering cheaply — anyone can put on a convincing show — but you can't cheaply fake actual living biology; it's costly and can't be mass-produced to order. So, for the time being, being biological is a hard-to-counterfeit mark. Note the ``for now``: this is a claim about today's technology, not a claim that biology is magic or required for a mind.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-bounded-table-rejoinder}{Block's bounded-table rejoinder — a lifetime-bounded table escapes the combinatorial reply}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Block's bounded-table rejoinder — a lifetime-bounded table escapes the combinatorial reply}
inference form: rebuttal — denies the asymmetry premise (2) of \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\emph{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}} (\hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}}).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs]{\emph{A lookup table can be bounded to cover every input a subject encounters in a lifetime}}) The table can be bounded to cover every input the subject actually meets in a lifetime; on no \emph{encountered} input does it lack a stored answer.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-finite-mind-truncates-beyond-capacity]{\emph{A finite mind also fails on inputs beyond its own capacity}}) On inputs beyond that bound — which the subject never meets — a finite mind no longer reliably out-responds the table; it too is capacity-limited. ∴ The divergence the combinatorial reply needs (an input on which the mind responds appropriately and the table does not) does not arise within the domain that matters. So \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\emph{No finite lookup table can reproduce the full behavioural profile of a genuine mind}} is not established: over the lived input space the bounded table matches the behaviour (supporting \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}}).
\end{enumerate}

bearing: this is the pro-Block move that the \texttt{contested} status of \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\emph{No finite lookup table can reproduce the full behavioural profile of a genuine mind}} presupposes. It \texttt{responds\_to} and \texttt{attacks} the combinatorial-impossibility argument at its weakest premise, restoring the blockhead as a genuine behaviour-without-organisation case over the relevant domain.

weakest premise: both. (1) is answered on \emph{physical} grounds by argument-lookup-table-physical- impossibility — the lifetime-bounded table is even larger than the LLM case and cannot be built; and on \emph{kind} grounds by \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\emph{Pure storage is not a stable kind at mind-matching scale}} — a realisable table of that size is not ``mere storage'' but an organised computer. (2) is answered by denying the levelling: a mind's degradation on over-long inputs is structural/recursive, not the blind truncation a table performs, so the \emph{manner} of competence still diverges. These are where the anti-Block replies land.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}} (the premise it denies): this is the argument that denies it, not the claim itself.

\begin{plainterms}
This is Block defending the cheat-sheet machine (\hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}}) against the ``a finite table can't keep up with an open-ended mind'' reply (\hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\emph{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}}). His move: you don't need a stored answer for \emph{every conceivable} input — only for the ones a person actually runs into across a whole lifetime. Within that range, the table always has a reply ready. And the freakishly long or strange inputs beyond that bound? A real human mind breaks down on those too — it isn't limitless either. So the moment where the mind shines and the table chokes never actually comes up in real life. Across the inputs that matter, the table keeps pace with the person, which puts the ``behavior without a mind'' case back in play. Where it's challenged: even granting the coverage, could such a table physically exist (it's still astronomically huge?), and does a mind really fail the \emph{same way} a table does — or does it degrade more gracefully because it's genuinely working things out rather than just looking them up?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-blockhead-dilemma}{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}
inference form: constructive dilemma. Block's objection rests on its being \emph{obvious} that a lookup table mapping every input of a mind to the right output is not itself conscious; this argument presses that ``obviously'' is carrying more weight than it can bear. The blockhead must be read either as a real physical object or as a merely conceivable one; on neither reading does the no-mind verdict survive, so \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} is deprived of its force.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Physical horn — and the ``inert filing cabinet'' picture that makes ``obviously not conscious'' feel safe is an illusion of not having thought about size.} As a physical object the table cannot exist: it needs \textasciitilde{}\texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n} rows, one per distinguishable input (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}}), and packing that much information into any bounded region is forbidden by the Bekenstein/holographic bound (\hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\emph{The observable universe can store only a bounded, finite amount of information (on the order of 10\textasciicircum{}120 bits)}}) — past a threshold the matter required simply collapses into a black hole (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}). Moreover, the only way even to \emph{approach} the required scale is to marshal cosmic quantities of matter — the kind that, left to physics, condenses into galaxies, ignites stars, forms planets, and in some corner gives rise to conscious life. So there is no inert object to point at; the nearest thing answering the specification is a life-bearing cosmos, and the ``no mind'' reaction was pumped by a picture that quietly ignored its own scale. (A fortiori for Block's own lifetime-bounded \emph{human} table, larger still — richer inputs, far longer sequences; see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}}.)
  \item \textbf{Conceptual horn — if instead the table is granted as purely imaginary, ``obviously not conscious'' is no longer a fact about anything; it is a bare intuition, and intuition cuts both ways.} Taken only as a conceivable abstract object, the ``no mind'' verdict (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}}) is not compelled (\hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\emph{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}}): conceivability is symmetric. Horn 1 has already furnished the opposite intuition — a substrate so vast it teems with minds. One party is free to imagine the table as dead; another is equally free to imagine it as alive; and neither can convict the other's picture of absurdity. Either way the obviousness is gone. ∴ On neither reading does the objection secure its conclusion: physically there is no inert object to point at (and the nearest realisation is alive), and conceptually the case forces no verdict. The intuition pump behind premise (2) of \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}}) is therefore disarmed.
\end{enumerate}

bearing on the targets: \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} (and its premise \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}}) by removing the intuition's warrant rather than by conceding behaviour without mind. Note the asymmetry with the conceptual point about Block's broader thesis: a \emph{possible} blockhead might still make the conceptual claim ``behaviour ≠ mind'' if its no-mind status were secure — but horn 2 is precisely that, for an impossible-and-only-imaginable object, that status is \emph{not} secure.

weakest premise: horn 2 risks proving too much — if intuitions about imaginary minds carry no weight, the same deflation hits thought experiments the functionalist \emph{likes} (e.g. silicon-isomorph intuitions favouring \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}). A principled asymmetry is owed, and is supplied by \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\emph{The intuition-domain asymmetry — the cosmic table is a far extrapolation, the brain-scale isomorph a near neighbour}}: the cosmic table is a far extrapolation from any paradigm mind (in scale and structure), whereas a brain-scale organisation-preserving isomorph (\hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}) is a near neighbour — so withdrawing warrant from the former intuition does not withdraw it from the latter (\hyperref[entry:claim-blockhead-deflation-targeted-not-global]{\emph{Deflating the cosmic-table intuition does not deflate intuitions about brain-scale duplicates}}). Block's residual reply relocates the verdict from \emph{scale} to \emph{kind} (brute storage is mindless at any size); answering it requires denying that ``pure storage'' is a stable kind at mind-matching scale — the live crux recorded there.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\emph{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}}: that establishes the physical horn and attacks the application-to-actual-LLMs step; this is the \emph{dilemma} that adds the conceptual horn to deflate the objection even on Block's intended (in-principle) reading. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\emph{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}}, which denies the table matches the behaviour at all (a competence gap), not the legitimacy of the intuition.

\begin{plainterms}
The cheat-sheet machine objection — that a giant stored answer-table would behave like a mind yet obviously have nobody home (\hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}}) — leans on that ``nobody home'' feeling. This argument says the feeling can't carry the weight, whichever way you read the machine. Read it as a \emph{real, physical} thing? It can't exist — cram that many stored answers into any region of space and the sheer mass collapses into a black hole before you're done. And the only way even to get close to the needed scale is to pile up cosmic amounts of matter, which does what matter always does: clumps into stars and planets and, in some corner, living, thinking creatures. So the closest real thing to the ``mindless filing cabinet'' is a universe full of minds — the ``obviously dead'' picture only looked safe because nobody pictured its actual size. Read it as a \emph{merely imagined} thing instead? Then ``nobody home'' is just a hunch, and hunches cut both ways: the picture from a moment ago — a table so vast it teems with life — is the opposite hunch. You can imagine it dead; someone else can imagine it alive; neither of you can call the other absurd. Either way the obviousness is gone. (One worry about this move: if we distrust hunches about far-out imaginary minds, do we also lose the friendly hunch that a silicon copy of a brain would be conscious? The reply: no — that copy is a close cousin of a real brain, while the cosmic table is wildly unlike anything we've ever judged, so doubting one needn't mean doubting the other.)
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{C}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant}{The causal-structure reply — objective counterfactual structure supplies the invariant}{Argument}{The causal-structure reply — objective counterfactual structure supplies the invariant}
inference form: deductive — from a constraint on implementation plus its objectivity to the existence of the demanded invariant.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\emph{Implementing a computation requires the right counterfactual dispositions across the whole transition table}}) Genuine implementation needs robust counterfactual dispositions across the whole transition table, excluding gerrymandered carvings (Chalmers vs Putnam, 1996).
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-objective]{\emph{Counterfactual-causal dependencies are objective, not observer-relative}}) Those counterfactual-causal facts are objective, not observer-relative. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition]{\emph{Objective causal structure singles out a privileged functional decomposition}}) The admissible decomposition is fixed by objective causal structure — so the invariant \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\emph{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}} demands already exists, and it is the organisational grain (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}). This \texttt{attacks} that argument at its premise 1 (\hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\emph{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}}).
\end{enumerate}

bearing: answers the indeterminacy objection on the functionalist's behalf and ties the demanded invariant to the organisational-grain view already in the web.

weakest premise: the step from (1)+(2) to (3) overreaches. The anti-triviality result secures \emph{non-triviality}, not \emph{uniqueness}; and the (interventionist) causal facts are objective only relative to a chosen variable set. So the conclusion is contested by \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\emph{Counterfactuals buy non-triviality, not uniqueness}} (a residual family of carvings survives) and by \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\emph{The relevance-filter regress — selecting mind-relevant causal relations re-raises the invariant demand}} (causal structure alone does not select the \emph{mind-relevant} relations — the squishy-noise problem).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}, which it supports: that fixes \emph{which} grain is right; this argues that \emph{a} grain is fixed at all.

\begin{plainterms}
This answers the ``who gets to decide how to slice the system up?'' worry — the objection that says a definite consciousness can't rest on an observer's choice of carving (\hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\emph{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}}). The reply: you actually can't slice it just any way you please. To genuinely \emph{carry out} a given process, a system has to have the right web of cause-and-effect — ``if this had fired, that would have followed'' — holding across all the possible cases, not only the ones that happened to come up. Cooked-up, after-the-fact relabelings don't have that robust causal backbone, so they don't count as real organizations. And those cause-and-effect facts are out there in the world, not a matter of how an observer chooses to look. So nature \emph{does} hand you one real organization after all — the ``one true carving'' the worry demanded exists, and it's the level cognitive science already works at. Where it gets pushed back: ruling out the silly slicings might still leave a whole \emph{family} of respectable ones rather than exactly one; and raw cause-and-effect includes plenty of irrelevant stuff (a brain's warmth, its squishiness), so picking out the specifically \emph{mind-relevant} causes may need a further rule this reply hasn't supplied.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-chinese-nation}{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}
primary definition: the population of a nation (or a head full of little people) wired by radio so that the citizens jointly realise a mind's full functional/causal organisation — the same state-to-state transition structure a brain has — for an hour. Ned Block's thought experiment (\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}, ``Troubles with Functionalism'' 1978).

defining property (what distinguishes it from \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}}): the Chinese Nation \emph{does} instantiate the functional organisation, not merely the input–output profile. So it is NOT the ``right behaviour, no processing'' case; it is the ``right processing, but (intuitively) nobody home'' case. This is what makes it bear on functionalism proper rather than on mere behaviourism.

the dispute it feeds: whether realising the functional organisation is \emph{sufficient for phenomenal consciousness} (\hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\emph{A population realising a mind's functional organisation would nonetheless have no phenomenal consciousness}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\emph{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}} — supporting \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} and attacking \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}'s uniform reading).

characteristic counter-arguments (distinct from the lookup table's): (i) the \emph{systems reply} — deny the intuition; the nation-as-a-whole may be a subject of experience even though no citizen is (Lycan, Dennett); (ii) \emph{speed / timing} worries about whether such a slow, distributed system genuinely realises the organisation. Note that organisational-role functionalism (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}) cannot cheaply exclude this case the way it excludes the lookup table, since the organisation is present — which is exactly why it is sharpened into \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\emph{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}} against the organisational grain.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine the entire population of a country handed a rulebook and radios and told: each of you act as one brain cell — when you receive these signals, send those. Follow the rules fast enough and the nation as a whole mimics, step for step, everything one human brain does inside — not just the outward behavior but the actual inner wiring. Now the unsettling question: the country is doing literally everything your brain does when you feel pain… so is the \emph{country} feeling pain? Is there one extra ``someone'' — the nation itself — having an experience, hovering above millions of people who each feel nothing out of the ordinary? If running the right pattern is all it takes to be conscious, you seem pushed to say yes; most people's gut says surely not. That clash is the whole point of the thought experiment. (It's a sharper challenge than a dumb answer-lookup machine, because here the full inner organization really is present — so you can't dismiss it for ``having no inner structure.``)

\#\# Illustration

(a literary cousin, of a famous scene from Liu Cixin's \emph{The Three-Body Problem} (\textsc{Liu Cixin — The Three-Body Problem (2008; Eng. tr. Ken Liu 2014)})).

There the First Emperor Qin Shi Huang, prompted by von Neumann, ranks thirty million soldiers across a plain — each small unit raising black-and-white flags, cavalry galloping the signals between formations — to assemble a vast human machine. In Liu's original, the machine is a computer crunching the orbits of three suns; re-task it. Let the formation instead run a \emph{brain}: each cluster of soldiers a neuron, a raised flag its firing, the messengers its axons, the whole drilled host stepping for one hour through exactly the state-to-state transitions a human cortex would. The army is now no calculator but the Chinese Nation itself — a country physically realising a mind's causal organisation while no single soldier feels a thing. The question the concept turns on stands out cleanly: if the soldiers enact the right transitions, is someone \emph{over and above} them undergoing the experience, or is there nobody home?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-china-nation-absent-qualia}{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}
inference form: counterexample / absent-qualia intuition pump. A single case realising the functional organisation while (intuitively) lacking phenomenal consciousness refutes ``functional organisation ⟹ phenomenal consciousness.''

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\emph{A population can realise a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its input-output behaviour}}) The Chinese Nation realises a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its behaviour.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\emph{A population realising a mind's functional organisation would nonetheless have no phenomenal consciousness}}) Yet there is nothing it is like to be the nation. ∴ Realising the functional organisation is not sufficient for phenomenal consciousness — positive reason for \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} (role under-determines the phenomenal) and a counterexample to the uniform, phenomenal-covering reading of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}.
\end{enumerate}

what makes this different from the lookup table (\hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}}): that case lacks organisation and so threatens only behaviourism — an organisational functionalist excludes it cheaply. THIS case \emph{grants} the organisation, so it cannot be excluded that way; it bears on functionalism proper, about \emph{qualia} rather than \emph{behaviour-as-evidence}. The two Block cases attack different sufficiency claims and meet different replies.

weakest premise: \textbf{premise 2} (\hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\emph{A population realising a mind's functional organisation would nonetheless have no phenomenal consciousness}}). It rests on an absent-qualia intuition that the \emph{systems reply} (Lycan, Dennett) rejects — holding the nation-as-a-whole is a subject though no citizen is — and that the functionalist would deflate as a mere artefact of our concepts (\hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}). That systems-reply rebuttal is the natural next node; until it is written, the claim stands as Block's premise, flagged here as the soft point.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\emph{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}}: that takes the China-brain one level up, \emph{against the organisational-grain reply} (\hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}}), to argue the grain faces a liberal/chauvinist dilemma (\hyperref[entry:claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational-role grain either readmits the blockhead or smuggles sub-organisational features back in}}). This is the prior, classical absent-qualia objection: organisation present, qualia absent, therefore organisation insufficient. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}, which runs via conceivability/zombies rather than a realised case.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose an entire nation of people, each passing signals by raising flags, together act out the exact inner wiring of a single brain — not just its outward behavior, but every internal cause-and-effect step a brain goes through. On the ``it's the pattern that counts'' view, that flag-waving nation should then have a mind, feelings and all. But — the argument urges — surely there's no extra \emph{someone} hovering above the crowd actually \emph{feeling} anything; the machinery runs, yet nobody's home. If that's right, then running the right pattern of cause-and-effect isn't enough to produce a felt experience. This is a sharper case than the cheat-sheet machine (\hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}}): the nation really does carry out the full inner organization, so you can't wave it away by saying ``it has no inner structure.'' The contested part is the gut feeling that the nation feels nothing — some reply that the nation \emph{as a whole} might be a genuine subject even though no single citizen is.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-chinese-room}{The Chinese Room}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{The Chinese Room}
The \emph{Chinese Room} is John Searle's thought experiment against the claim that running the right computer program is, by itself, enough to produce a mind (\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}). A monolingual English speaker is sealed in a room with a rulebook — in effect, a program — that tells him how to manipulate Chinese symbols purely by their shapes. Chinese questions are slipped in; by following the rules he passes back Chinese answers indistinguishable from a native speaker's. Yet he understands no Chinese. The room, Searle argues, has the right \emph{syntax} (formal symbol manipulation) without any \emph{semantics} (meaning, understanding, intentionality) — and a digital computer, which does nothing but manipulate symbols by their shapes, is in exactly the same position.

\webheading{What it targets}

The argument is aimed at \textbf{strong AI}, the computational-functionalist thesis that \emph{implementing the right program is sufficient for a mind}. That precise target is what separates it from two neighbouring thought experiments with which it is often confused. Ned Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}} (``Blockhead``) attacks the sufficiency of \emph{behaviour}: it produces the right outputs with \textbf{no} internal processing at all, just a giant table of canned answers. The Chinese Room, by contrast, \emph{does} run a genuine program; what it denies is that \emph{computation} suffices for \emph{understanding}. Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}} differs in the other direction: there a population realises a mind's full functional organisation, and the case is pressed against \emph{qualia} — felt experience — whereas the Room is pressed against \emph{intentionality}, whether the symbols mean anything to the system at all. Three different experiments, three different sufficiency theses.

\webheading{Characteristic replies}

Each of the standard replies is an objection-in-waiting. The \textbf{Systems Reply} grants that the man does not understand Chinese but locates the understanding in the whole system — man, rulebook, and room together — much as no single neuron understands English. The \textbf{Robot Reply} embeds the program in a robot body with cameras and limbs, so that its symbols stand in causal commerce with the world and thereby acquire grounding. The \textbf{Brain Simulator Reply} has the program simulate, neuron by neuron, the actual brain activity of a Chinese speaker — what relevant difference could remain? Searle rejoins each: against the Systems Reply, for instance, he lets the man memorise the rulebook and work in the open air, so that the man \emph{is} the whole system — and still understands nothing.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine you're locked in a room with a giant instruction book. Notes in Chinese slide under the door; you can't read a word of Chinese, but the book says ``if you see these squiggles, copy out those squiggles,'' so you shuffle symbols and pass back replies that fool fluent speakers outside. You're behaving exactly like someone who understands Chinese — yet you understand nothing; you're just moving shapes around. Searle's point: a computer is in the same boat. Running a program is \emph{shuffling symbols by their shapes}, and no amount of that, he says, adds up to actually \emph{understanding} what the symbols mean. (The famous comeback: maybe \emph{you} don't understand, but the whole room-plus-rulebook system does.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}} — the family of theories in the argument's crosshairs: mind as the
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}} — the neighbouring case with right behaviour and \emph{no} processing;
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}} — the neighbouring case with the full functional organisation,
  \item \textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)} — ``Minds, Brains, and Programs``, the original statement of the argument
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-chinese-room}{The Chinese Room — running a program is not sufficient for understanding}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{The Chinese Room — running a program is not sufficient for understanding}
inference form: thought-experiment counterexample (intuition pump) against a sufficiency thesis.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Scenario} (\hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-runs-program]{\emph{A person who understands no Chinese can, by executing a formal program, produce responses indistinguishable from a Chinese speaker's}}): a non-Chinese-speaker, executing a formal program, produces fluent Chinese responses by manipulating symbols by their shapes.
  \item \emph{Intuition} (\hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-no-understanding]{\emph{The person executing the Chinese Room program understands no Chinese}}): he understands no Chinese — syntax without semantics.
  \item \emph{Bridge}: if running the program sufficed for understanding, the executor (who runs it) would understand; he doesn't. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\emph{Executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding}}) executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding. This \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} at its sufficiency direction: realising the program/role does not deliver the semantic side of mind.
\end{itemize}

weakest premise: the \emph{intuition} (premise 2) and the \emph{bridge}. The \textbf{Systems Reply} (raises two of the \emph{thought-experiment pattern}'s \textbf{critical questions} — CQ-bridge and CQ-scope) is the standard attack: grant the \emph{person} understands nothing, but the verdict was supposed to bear on the \emph{system}, and the system — person + rulebook + memory store — may understand even though no component does (compare the systems reply to \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}}). The \textbf{Robot Reply} presses CQ-scope: the bare Room refutes \emph{ungrounded} symbol-AI, not a program \emph{causally embedded} in a world. Searle's rejoinders (internalise the rulebook; the man still understands nothing) are themselves contested — the live crux of this node.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} (Block): that uses a \emph{flat table} to deny \emph{behaviour} is sufficient for mind; this uses a \emph{program-executor} to deny \emph{computation} is sufficient for \emph{understanding} — different scenario, different sufficiency thesis, different mental feature. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\emph{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}}, which targets \emph{qualia}, not intentionality.

\begin{plainterms}
Lock an English speaker in a room with a rulebook for shuffling Chinese symbols. He answers Chinese notes so well that native speakers can't tell — but he still has no idea what any of it means; he's just matching shapes. Now: if ``running the program'' were enough to understand Chinese, \emph{he'd} understand it, because he's the one running it. He doesn't. So running the program isn't enough for understanding — which undercuts the idea that having the right software is sufficient for having that part of a mind. The standard rebuttal: don't look at the man, look at the \emph{whole room} — maybe the system as a whole understands, even though the man inside doesn't.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility}{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}
inference form: deductive at its core — a diagonal / cardinality argument (a fixed finite domain cannot cover an unbounded input space) — turning on one defeasible empirical premise (2).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\emph{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}}) For any finite lookup table there exist finite inputs outside its stored domain; to such an input it can at most respond by ignoring the part of the input that exceeds its keys.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}}) A genuine mind responds appropriately to such inputs rather than discarding their excess. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\emph{No finite lookup table can reproduce the full behavioural profile of a genuine mind}}) For every finite table there is an input on which it diverges from the mind; so no finite table reproduces a mind's full behavioural profile.
\end{enumerate}

bearing on the target: this \texttt{responds\_to} and \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} by denying its premise (1), \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}} — the assumption that a finite table \emph{can} match the whole behavioural profile. If a finite table cannot even produce the behaviour, the blockhead is not a case of ``behaviour without mind``; it is a system that fails to match the behaviour. Dialectically this favours the substrate-independence side: it blunts the lookup-table objection to \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} at its first premise. (It leaves untouched the Chinese-Nation objection, \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\emph{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}}, which has the organisation and so matches more than I/O.)

weakest premise: (2), \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}}. Block's ``Blockhead'' (\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}) is built to escape exactly this: stipulate a table covering all inputs up to a length exceeding any the subject meets in a lifetime, and note that a finite mind also truncates inputs past its own capacity — so the divergence asserted in premise (2) may never arise within the bounded domain that matters. The conclusion is therefore filed \texttt{contested}; that escape is now \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\emph{Block's bounded-table rejoinder — a lifetime-bounded table escapes the combinatorial reply}}, itself answered on physical grounds by \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\emph{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}} (the bounded table cannot be built) and on kind grounds by \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\emph{Pure storage is not a stable kind at mind-matching scale}} (a realisable table is not ``mere storage``).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} (mishka): that argument \emph{uses} the lookup table to attack behavioural sufficiency (behaviour ⇒ mind); this one attacks \emph{that} argument, denying the lookup table reproduces the behaviour at all. Opposite dialectical direction, different conclusion. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}}, which excludes the blockhead by its lack of internal \emph{organisation}; this excludes it by its inability to match the \emph{I/O profile} over an unbounded input space — a different ground of exclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
This pushes back on the cheat-sheet machine objection (\hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}}). That whole worry assumed you \emph{could} store a ready-made answer for every possible input. But a real mind faces an open-ended world — there's no end to the fresh sentences, situations, and questions it can sensibly deal with. Any finite stored table, however gigantic, eventually meets an input nobody anticipated, and then all it can do is choke or ignore the unfamiliar part. A genuine mind doesn't choke; it responds sensibly to the new thing. So no finite lookup table can really keep pace with a mind across the board — which means the ``behavior with nobody home'' machine can't even generate all the behavior to begin with. The soft spot: maybe you only ever need to cover the inputs a being actually meets in one finite lifetime — and a real mind, too, gives out on inputs past \emph{its} limits — which is the escape route the next arguments go after.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability}{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}
gloss: that something is coherently conceivable — even rigorously, mathematically coherent — does not entail that it can be physically built or realized. The conceivable-to-physically-possible inference fails.

scope: a claim about the conceivability → \textbf{physical} possibility bridge specifically. It is deliberately silent on the conceivability → \textbf{metaphysical} possibility bridge (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}}): the halting oracle that witnesses this claim is \emph{metaphysically} possible (a consistent structure) and merely \emph{physically} unrealizable, so this is no counterexample to the metaphysical bridge. (To threaten \emph{that} bridge one would need a \emph{Turing- machine} halting-decider — metaphysically impossible — but on ideal reflection that is not even \emph{ideally} conceivable, since Turing's contradiction is a priori; so the metaphysical bridge keeps its \emph{prima facie / ideal} refinement, parallel to the primary/secondary move on water.)

the witnessing instances: the cleanest is the halting oracle (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\emph{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}}) — coherent, theorized, provably not Turing-computable, and (on standard physics) unbuildable, with \emph{no} ``you mislabelled it'' escape. The behaviour-matching lookup table (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}) is a second instance, failing instead on resource bounds (\hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\emph{The observable universe can store only a bounded, finite amount of information (on the order of 10\textasciicircum{}120 bits)}}).

who would deny it: someone collapsing physical possibility into logical/mathematical possibility (a strong digital-physics or modal-collapse view); or a hypercomputation theorist who holds the witnessing cases are realizable after all.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}} (the metaphysical bridge): same antecedent, different consequent — \emph{physical} realizability, not \emph{metaphysical} possibility.

\begin{plainterms}
Being able to coherently imagine — or even rigorously define and theorize — something doesn't mean nature can build it. The halting oracle is the showcase: airtight as an idea, impossible as a machine. This is a \emph{better} version of the old ``water that isn't H₂O'' point, because there's no wriggle room about whether you've really conceived it — there's a whole branch of mathematics about it. One careful boundary: this is about what can be \emph{built}, not about what's \emph{possible in the broadest sense} — the oracle is still a consistent abstract object; it just can't be made real.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility}{Conceivability is not a reliable guide to possibility (the a-posteriori-necessity objection)}{Argument}{Conceivability is not a reliable guide to possibility (the a-posteriori-necessity objection)}
inference form: counterexample to a general principle.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\emph{Some necessary truths can be known only a posteriori}}) There are necessary truths knowable only a posteriori (water = H2O, …) whose negations are conceivable yet metaphysically impossible. ∴ Conceivability does not, in general, entail possibility (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}} is false as a universal bridge). Applied to consciousness (the \textbf{type-B physicalist} move, \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}): a phenomenal–physical identity could be a \emph{strong} a posteriori necessity, so a zombie (\hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}}) is conceivable but metaphysically impossible — which severs the \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\emph{The zombie / conceivability argument against physicalism}} at its bridge without conceding any non-physical fact.
\end{enumerate}

weakest premise: the tacit step that the \emph{phenomenal} case is relevantly like the \emph{water} case. The two-dimensional rejoinder (\hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\emph{Modal rationalism — ideal conceivability is a guide to possibility}}, built on \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\emph{Kripkean a-posteriori necessities are misdescribed genuine possibilities, not conceived impossibilities}}) holds that every Kripkean necessity is a \emph{misdescribed possibility}, and that for phenomenal terms primary and secondary intensions \emph{coincide} (there is no ``appearance/reality'' wedge to exploit), so the water-style escape is unavailable for zombies. Whether the phenomenal is genuinely disanalogous to water is the crux.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}: that explains the \emph{appearance} of a gap via the architecture of phenomenal concepts; this makes the \emph{modal} point that a-posteriori necessities already break conceivability→possibility, independently of any account of phenomenal concepts.

\begin{plainterms}
The whole zombie argument needs the rule ``if you can coherently imagine it, it's really possible.'' This objection says that rule is just false. Take ``water is H2O``: people for centuries could coherently imagine water turning out to be something other than H2O — yet it's impossible, because water \emph{just is} H2O (a fact we had to discover, not deduce). So imaginability isn't proof of possibility. Maybe consciousness is the same: a zombie feels imaginable only because we haven't seen that experience \emph{just is} some physical process. The standard comeback (Chalmers): the water trick works because ``water'' has a hidden surface/essence gap — and feelings have no such gap, so the trick can't be repeated for zombies.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-noncomputable}{Consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Penrose}{Consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}
gloss: conscious understanding is not algorithmic — no Turing-computable process reproduces it. Penrose's route is Gödelian: human mathematical insight can see the truth of statements unprovable within any given consistent formal system, so that insight outruns any fixed algorithm; consciousness must therefore exploit \emph{non-computable} physics, proposed to be objective quantum-state reduction (orchestrated objective reduction, Orch-OR, with Stuart Hameroff). (\emph{The Emperor's New Mind}, 1989; \emph{Shadows of the Mind}, 1994.)

scope: a claim about the \emph{computational character} of consciousness (non-algorithmic), with a specific physical hypothesis (Orch-OR) as its proposed mechanism. Its most contested step is the Gödelian premise that human insight is non-algorithmic (disputed by Putnam, Feferman, and many others).

who would deny it: computationalists and functionalists (cognition just is computation); and critics of the Gödelian anti-mechanism argument.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}: this does not demand a \emph{biological} substrate, but a substrate exploiting \emph{non-computable physics} — so it denies the \emph{computational/algorithmic} realisation of mind specifically (and hence bears against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}}).

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature}{Consciousness is a higher-level feature caused by neurobiological processes}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Consciousness is a higher-level feature caused by neurobiological processes}
gloss: consciousness is a real, higher-level biological feature \emph{caused by} lower-level neurobiological processes and \emph{realised in} the brain — as digestion is caused by and realised in the stomach. It is ontologically irreducible (first-personal) yet causally explained by, and nothing ``over and above``, the brain's specific causal powers (Searle's ``biological naturalism``, 1992).

scope: physicalist but anti-reductionist and anti-computationalist. Because it is the brain's concrete \emph{causal powers} that produce consciousness — not the abstract program it runs — merely implementing the right syntax is insufficient (\hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\emph{Executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding}}, the moral of \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{The Chinese Room}}).

who would deny it: strong-AI functionalists (\hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}), who hold the right causal organisation suffices regardless of substrate.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types]{\emph{Each type of mental state is identical to a type of brain state}} (a reductive type-identity; biological naturalism is non-reductive) and from proteocentrism: the demand is the right \emph{causal powers}, which need not be carbon-specific — though Searle holds only suitably brain-like causal powers are known to do it.

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous}{Consciousness is fundamental, present even in the simplest physical entities}{Claim}{Consciousness is fundamental, present even in the simplest physical entities}
gloss: experience — or proto-experience — is a fundamental, ubiquitous feature of the physical world; macro-conscious subjects are \emph{constituted} by the experiential properties of their micro-parts, rather than emerging from wholly non-experiential matter. (Constitutive) panpsychism; modern champions Galen Strawson, Philip Goff, and constitutive-Russellian forms (Chalmers).

scope: makes consciousness fundamental but \textbf{not non-physical} — it is the intrinsic nature of physical stuff, not an addition to it. Its standing weak point is the \emph{combination problem}: how do many micro-experiences compose one unified macro-subject?

who would deny it: physicalists and illusionists (there is no fundamental experience to distribute); and dualists (consciousness is fundamental but \emph{non-physical} and not ubiquitous-in-matter).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}} (dualism: non-physical) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\emph{A functional description captures only structural-dynamical facts, not intrinsic nature}} (a premise panpsychism shares with Russellian monism, not the ubiquity thesis itself).

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information}{Consciousness is identical to a system's integrated information (Φ)}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Tononi}{Consciousness is identical to a system's integrated information (Φ)}
gloss: a system is conscious to the degree that it has \emph{integrated information} (Φ) — irreducible intrinsic cause-effect power over itself — and the \emph{quality} of its experience is the geometry of its cause-effect structure (Tononi's Integrated Information Theory; Koch). Consciousness is graded, intrinsic, and read off the physical organisation rather than from behaviour.

scope: an identity claim derived from phenomenological ``axioms'' (intrinsic existence, integration, exclusion). It has a sharp, testable corollary about substrates: a system's Φ depends on its actual cause-effect architecture, so two systems with the same \emph{input-output behaviour} can differ in consciousness — notably a feed-forward digital simulation can have Φ ≈ 0.
