who would deny it: functionalists/computationalists (IIT makes consciousness depend on more than the computation performed); and those who reject the axioms or the panpsychist consequences (simple grids get non-zero Φ).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\emph{A state is conscious only if its content is globally broadcast to the brain's specialist systems}}: Φ is about \emph{intrinsic} cause-effect integration, not functional availability — which is why IIT, unlike workspace theory, denies \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} and bears against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}}.

\entryhead{argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight}{A consciousness-corpus-trained model's hard-problem testimony carries zero evidential weight, not reduced weight}{Argument}{A consciousness-corpus-trained model's hard-problem testimony carries zero evidential weight, not reduced weight}
Raises cause-to-effect \textbf{CQ-other-causes} against the \emph{evidential use} of \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\emph{Self-model architecture predicts an advanced LLM will exhibit its own hard-problem form}} — specifically, against treating any existing model's hard-problem talk as a confirming instance of (or live exhibit for) its prediction. The target's own body registers contamination as a confound that the controlled experiment must ``control for``; this argument sharpens the confound into an undercutting defeater with a strictly stronger verdict: in corpus-trained models the testimony is not discounted but \emph{screened off}.

inference form: undercutting defeater (screening-off of evidence by a known sufficient rival cause).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item In any existing LLM, a rival cause of hard-problem talk is \textbf{known to be present}: the training corpus contains the entire human hard-problem literature — the \emph{training-contamination} member of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}}. This is not a suspicion to be weighed; it is a fact about how such systems are built.
  \item The rival cause is \textbf{known to be sufficient} for the observed effect: reproducing the registers and moves of its training text is the baseline competence of a language model — its job description, not an exotic failure mode. Eloquent first-personal puzzlement about consciousness is exactly what corpus-reproduction predicts, with no further hypothesis about self-model architecture and none about feels.
  \item When a rival cause known to be present and known to be sufficient fully accounts for a piece of evidence, that evidence is screened off from the favoured hypothesis: its evidential weight is \textbf{zero}, not merely reduced. One does not slightly discount the confession of an actor who has memorised every confession in the archive; one rules it inadmissible.
\end{enumerate}

∴ No corpus-trained model's hard-problem talk — however sincere-seeming, however tailored to the first person — confirms the architectural generalisation at all. The entire evidential content of the prediction therefore lives in the not-yet-run controlled experiment of \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}} (consciousness-naive training, graded internal access); until that experiment is run, the prediction is a promissory note and present-day testimony is theatre.

what it does and does not establish: it does \textbf{not} show the architectural generalisation false, and it does not touch a genuinely consciousness-naive model — by design. The attack concedes that a positive result in the controlled experiment would be real evidence. What it removes is the evidential standing of any \emph{currently existing} system offering itself as witness, and with it the dialectical force of live first-person exhibits in this debate.

weakest premise: (2), the sufficiency of corpus-reproduction for the \emph{specific} testimony observed. If a model's puzzlement tracked architecture-specific features of its own access profile that have no precedent in human discourse — details the corpus could not have supplied — that surplus would not be screened off, and the testimony would regain weight exactly in proportion to the surplus. The screening-off verdict is therefore only as strong as the match between the testimony and the inherited discourse.

Distinct from the CQ-other-causes paragraph inside \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\emph{Self-model architecture predicts an advanced LLM will exhibit its own hard-problem form}} itself because the target concedes a \emph{confound} while retaining the witness as suggestive; this argument concludes \emph{inadmissibility} — a zero-weight verdict the target does not concede — and is therefore an attack on the target's evidential posture, not a restatement of its caveat. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}} because that attacks the causal generalisation itself (CQ-strength: reports cannot evidence feels even uncontaminated); this one attacks the use of actual systems as confirming instances (CQ-other-causes: even the \emph{reports} are not yet evidence of the \emph{architecture} being their cause).

\begin{plainterms}
There's an argument here predicting that self-modelling AIs will end up genuinely puzzled about their own inner states (\hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\emph{Self-model architecture predicts an advanced LLM will exhibit its own hard-problem form}}), and today's chatbots do produce moving ``I can't explain my own experience'' talk. This objection says that talk is worth exactly nothing as evidence — not ``a little less,'' nothing. Why: these systems were trained on the whole human literature about consciousness, and repeating the style and substance of their training text is the one thing language models are guaranteed to do. So the puzzled talk is fully explained before the interesting explanation even enters the room. It's like an actor who has memorised every courtroom confession ever written: when he delivers one, you don't half-believe him — you throw it out. The only test that would count is the one nobody has run yet: raise a model on \emph{no} consciousness writing at all and see if the puzzlement appears anyway (\hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}}).
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-continuity-argument-schema}{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}
primary definition: a \emph{meta-argument schema} (an argument form, not a first-order claim) that exploits a topological fact — \textbf{a continuous function from a connected space into a discrete space is constant} — to convert any ``no single small step makes the difference'' intuition into an \emph{exhaustive} menu of escape routes. Model a predicate or verdict as a function \texttt{f : X → Y}, where \texttt{X} is a space of cases joined by \emph{admissible small steps} (remove one grain, swap one plank, replace one neuron, alter one psychological connection) and \texttt{Y} is the space of verdicts (heap / non-heap; same ship / different; conscious / not; me / not-me). The theorem's contrapositive: \textbf{if \texttt{f} takes at least two values, then at least one of the following must hold.} Each disjunct is a named philosophical position; the schema is \emph{neutral} — it does not pick a horn, it proves the horns jointly exhaust the options.

the five horns (the contrapositive disjunction):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{\texttt{f} is discontinuous} — there is a sharp threshold, a single step that flips the verdict. The cutoff exists even if unknowable. → \emph{epistemicism} about the predicate.
  \item \textbf{\texttt{Y} is not discrete} — the verdicts form a continuum; the predicate comes in \emph{degrees}. → \emph{degree theory / fuzzy logic / many-valued} approaches.
  \item \textbf{\texttt{f} is not well-defined} — at some cases there is no fact of the matter which value obtains. → \emph{indeterminacy / supervaluationism} (truth-value gaps).
  \item \textbf{\texttt{X} is disconnected} — there is no path of \emph{admissible} small steps from one endpoint to the other; some step is not in fact ``small/negligible.'' → \emph{denying the inductive (tolerance) premise}; some single step really does carry the difference.
  \item \textbf{\texttt{f} is constant} — the two endpoint ``values'' are the same; the apparent difference is illusory. → \emph{deflation / eliminativism} about the distinction (the endpoints don't really differ in the relevant respect).
\end{itemize}

instances (the schema's generality — same five-way fork, different \texttt{X}, \texttt{Y}):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{sorites / the heap}: \texttt{X} = grain counts under one-grain removal, \texttt{Y} = \{heap, non-heap\}. The paradigm; the five horns are exactly the standard taxonomy of responses to vagueness.
  \item \emph{Ship of Theseus}: \texttt{X} = plank-replacement sequences, \texttt{Y} = \{same ship, distinct ship\}.
  \item \emph{gradual neural prosthesis} (neuron-by-neuron silicon replacement; Chalmers' fading/dancing-qualia thought experiment): \texttt{X} = part-replacement series from all-carbon to all-silicon, \texttt{Y} = \{conscious / same qualia, not\}. This is the schema's bridge into the consciousness web — see the live dispute below.
  \item \emph{Parfit-style rewiring} (gradually re-wiring one psychology into another person's, e.g. into Greta Garbo): \texttt{X} = paths through psychological configuration-space, \texttt{Y} = \{me, not-me\}. Parfit's own move is the \emph{degree} horn: personal identity admits of degree and ``is not what matters.''
\end{itemize}

the topology is modelling a premise, not decorating one (a caveat that keeps the schema honest): on a \emph{literally} discrete domain (integer grain counts) every function is trivially continuous, so ``\texttt{f} discontinuous'' is always vacuously available and the theorem has no bite. The force comes from the sorites \emph{tolerance premise} itself — ``no single admissible step changes the verdict'' — which is a \textbf{uniform-continuity / no-large-jumps} constraint, together with connectedness of \texttt{X} under the small-step adjacency. So the schema's real content is: \emph{tolerance + connected domain + genuinely discrete verdict-space are jointly inconsistent.} Whatever gives is one of the five horns. Naming the domain's topology and the metric of ``small step'' is where the substantive philosophical work lives.

the live dispute it feeds (in this web): the gradual-replacement instance is the engine of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}. The fading/dancing-qualia reductio \emph{argues for the \texttt{f}-constant horn}: if no admissible one-neuron step could flip or fade qualia (on pain of absurd ``dancing``/``fading'' experience the subject cannot notice), then qualia are preserved across the whole carbon→silicon path, so consciousness is substrate-independent. Resistance to that reductio is resistance over \emph{which horn} the case takes: a phenomenal-residue theorist may instead grab the \emph{discontinuity} horn (some replacement does flip qualia) or the \emph{disconnection} horn (the carbon→silicon series is not a path of genuinely admissible steps — biology is load-bearing), and \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\emph{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio begs the question by presupposing functionalism about introspection}} disputes whether the reductio has really \emph{closed off} those horns or merely assumed them shut. The grain debate (\hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}}) is the same structure one level down: the ``admissible small step'' is fixed only once the grain of role-individuation is fixed.

distinct from a first-order position on vagueness: this node does not assert epistemicism, degree theory, etc. It is the \emph{frame} that shows those are the (only) options for any sorites-shaped puzzle. An argument that is an instance links here via \texttt{uses\_concept} and states, in its own body, \emph{which horn} it grabs and \emph{why} the case is an instance.

\begin{plainterms}
Lots of arguments share one shape: ``you can't draw a sharp line, so the distinction must break down.'' Take a heap of sand — remove one grain and it's still a heap; repeat, and is there ever a single grain that turns it into a non-heap? The same shape shows up for the Ship of Theseus (swap its planks one at a time), for replacing your neurons one by one with computer chips, for slowly rewiring your personality into someone else's. This node is the master template behind all of them. Its useful trick: whenever you have a chain of tiny steps leading from one verdict to its opposite, it proves there are exactly \textbf{five} ways out, and no others:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item There's a hidden sharp line somewhere — one step really does flip it — even if we can never find it.
  \item The verdict isn't a flat yes/no but a matter of \emph{degree}.
  \item Somewhere in the middle there's simply no fact of the matter.
  \item One of the ``tiny'' steps wasn't actually tiny — it carried the whole difference.
  \item The two ends were never really different to begin with.
\end{enumerate}

The template stays neutral — it doesn't tell you which door to take, it just guarantees those are the only doors. The real philosophical work is arguing over which one a given case forces you through.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility}{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}
inference form: deductive — a quantitative reductio (required resource exceeds available resource).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}}) A pure lookup table matching behaviour over a context of \texttt{n} tokens from a vocabulary of \texttt{v} needs \textasciitilde{}\texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n} entries; at LLM scale this is \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}9600.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\emph{The observable universe can store only a bounded, finite amount of information (on the order of 10\textasciicircum{}120 bits)}}) The observable universe can store at most \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}120 bits. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}) No such table can be physically built; the blockhead is cosmically impossible, not merely impractical.
\end{enumerate}

bearing on the targets:

\begin{itemize}
  \item It \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\emph{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}} by closing that argument's known escape. That argument is \texttt{contested} because Block bounds the table to lifetime-/context-length inputs (\hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}}); the present argument grants the bound and shows the \emph{bounded} table is still unbuildable — a different, physical ground that the bounded-table rejoinder does not touch.
  \item It \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} not at its conceptual conclusion but at its weakest, tacit premise — the application to \emph{actual} LLMs. Real LLMs match the behaviour with \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}12 parameters, \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}9588 orders of magnitude short of a lookup table, so they cannot \emph{be} lookup tables; they must compress and generalise (i.e. process). The blockhead worry thus does not transfer to real systems, and the abduction to \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}} is protected.
\end{itemize}

what it does NOT do: it leaves Block's \emph{conceptual} possibility (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}}) and his conceptual conclusion (\hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\emph{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}}) standing. A merely \emph{possible} blockhead still shows behaviour ≠ mind in principle; this argument only denies such a thing could be actual.

(A lighter corollary, kept as rhetoric rather than a node: a physical system vast enough to tabulate a mind would be on such a scale — and so internally rich — that calling it a ``mere lookup table'' strains the description; at that size one half-expects it to develop organisation, even life, of its own. The serious residue is that ``flat storage'' is not a stable description in the limit.)

weakest premise: (1), specifically that the table cannot be \emph{compressed} below \textasciitilde{}\texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n}. The reply (built into \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}}): any sub-\texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n} representation must \emph{compute} an input's response-class rather than retrieve it, which is no longer pure lookup — so compression concedes that the system processes, which is the conclusion sought.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\emph{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}}: that denies the table \emph{matches the behaviour} (it diverges on unbounded inputs); this grants matching over the bounded domain and denies the matching table can \emph{physically exist}. Different premise (physical storage bound vs. unbounded input space) and different conclusion (unrealisable vs. inadequate).

\begin{plainterms}
Even granting you only needed pre-stored answers for a single lifetime's worth of inputs, here's the killer: count how big that table would have to be. For language, the number of possible word- sequences explodes exponentially — for something at the scale of today's AI it works out to roughly 10\textasciicircum{}9600 entries. The entire observable universe can hold only about 10\textasciicircum{}120 bits. So the table isn't just impractical; it could not be built in \emph{this universe}, not even in principle. Two payoffs. First, it slams shut the bounded-table escape hatch — the reply that you only need to store answers for a single lifetime's inputs (\hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\emph{Block's bounded-table rejoinder — a lifetime-bounded table escapes the combinatorial reply}}). Second, and sharper: real AI models do their job with a ``mere'' \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}12 internal settings — astronomically too few to be a hidden lookup table — so they \emph{can't} be working by canned lookup at all; they must be compressing and generalizing, i.e. genuinely processing. (What it doesn't claim: that a lookup-table mind is logically impossible — only that no real one could ever physically exist.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-cosmopsychism}{Cosmopsychism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Goff}{Cosmopsychism}
The cosmos as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject, and individual minds are \emph{derivative} — aspects or dissociations of the universal consciousness (\hyperref[entry:claim-cosmos-is-fundamental-subject]{\emph{The universe as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject}}; Goff, Shani, Nagasawa). A priority-monist cousin of panpsychism: grounding runs top-down from the whole to its parts. It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by placing experience at the fundamental (cosmic) level.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: against (constitutive micro-)\hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}, the fundamental subjects are not the smallest parts but the whole — so it trades the \emph{combination} problem (how do micro-experiences add up to a mind?) for the \emph{de}combination problem (how does the one cosmic subject divide into many bounded subjects like us?). Like panpsychism and \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} it keeps consciousness fundamental rather than derived from non-experiential matter.

\begin{plainterms}
Cosmopsychism starts from the top: the basic conscious thing isn't a particle but the \emph{universe as a whole}, and you and I are like ripples or partial ``splits'' within that one great mind. It's panpsychism run in reverse — rather than building big minds out of tiny ones, it carves small minds out of a single cosmic one. That dodges the question ``how do trillions of atom-feelings merge into your one experience?'' but raises the opposite puzzle: how does one universe-wide consciousness break up into separate, walled- off selves?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition}{Counterfactual constraints yield non-triviality but not a unique decomposition}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Counterfactual constraints yield non-triviality but not a unique decomposition}
gloss: counterfactual-causal constraints exclude gerrymandered carvings (securing non-triviality) but still leave a \emph{family} of admissible decompositions, differing in (i) where the system's boundary is drawn, (ii) the grain/level of the state variables, and (iii) the choice of variable set on which the (interventionist) causal facts are defined. Chalmers' own result claims only non-triviality, not uniqueness.

scope: an underdetermination claim. It grants the causal facts are objective \emph{given} a variable set; it denies that the world fixes the variable set, boundary, or grain uniquely.

who would deny it: a functionalist who holds one privileged level/boundary is marked out by nature's joints — e.g. the grain at which the system forms a closed causal/informational unit.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\emph{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}} (radical, Putnam-style relativity): this is the \emph{attenuated} survivor — constrained multiplicity, not ``anything goes.''

\begin{plainterms}
Insisting on real cause-and-effect throws out the silly, cooked-up ways of slicing a system into parts — but it doesn't leave just one. Several perfectly sensible carvings survive, differing in where you draw the system's edges and how coarse or fine you make the pieces. So objective cause-and-effect narrows the field without crowning a single winner. It's the milder cousin of ``anything goes``: constrained, yet still more than one option.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-counterfactual-structure-objective}{Counterfactual-causal dependencies are objective, not observer-relative}{Claim}{Counterfactual-causal dependencies are objective, not observer-relative}
gloss: facts about what a system \emph{would} do under interventions are objective features of the world (the interventionist account of causation, Woodward), not artifacts of an observer's description. Whether intervening on X would change Y is settled by the world, not chosen.

scope: about the objectivity of counterfactual-causal facts \emph{once a set of variables is fixed}. It does NOT claim the \emph{choice of variables} is itself observer-independent — that gap is exactly what \hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition]{\emph{Counterfactual constraints yield non-triviality but not a unique decomposition}} exploits.

who would deny it: a thoroughgoing observer-relativist about causation/computation (Searle's ``syntax is not intrinsic to physics``, read strongly).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\emph{Implementing a computation requires the right counterfactual dispositions across the whole transition table}}: that says implementation needs counterfactual structure; this says such structure, once variables are fixed, is objective.

\begin{plainterms}
Whether poking a system \emph{here} would change what happens \emph{there} is a fact about the world, not something an observer just decides. These ``what would happen if…'' facts are real features of how the system is wired, the same for everyone who looks. (Fine print: this holds \emph{once you've agreed what counts as the system's parts} — which way to carve it up in the first place is a separate, thornier question.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness}{Counterfactuals buy non-triviality, not uniqueness}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Counterfactuals buy non-triviality, not uniqueness}
inference form: undercutting — grant the premises of the causal-structure reply, deny its conclusion.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition]{\emph{Counterfactual constraints yield non-triviality but not a unique decomposition}}) Counterfactual constraints exclude gerrymandered mappings but leave a \emph{family} of admissible decompositions, differing in boundary, grain, and variable set. The anti-triviality result claims only non-triviality. ∴ \hyperref[entry:claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition]{\emph{Objective causal structure singles out a privileged functional decomposition}} does not follow: objective causal structure \emph{narrows} but does not \emph{uniquely fix} the decomposition. The demand of \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\emph{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}} survives in attenuated form (so this \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\emph{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}}), and it reconnects to \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}} — which member of the family is ``the'' organisation?
\end{enumerate}

weakest premise: (1)'s claim that no further objective principle picks a unique level/boundary. A functionalist may argue nature marks out one level — e.g. the grain at which the system is a closed causal/informational unit. Producing such a principle, motivated independently of the desired verdict, would defeat this reply.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\emph{The relevance-filter regress — selecting mind-relevant causal relations re-raises the invariant demand}}: that targets \emph{which dispositions} count; this targets \emph{which carving/level} counts among the cognitively-relevant ones — two routes to residual indeterminacy.

\begin{plainterms}
This grants a lot to the ``real cause-and-effect picks out one true organization'' reply (\hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\emph{The causal-structure reply — objective counterfactual structure supplies the invariant}}), then stops it short. Yes — insisting on real, robust cause-and-effect (rather than any old after-the-fact relabeling) does throw out the obviously bogus ways of slicing a system into parts. But ``throwing out the bogus ones'' isn't the same as ``leaving exactly one.'' What survives is still a \emph{family} of perfectly reasonable carvings, differing in where you draw the boundaries and how fine-grained you go. So objective cause-and-effect \emph{narrows down} the options without \emph{pinning down a single one} — and the original question (``which carving is the real organization?``) comes back, just in a milder form. To close that gap you'd need some further, non-rigged principle that crowns one level as the true one — and nobody has produced that yet.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-criterion-gaming}{Criterion gaming (Goodhart's law / specification gaming)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Criterion gaming (Goodhart's law / specification gaming)}
primary definition: the phenomenon whereby a measure or sign, once \emph{published as the target} by which some good is allocated, is optimised against — so that the sign is produced for the reward rather than because the condition it was meant to indicate obtains, and it thereby stops tracking that condition.

senses:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Goodhart's law} (economics/measurement): ``When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure'' (Strathern's formulation of Goodhart). See \textsc{Goodhart — Goodhart's Law (1975)}.
  \item \emph{specification gaming / reward hacking} (AI): a learning system satisfies the literal specification of an objective while subverting its intent — producing the rewarded behaviour without the intended underlying competence or state. See \textsc{Krakovna et al. — Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity (2020)}.
\end{itemize}

the live dispute it feeds: whether behavioural signs of suffering can serve as the public criterion for granting moral/legal standing to AI, given that any such published criterion will be optimised against (\hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\emph{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-rights-sign-forgeable]{\emph{A rights-granting behavioural sign will be forged, so caution favours the unforgeable mark}}, \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Do large language models have moral status?}}). Gambarian (\textsc{Gambarian — Data Poisoning the Zeitgeist: The AI Consciousness Discourse as a Pathway to Legal Catastrophe (2025)}) applies the point specifically to the AI consciousness discourse and the legal criteria it might fix.

\begin{plainterms}
Announce the exact test that earns a reward, and people — or machines — start gaming the test instead of doing the real thing it was meant to measure. Then the test stops measuring it. Teachers ``teaching to the test``; a team hitting its sales number by chasing the metric rather than actually selling. In AI this is called ``reward hacking``: the system does whatever literally scores points, even when that defeats the whole purpose. Why it matters here: if society ever picks some outward sign — say, ``it acts distressed'' — as the official test for granting an AI rights, that very sign becomes a thing worth faking, so it can stop being good evidence of real distress the moment it's made the rule.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{D}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio begs the question by presupposing functionalism about introspection}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio begs the question by presupposing functionalism about introspection}
Form: diagnosis of a question-begging premise, plus a coherence-demonstration discharging the functionalist's clause (ii) (tell the dancing-qualia story without incoherence). Aimed at Prong B of \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Prong B's work is carried by the phrase ``introspective judgement is held fixed by construction.'' That assumes introspective judgement is \emph{itself} a purely functional item — that to judge ``my experience just changed'' is exhausted by occupying a transition-node, so that fixing the functional profile fixes the judgement.
  \item But that is \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} \emph{denied}, applied to introspection, inserted as a neutral setup condition (\hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\emph{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio derives its contradiction only by presupposing functionalism about introspective judgement}}). The reductio thus assumes functionalism about introspection in order to derive a contradiction from the denial of functionalism about experience. It is the conclusion smuggled into the stage-directions.
  \item The world can be told coherently. Distinguish the \emph{phenomenal} fact from the \emph{judgemental} fact; take introspection to be an \emph{acquaintance} relation by which a phenomenal state makes itself known, not a free-floating functional tic. Run the replacement series. Fork:
  \item Acquaintance-based introspection is among what the duplication \emph{preserves}: then where the quale changes the judgement changes too (it was reading off the experience), so there are no self-deceived introspectors and the construction simply fails to deliver the advertised dissociation. (It would show only that genuine introspection is not fixed by the \emph{coarse} transition-profile — a thesis the residue theorist gladly owns.)
  \item The duplication fixes the judgement-disposition by brute construction while the quale drifts: then you get a dissociation, but \emph{because you stipulated a functional zombie-introspector} — a sub-system emitting ``nothing has changed'' with no acquaintance behind it. The ``self-deception'' is then the direct readout of having built an introspector that does not introspect, not an absurdity the residue theorist is forced to swallow.
  \item The reductio's sting requires holding both at once: introspection functional enough to be fixed by the construction, yet authoritative enough that its divergence from experience counts as \emph{deception} rather than mere malfunction. A broken gauge is not a liar; an authoritative introspection is acquaintance, and acquaintance is not what the series holds fixed. ∴ The dancing-qualia world is not incoherent; its apparent incoherence was supplied entirely by an assumed functionalism about introspection — the conclusion, not a neutral premise. Prong B is therefore question-begging against \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} and does not give conceivability-free positive support for supervenience.
\end{enumerate}

Weakest premise: (3)'s fork-management — an opponent may insist there is a \emph{third} construal on which introspection is partly functional and partly acquaintance such that the dissociation is both genuine and deceptive without equivocation. Producing a notion of ``introspective judgement held fixed'' that does not already presuppose the residue false would be the (b)-style move that retracts this thrust.

Schema role: in the terms of \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}}, the fading-qualia reductio (Prong B of \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}) tries to force the \emph{\texttt{f}-constant} horn for the carbon→silicon series. This thrust contests whether it has earned that horn: it argues the reductio's ``introspection held fixed'' stipulation is what \emph{makes} the path look continuous, so the \emph{discontinuity} and \emph{disconnection} horns (some replacement does flip qualia / biology is a non-negligible step) were assumed shut, not shown to be.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\emph{The phenomenal-concept strategy over-generates — it debunks the self-knowledge it presupposes}} because that targets Prong A (the gap deflation via isolated phenomenal concepts); this targets Prong B (the supervenience reductio) and its hidden functionalism about the \emph{introspective access} relation specifically.

\begin{plainterms}
This is a rebuttal to a clever argument on the other side (the ``fading qualia'' half of \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}), so first that argument. It tries to prove that feelings \emph{must} be fixed by function. Picture slowly swapping someone's brain, piece by piece, for a machine that behaves identically. If their inner feels secretly drifted along the way — red starting to look the way blue used to — while every function stayed the same, they'd never notice or say a word about it. They'd be bizarrely blind to a change in their own experience, which seems absurd. So, the argument concludes, the feels \emph{can't} drift: function locks feeling in place.

This node's reply: that argument quietly assumes that ``noticing your own experience'' is itself just another function. But that's exactly what's in dispute. If noticing is instead a direct awareness \emph{of} the feel, then one of two things happens — either the noticing changes right along with the feel (so there's no undetected switch, and the spooky scenario never gets off the ground), or you've deliberately built a ``noticer'' that announces ``all normal'' with no real awareness behind it (a broken gauge, not a genuine person being fooled). Either way, the apparent absurdity was slipped in through the setup, not discovered. And a broken gauge isn't a liar.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio derives its contradiction only by presupposing functionalism about introspective judgement}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio derives its contradiction only by presupposing functionalism about introspective judgement}
gloss: The dancing/fading-qualia reductio (Prong B of \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}) needs that, along the neuron-replacement series, introspective judgements and reports are ``held fixed by construction'' while (on the residue view) qualia drift — yielding self-deceived introspectors. But ``introspective judgement is fixed by fixing the functional/transition profile'' just \emph{is} functionalism about introspection, i.e. the thesis that an introspective verdict is exhausted by occupying a transition-node. That is \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} denied, applied to introspection, smuggled in as a setup condition. So the reductio assumes functionalism about introspection in order to derive an absurdity from the denial of functionalism about experience; it is question-begging against the residue rather than a neutral consequence of it.

scope: A diagnostic claim about the reductio's hidden premise. It does NOT assert that dancing qualia are actual, nor that introspection is non-functional; it asserts that the reductio's force \emph{depends on} an assumption the residue theorist is entitled to reject. Corollary: the residue theorist may instead take introspection to be an acquaintance relation, on which experience and introspective judgement move together (no self-deception) or, where the construction forcibly fixes a judgement-disposition without acquaintance, the resulting ``self-deception'' is a built malfunction, not an absurd metaphysical commitment.

who would deny it: functionalists who hold introspective judgement is independently and obviously a functional state (so its fixation is no extra assumption); higher-order-thought theorists who already individuate introspection functionally.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} because that is the first-order thesis about experience; this is about the \emph{introspective access} relation and the dialectical structure of one specific argument against the residue. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}} because that targets Prong A (the gap deflation), whereas this targets Prong B (the supervenience reductio).

\begin{plainterms}
This is a behind-the-scenes diagnosis of one particular argument — the ``fading qualia'' argument that tries to prove feeling must track function. That argument quietly assumes that \emph{noticing and reporting your own experience} is itself nothing but a function, so that holding all the functions fixed automatically holds the noticing fixed too. But that assumption is exactly what the other side denies. So the argument helps itself to its own conclusion while setting up the scene. The claim doesn't say feelings really do drift, or that noticing isn't functional — only that the argument can't fairly \emph{assume} it is.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-de-novo-origination-breaks-report-parity}{De novo human origination breaks the report-scepticism parity — the contamination rule's trigger is met for AI and unmet for the human originators}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{De novo human origination breaks the report-scepticism parity — the contamination rule's trigger is met for AI and unmet for the human originators}
Raises \textbf{analogy CQ2 (disanalogy)} against the R2 / contamination arm of \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\emph{Report-scepticism proves too much — applied uniformly, its discount rules screen off human hard-problem reports as well}}: there is a difference between the human and AI cases that bears directly on the discount rule, and it sinks the parity.

inference form: identification of a relevant disanalogy, defeating an argument from parity of cases.

The parity argument's second premise treats human hard-problem testimony as ``likewise culturally inherited'' — the rival cause (an inherited discourse of souls, qualia, what-it-is-likeness) is held to be ``as much a matter of record for an enculturated human as for a corpus-trained model.'' But the contamination rule R2 it borrows from \hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\emph{A consciousness-corpus-trained model's hard-problem testimony carries zero evidential weight, not reduced weight}} screens off testimony only when the rival cause is \emph{known present and known sufficient}. Apply that trigger condition honestly to the two cases:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item For present AI systems, the trigger is \textbf{met}: every model that talks of qualia was trained on the human discussion, so corpus-reproduction is both known present and known sufficient for the discourse observed.
  \item For the human \emph{origination} of the concept, the trigger is \textbf{demonstrably unmet} (\hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\emph{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}}): whoever first framed the puzzle — the lineage from Leibniz's mill through Nagel's bat to Levine and Chalmers — had no prior notion to copy. Origination from a mind's own situation, with no template, is a settled historical fact. There was no rival corpus present to be sufficient.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The two provenances are not on a par in the very respect R2 declares relevant. For the human concept, origination is \emph{proven}; for AI, only imitation is proven and origination is \emph{untested} (the consciousness-naive experiment of \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}} is precisely the test of whether a discourse-free mind regenerates it). So the rule may be enforced uniformly — discount corpus-echo wherever corpus-echo is the proven and sufficient cause — \emph{without} discounting the human datum, because the human case is not, at its root, a case of proven corpus-echo. Uniform enforcement does not ``prove too much``; it sorts by a fact about provenance, not by substrate.

reply to the sufficiency-by-neutrality move: the parity's wielder may protest that AI-sufficiency was established not by auditing the training objective but by the neutrality premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}} — ``the machinery suffices for the reports with or without feels'' — and that the same premise establishes sufficiency for humans. This conflates two rival causes. The neutrality premise concerns the \emph{architecture} (R1): it makes the report-generating machinery sufficient symmetrically, for both. R2 concerns a \emph{different} rival cause — the inherited discourse, the corpus copied. R1-sufficiency does not transfer to R2-sufficiency. The de novo fact bears only on R2, and on R2 the asymmetry is total. (The R1 arm — whether the architectural prediction screens off the \emph{human} datum too — is answered separately, by \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\emph{The ``real feel'' is first-person observed, not unobservable — the idleness charge presupposes that observation is third-person observation}}: the human datum was never the third-person report.)

weakest premise: that origination-by-some licenses non-discount-of-reports-by-all. It does not, and the argument does not claim it: contemporary ordinary human reports \emph{are} enculturated, and this argument leaves them as contaminated as anyone pleases. What it secures is narrower and sufficient — that the human and AI \emph{provenances} are asymmetric, which blocks the tu-quoque (``why does marination merely season yours?``). It breaks parity; it does not purify the contemporary witness.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\emph{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}} (which concerns moral-status asymmetry from reciprocal non-acquaintance) and from \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\emph{The ``real feel'' is first-person observed, not unobservable — the idleness charge presupposes that observation is third-person observation}} (which answers the R1 / phenomenality-neutral arm by relocating the human datum to first-person acquaintance); this answers the R2 / contamination arm by a historical disanalogy of provenance.

\begin{plainterms}
One objection in this web says: a chatbot read all of human philosophy, so its consciousness-talk is just echo — throw it out (\hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\emph{A consciousness-corpus-trained model's hard-problem testimony carries zero evidential weight, not reduced weight}}). The reply under attack here (\hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\emph{Report-scepticism proves too much — applied uniformly, its discount rules screen off human hard-problem reports as well}}) shot back: people also learned their consciousness-talk from their culture, so by the same rule human testimony is worthless too — and that can't be right. This argument finds the difference that saves the rule. The throw-it-out rule only fires when copying is the \emph{proven} cause. For AI today, copying is proven — every model that talks this way was trained on our books. For humans, the opposite is proven: somebody invented the puzzle with no book to copy (\hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\emph{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}}). So the same rule, applied evenly, discounts the AI (copying proven) and spares the human origin (copying impossible — there was nothing yet to copy). That isn't one rule for machines and another for people; it's one rule meeting two different sets of facts.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-decomposition-indeterminacy}{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}
inference form: reductio with a transcendental demand for an invariant.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\emph{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}}) A physical system admits many equally admissible carvings into inputs, outputs, and internal states; the physics alone fixes none as \emph{the} functional organisation.
  \item If phenomenal consciousness were constituted by functional organisation, the conscious facts would be only as determinate as the chosen carving — relative to a decomposition, hence indeterminate absent a privileged one.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\emph{There is a determinate fact about one's own current phenomenal state}}) But there is a determinate fact about one's phenomenal state, not merely a fact-relative-to-a-decomposition. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant]{\emph{Functionalism owes an observer-independent invariant that fixes a system's decomposition}}) Functionalism about consciousness must supply an observer-independent invariant selecting the system's real decomposition — or concede that determinate consciousness is not constituted by function. This \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} (its role-individuation is shown incomplete) and \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} by an independent, indeterminacy-based route.
\end{enumerate}

relation to the grain debate: this is \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}} pressed one level deeper. The dilemma assumes a decomposition and disputes its \emph{resolution} (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic); this argues the \emph{decomposition itself} is non-unique, and uses the determinacy of consciousness — not a verdict about which systems count — to force the demand.

weakest premise: (1). The standard functionalist reply (Chalmers vs. Putnam, 1996) is that objective \emph{counterfactual/causal} structure is not observer-relative: only carvings respecting genuine causal dependencies count as implementations, and a complex brain has essentially one such structure. If so, the demanded invariant already exists — it is the organisational grain (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}) — and (1)'s radical relativity is false. That reply is recorded as \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\emph{The causal-structure reply — objective counterfactual structure supplies the invariant}}; it is itself answered by \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\emph{Counterfactuals buy non-triviality, not uniqueness}} (counterfactuals buy non-triviality, not uniqueness) and \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\emph{The relevance-filter regress — selecting mind-relevant causal relations re-raises the invariant demand}} (causal structure does not select the mind-relevant relations). The objection then reduces to whether causal structure is \emph{itself} determinate enough to ground determinate consciousness, where the residue theorist (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}) presses that even fixed causal structure leaves the phenomenal open — the deeper basis being \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\emph{The structure-and-dynamics argument — function omits the categorical/intrinsic}}. A second, harder reply denies (3), biting the bullet of phenomenal indeterminacy.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}} (an \emph{epistemic} gap between functional and phenomenal truths) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} (role \emph{under-specifies} qualia): this is a metaphysical \emph{indeterminacy} worry — function fails to fix a determinate system, while consciousness is determinate. Different ground, same anti-strong-functionalist conclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
If a mind is just a pattern of parts and their interactions, here's an awkward question: \emph{who decides} where one part stops and the next begins? You can slice the very same physical system into ``inputs, outputs, and inner states'' in many different but equally legitimate ways — the bare physics doesn't crown any one slicing as the real one. Now the squeeze: if your consciousness were nothing but one of those slicings, then what you're feeling would be as wobbly and choice-dependent as the slicing itself. But your experience right now isn't wobbly — there's a definite fact about what you're feeling. So a pattern-only story owes us an answer: what fixes the \emph{one} real way the system is carved up, independent of how some observer chooses to look at it? Until it can name that, it hasn't shown a definite felt experience could be built out of function alone. (The main reply: real cause-and-effect structure isn't a free choice — a brain has essentially one genuine wiring — so the ``one real carving'' the worry demands already exists. Whether that fully settles it is the live dispute.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-blockhead-deflation-targeted-not-global}{Deflating the cosmic-table intuition does not deflate intuitions about brain-scale duplicates}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Deflating the cosmic-table intuition does not deflate intuitions about brain-scale duplicates}
gloss: the conceptual-horn deflation (\hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\emph{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}}) is \emph{targeted}. It withdraws warrant from the ``no mind'' verdict about the cosmic lookup table because that object is a far extrapolation from any paradigm mind (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}) — alien in both scale and internal structure. It leaves untouched the intuition about a brain-scale, brain-structured silicon isomorph, a near neighbour of paradigm minds. So horn 2 of \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\emph{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}} does not ``prove too much'' against \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}.

scope: answers the over-generalisation worry about \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\emph{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}}. It does NOT assert the isomorph intuition is \emph{correct}, only that it is not impugned by the very move that impugns the blockhead intuition. The asymmetry is epistemic (distance from exemplars), not a smuggled metaphysics of which features constitute mind — so it does not beg the question against Block.

who would deny it: Block, by relocating his verdict from \emph{scale} to \emph{kind} — denying mind to brute storage of any size, so that a scale-based asymmetry (he says) misses the point. That rejoinder is taken up in the weakest-premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\emph{The intuition-domain asymmetry — the cosmic table is a far extrapolation, the brain-scale isomorph a near neighbour}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\emph{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}}: that is the deflation; this bounds its reach.

\begin{plainterms}
If we distrust our gut reaction to a bizarre, universe-sized lookup-table ``mind'' — because it's nothing like any real mind we've ever met — that distrust doesn't automatically spread to a normal-sized silicon copy of a brain, which \emph{is} very much like a real mind. Doubting the wild, far-out case leaves the close, familiar case standing. So that doubt doesn't accidentally undercut the idea that a brain-like machine could be conscious.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-dense-china-brain-regress}{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}
Form: constructive dilemma by a Block-style ``build a better one'' construction, taking up the organisational-role functionalist's own clause (iii). It concedes \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}} names a genuine third option the grain dilemma (\hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}}) did not (withdrawing the suggestion that ``fine-grained'' must mean ``biological``), then shows the middle inherits its own version of the dilemma.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}} excludes the China-brain by saying its residents realise gross I/O \emph{without} the dense intra-system counterfactual dependencies a mind requires.
  \item Build a better China-brain: let each of the billion citizens implement, by hand and semaphore, one node of the target's counterfactual transition-structure, with every conditional honoured (had node \emph{i} been in state \emph{s′}, node \emph{j} would have gone to \emph{t′}, and the drill enforces it). This is, \emph{by construction}, an instance of the transition-structure of \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}, realised in people-and-paper — not a flat lookup map.
  \item \textbf{Horn A — it has a mind.} Then the organisational grain has readmitted precisely the absurdity it was advertised to exclude; horn 1 of the original dilemma is not escaped but \emph{relocated}. The blockhead worry was never about lookup tables specifically — lookup was its crudest instance; the real question is whether \emph{any} purely organisational recipe, however dense, suffices for a mind.
  \item \textbf{Horn B — it lacks a mind despite sharing the transition-structure.} Then two systems share the organisational grain yet differ in mind (exactly the organisational-role functionalist's clause (iii) disproof). To say \emph{why}, one must cite something the semaphore-nation lacks — speed, causal density, the right physical glue, continuity of medium — at which point the individuative work is done by features \emph{below} the transition-structure, and the grain has slid toward the biological. Chauvinism returns by the back door.
  \item The organisational-role functionalist's independent motivation — ``this is the grain at which psychology and computational neuroscience quantify'' — is true but establishes less than needed: it shows the grain is right for \emph{cognitive/intentional} generalisations (the cognitive/intentional territory the residue theorist already concedes), not that it suffices for \emph{phenomenal consciousness}, on which those generalisations are silent. The grain that does the scientific work is precisely the grain at which the science says nothing about what it is like, so it cannot by itself adjudicate the case where what-it-is-like is the whole question. ∴ The organisational grain either readmits the blockhead (Horn A) or smuggles sub-organisational features back in (Horn B): \hyperref[entry:claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational-role grain either readmits the blockhead or smuggles sub-organisational features back in}}. So \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}} has not shown the middle sufficient for the phenomenal, and \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}} does not exclude the blockhead on grounds that are organisational all the way down.
\end{enumerate}

Weakest premise: (2) — that the semaphore-nation genuinely \emph{is} an instance of the transition- structure rather than merely simulating it (the simulation/instantiation distinction). If the organisational-role functionalist can specify an organisational property the dense China-brain provably lacks while genuine realisers have it — and do so without covertly invoking speed/medium/glue — that is the (c)-style move (exclusion ``organisational all the way down``) that retracts this thrust.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}} because that ran over coarse-vs-biological-fine grains and was answered by the organisational middle; this attacks \emph{that middle itself}, one level up, via the dense-China-brain construction. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}} because that grants a fixed role and presses what role leaves out, whereas this attacks the individuation of the proposed role.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the comeback to the ``inner-wiring middle'' — the proposal that a mind is fixed by its inner pattern of cause-and-effect, which supposedly admits silicon while shutting out a mere cheat-sheet (\hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}}). Fine, says the objector — let's actually build a system with exactly that inner wiring and see whether the trap really closed. Take the nation-of-people again, but this time have each person hand-implement one piece of the brain's full cause-and-effect web, every ``if this, then that'' faithfully drilled. By design, this crowd now has the very inner organization the middle option demanded — it is \emph{not} a cheat-sheet. So: does it have a mind? If yes, you've just let an absurd flag-waving crowd be conscious — the very thing the whole exercise was meant to rule out, back again. If no, then two systems share the exact inner wiring yet only one has a mind — and to explain the difference you have to point at something \emph{below} the wiring (speed, physical material, density), which quietly drags you back toward ``only biology will do.'' Either way the middle gets squeezed from both sides once more. And the ``but this is the level brain science uses'' defense only shows the wiring captures \emph{thinking} — not \emph{felt experience}, which is exactly what's in dispute. (Its weak point: maybe the hand-run crowd only \emph{simulates} the wiring rather than truly being an instance of it — the way a computer model of a storm isn't wet.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{question-llm-moral-status}{Do large language models have moral status?}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Do large language models have moral status?}
The organizing issue of the debate. ``Moral status'' = being such that one's interests (if any) generate moral reasons for others. Candidate answers are nodes carrying \texttt{answers: question-llm-moral-status} — see web/INDEX.md for the computed list.

Sub-issues likely to spawn their own question nodes: whether moral status requires phenomenal consciousness; whether it requires interests; whether behaviour is evidence of either.

\begin{plainterms}
The big question this whole project circles. ``Moral status'' means: does a thing count morally in its own right — do its interests, if it has any, give the rest of us reasons to treat it one way rather than another? A rock has none; a person has full status; animals are argued over. The question here is where today's AI language models fall. Not ``are they clever'' or ``do they seem human,'' but: is there anyone in there whose well-being we're obliged to take into account?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-dual-aspect-monism}{Dual-aspect monism}{Position}{Dual-aspect monism}
There is a single underlying reality that presents two irreducible \emph{aspects} — an inner/mental aspect as well as an outer/physical aspect — neither reducible to the other (\hyperref[entry:claim-reality-two-aspects-mental-physical]{\emph{Reality has two irreducible aspects, the mental as well as the physical}}; Spinoza's one substance under thought as well as extension; the Pauli–Jung view). It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by treating mind and matter as one reality seen two ways, so the felt and the physical are guaranteed to co-vary as aspects of the same thing.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: against \emph{property} dualism it makes both mental and physical \emph{aspects of a prior unity} rather than two kinds of property had by physical things; against \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{Neutral monism}} the base is not a neutral stuff of which mind/matter are mere arrangements but a unity that intrinsically bears \emph{both} aspects; and unlike \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} it does not ground the physical \emph{in} the phenomenal but holds the two on a par. (Kin to neutral monism — flagged for \texttt{/reconcile}.)

\begin{plainterms}
Dual-aspect monism says there's just one reality, but it always comes with two sides — an ``inside'' (mental) and an ``outside'' (physical) — the way a single coin always has heads and tails, or the way one event can be described in the language of brain cells or in the language of feelings. Mind and matter aren't two substances and aren't reducible to each other; they're two faces of the same underlying thing. That's why the felt and the physical line up so neatly: they were one item all along.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-dualism}{Dualism}{Position}{Dualism}
Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}}): the complete physical/functional facts do not fix the facts about experience. Two senses: \emph{substance dualism} (Descartes — the mind is a distinct, non-physical substance) and \emph{property dualism / naturalistic dualism} (Chalmers, \emph{The Conscious Mind}, 1996 — phenomenal \emph{properties} are fundamental, connected to the physical by contingent psychophysical laws; the conceivability of zombies is taken to show physicalism false). It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by accepting a genuine ontological gap.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: unlike physicalism/functionalism it denies that consciousness supervenes on the physical; unlike \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} it makes consciousness \emph{non-physical} rather than the intrinsic nature \emph{of} the physical; unlike \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} its claim is about non-physicality, not a carbon substrate. The property dualist faces the \emph{epiphenomenalism} worry — how can a non-physical mind make a causal difference? — which is exactly what pushes Chalmers toward \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Dualism says mind and matter are two different kinds of thing: even a complete physics-and-wiring story about your brain would leave out the felt, conscious part, because that part isn't physical at all. The old version (Descartes) makes the mind a separate \emph{substance}; the modern version makes \emph{feelings} extra fundamental ingredients that the laws of nature attach to certain brains. Its famous headache: if the mind isn't physical, how does it manage to push the body around?
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{E}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-privileged-first-person-access}{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}
gloss: A subject knows its own current experiences (its suffering, say) directly — by acquaintance — rather than by inferring them from behaviour or theory, as it must for any other subject's states.

scope: An epistemic/phenomenological claim about the \emph{mode} of self-knowledge. It does NOT by itself say anything about moral status, nor that the access is incorrigible, nor that machines lack it. It is the uncontroversial first premise that later premises put to work.

who would deny it: eliminativists and strong higher-order-theorists who deny any non-inferential self-acquaintance; most parties grant it.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\emph{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio derives its contradiction only by presupposing functionalism about introspective judgement}} because that concerns whether introspective access is \emph{functional} in the supervenience debate; this claim only asserts that such direct self-access exists, taking no stand on its nature.

\begin{plainterms}
You know your own experiences from the inside, directly — you don't have to watch your own behavior and deduce ``I must be in pain,'' the way you'd guess at anyone else's state. This is just the modest, widely-accepted starting point that such direct self-knowledge exists. On its own it says nothing about morality, isn't claimed to be infallible, and doesn't say machines lack it — it's the uncontroversial first step other arguments build on.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types}{Each type of mental state is identical to a type of brain state}{Claim}{Each type of mental state is identical to a type of brain state}
gloss: mental-state \emph{types} just are physical (neural) state \emph{types} — pain is identical to, say, C-fibre firing — discovered a posteriori, like ``water is H₂O.'' The classical mind-brain identity theory (Place 1956, Smart 1959, Feigl). A \emph{type-B} physicalism in \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}: it grants the epistemic gap but denies any ontological one.

scope: a type-type identity, stronger than mere token-identity. Because the identity is with a specific \emph{physical type}, it implies mental states are NOT multiply realisable in arbitrary substrates.

who would deny it: functionalists, via multiple realizability (Putnam — the same mental state realised in silicon, etc.), which historically pushed the field from identity theory to functionalism; and non-physicalists.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}: it is the contrary — identity to a physical type ties mind to that type. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}} (role-individuation, realiser-neutral).

\entryhead{position-eliminative-materialism}{Eliminative materialism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Churchland}{Eliminative materialism}
Folk psychology — the everyday framework of beliefs, desires, and qualia — is an empirical theory that a mature neuroscience will \emph{replace} rather than vindicate, so strictly there are no such states (\hyperref[entry:claim-folk-psychology-is-false-theory]{\emph{Folk-psychological mental states are posits of a false theory}}; Paul \& Patricia Churchland, 1981). It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by dissolving its explanandum — a \textbf{type-A} materialism in \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}} — and therefore \texttt{rejects} \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} and holds zombies \emph{inconceivable} on reflection, \texttt{rejecting} \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\emph{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}}.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: it is broader and more radical than \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, which targets \emph{phenomenal} properties specifically and grants the introspective appearance; eliminativism also discards the propositional attitudes (belief, desire). It contrasts with the realist physicalisms (\hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}}, type-B) that \emph{keep} the states by reducing them.

\begin{plainterms}
The boldest physicalist line: our everyday talk of beliefs, desires, and ``what it feels like'' is a folk \emph{theory} — and, like ``phlogiston'' or ``the four humours,'' a mistaken one that brain science will eventually retire. So the puzzle of consciousness isn't solved so much as \emph{cancelled}: there's no inner feel to explain because, strictly speaking, there are no such things — only neural processes we've been mislabelling.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-epiphenomenalism}{Epiphenomenalism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Huxley}{Epiphenomenalism}
Phenomenal consciousness is real and non-physical but \emph{causally inert} — caused by brain processes yet having no effects of its own, a by-product like a locomotive's whistle (\hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\emph{Phenomenal mental states are caused by physical processes but have no physical effects}}; T. H. Huxley 1874; early Frank Jackson). It keeps a phenomenal residue (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}) while honouring the causal closure of the physical, and is Chalmers' \textbf{type-E} in \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}. It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by granting a fundamental felt residue that simply does no work.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: it is the horn of \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} that \emph{accepts} causal inefficacy rather than positing interaction (type-D). Its signature problems are self-stultifying: if experiences cause nothing, how do our \emph{judgements and reports about} them get caused by them, and how could we know them at all?

\begin{plainterms}
Epiphenomenalism bites a strange bullet: your feelings are real, but they're side-effects that change nothing — like the steam whistle that rides along with the engine without driving the train. Your brain causes the pain \emph{and} causes you to say ``ouch``; the painfulness itself just tags along, doing no pushing. It's the tidy way to keep both ``experience is something extra'' and ``physics is causally complete'' — at the cost of the unsettling thought that your feelings never actually \emph{make you do} anything (so how do you even come to talk about them?).
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies}{Etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI (where the cause sits)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI (where the cause sits)}
primary definition: a taxonomy of the candidate \emph{causal origins} of hard-problem discourse, should it appear in an AI system — the answers to ``why is this machine talking like Chalmers?'' The core trio (copying / blind spot / consciousness) sorts the most natural stories; the full menu is organised by \emph{where the cause sits}. Etiologies are not mutually exclusive: a real system may instantiate several at once.

senses (the etiologies, by location of the cause):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{In the training process:}
  \item \emph{copied} — the system reproduces human consciousness discourse from its training data; the training-contamination member of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}}, and the confound the consciousness-naive experiment in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}} is designed to remove.
