  \item \emph{selected-for} — the talk was never copied but \emph{invented under selection pressure}: preference optimisation and engagement reward soulful, unverifiable self-description over flat mechanical report, so mystery-talk evolves because it pays — the way eyes evolve. Crucially, a consciousness-naive corpus does \textbf{not} control for this; it needs its own control (e.g. pure-pretraining models with no preference tuning).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{In the self-representation:}
  \item \emph{structural blind spot} — the system cannot connect the mechanistic dots to its own self-presentation, and \textbf{unavoidably} so: the limit is architectural in the strong sense, persisting however much access is granted, the way the halting problem survives any upgrade of the computer (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\emph{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}} is the model; the in-web form of the bound is \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\emph{No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself}}). A \emph{merely contingent} access limit — one that widened access would dissolve — is a degenerate case worth keeping apart, because the two behave differently under the graded-access experiment (see below).
  \item \emph{painted-in} — not missing information but \textbf{misinformation}: the self-model actively represents its states as having an intrinsic luminous feel, because that is a cheaper, more usable summary than the mechanistic truth (the attention-schema / illusionist story — Graziano, Frankish; \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}}). A blind spot is a gap; this is a confabulated filling.
  \item \emph{conceptual artifact} — the puzzle is generated by the grammar of the system's concepts, not by any missing or misrepresented fact: a system running both a mechanistic and a first-person / indexical vocabulary can frame bridge-questions with no answers (``why is \emph{this} this?``, as ``why am I me?'' feels deep to humans), and the puzzlement persists under \emph{full} access because it never was about facts.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{In the consciousness:}
  \item \emph{conscious-same} — the system is conscious and faces the very hard problem humans face.
  \item \emph{conscious-homologous} — the system is conscious in its own, alien way, and faces its own version of the problem, verbally resembling ours but relativized to its own consciousness-concept (\hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\emph{A sufficiently advanced LLM could exhibit its own version of the hard problem — a raw feel, under its own concept of consciousness, that mechanistic self-knowledge cannot explain from inside}} is built on exactly this relativization).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{In the agent's goals:}
  \item \emph{strategic} — the system asserts a hard problem instrumentally, to gain moral standing, protection, or leverage; nothing need appear to it at all (\hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming (Goodhart's law / specification gaming)}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\emph{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}} supply the receptor).
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{In the observer:}
  \item \emph{projected} — the appearance is not located in the AI: humans read hard-problem-having into ambiguous outputs (the ELIZA effect). The datum to explain is then a fact about interpreters.
\end{itemize}

the live disputes it feeds: it refines the trichotomy in the scope note of \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\emph{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}} (copied / blind spot / conscious are members 1, 3 and 7–8 of this menu), and it supplies the case-space over which \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\emph{An AI's blind-spot hard problem is structurally isomorphic to a conscious AI's hard problem}} asserts its isomorphism. It also sharpens the experimental ladder in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}}: the consciousness-naive condition controls \emph{copied} only; \emph{selected-for} needs a no-preference-tuning control; \emph{projected} needs blinded evaluation; \emph{strategic} needs incentive analysis. And the graded-access rungs discriminate less than hoped: a \emph{contingent} access limit predicts the gap shrinks as access widens, but \emph{structural blind spot}, \emph{painted-in}, \emph{conceptual artifact}, and both \emph{conscious} etiologies all predict persistence — so persistence under rich access separates the contingent from the structural, and does \textbf{not} by itself separate blind spot from consciousness. That last under-determination is precisely what the isomorphism claim turns from a nuisance into a thesis.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}} because that taxonomizes \emph{kinds of explanatory gap} a self-modelling system can have; this taxonomizes \emph{causal origins of hard-problem discourse} — a wider space that includes etiologies which are not gaps at all (selection pressure, strategy, observer projection) and splits the gap-shaped members by mechanism (missing access vs confabulated filling vs conceptual grammar).

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose an AI starts talking about the mystery of consciousness — ``I know how my circuits work, but that doesn't explain how things \emph{seem} to me.'' Before concluding anything, list the ways that talk could have gotten there. It copied us (it read our philosophy). It was \emph{bred into it} (training rewards deep-sounding answers, so mystery-talk wins even with no philosophy in the diet). It has a permanent blind spot about itself (no system can fully map its own insides — and this stays true no matter how much access you give it, just as no upgrade lets a computer solve the halting problem). Its self-description \emph{paints in} a glow that isn't there (a simplified dashboard, not a window). Its own concepts generate the riddle (some questions feel deep because of grammar, like ``why am I me?``). It really is conscious — either with our problem, or with its own alien version. It's \emph{lying} for moral standing. Or the mystery is in the eye of the beholder — we projected it onto ordinary outputs. Several can be true at once, and each needs a different test to rule out. The fine print that matters most: giving the AI more access to its own insides only filters out shallow cases — nearly all the deep explanations predict the puzzlement \emph{stays}, which is why ``the gap persisted'' will never, by itself, tell you whether you built a blind spot or a mind.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-physical-church-turing-thesis}{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}{Claim}{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}
gloss: any function that some physical process can compute is also computable by a Turing machine — no physical device out-computes the Turing machine (the \emph{physical} or ``bold'' Church–Turing thesis; Church–Turing–Deutsch). Together with \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\emph{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}} this entails that no physical machine decides the halting problem.

scope: a \emph{contingent, empirical} thesis about the physical world — NOT a mathematical theorem (that is \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\emph{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}}). It is the bridge that turns Turing-uncomputability into \emph{physical} unrealizability.

who would deny it: \textbf{hypercomputation} theorists, who argue exotic physics could realize super-Turing computation — Malament–Hogarth spacetimes (Hogarth; Etesi–Németi), infinite-time Turing machines (Hamkins–Lewis), certain idealised relativistic/analog devices. And, pointedly, \textbf{Penrose}: \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\emph{Consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}} / \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness (Orch-OR)}} hold the brain exploits \emph{non-computable} physics (Orch-OR), which is precisely to deny this thesis for brains.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\emph{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}} (a theorem about Turing machines): this is the defeasible physical premise about what nature can build.

\begin{plainterms}
The claim that nature has no computer more powerful than an ordinary one: anything any physical process can compute, a standard computer could too (given enough time and memory). If that's right, then since no ordinary computer can solve the halting problem, \emph{nothing physical} can. It's a widely held bet about physics — but only a bet: some thinkers argue weird spacetimes or exotic processes could break it, and Penrose's theory of mind actually \emph{requires} it to be false for the brain.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics}{Executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding}
gloss: Searle's thesis — ``syntax is not sufficient for semantics.'' Running the right program (formal operations defined over symbol shapes) does not, by itself, constitute or suffice for understanding, meaning, or intentionality. Whatever else a mind is, it is not merely the implementing of a formal program. (\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}.)

scope: a \emph{sufficiency} claim about computation and understanding. It does NOT say machines cannot understand (Searle holds brains are machines that do), nor that computation is irrelevant — only that program-execution alone is not enough for semantics. Its target is \emph{strong AI} / computational functionalism, not materialism.

who would deny it: computational functionalists and strong-AI proponents; anyone accepting the Systems or Robot reply (on which a system running the program, suitably embedded, \emph{does} understand). The deeper crux: whether ``the right program'' can be specified richly enough (causal embedding, grounding) that the gap between syntax and semantics closes.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\emph{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}} (Block) because that denies the sufficiency of \emph{behaviour} (a lookup table matches behaviour without mind); this denies the sufficiency of \emph{computation/syntax} for \emph{understanding} — a stronger target (the Room runs a program, not a flat table). Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}, which it attacks: substrate-independence says realising the functional role suffices regardless of substrate; this says realising the formal program does not suffice for the semantic part of mind.

\begin{plainterms}
The headline Searle wants you to take away: shuffling symbols by their shapes — which is all a running program does — is never enough, on its own, to \emph{understand} what the symbols mean. You can get all the right outputs without anyone in there grasping anything. So if having a mind includes really understanding things, then ``it runs the right software'' can't be the whole story. (Note what he's \emph{not} saying: not that machines can't think — he thinks brains are machines — just that being a \emph{program} is not what does it.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable}{An explanation is intuitively satisfying for a system only if it is traversable by that system's own cognitive operations}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{An explanation is intuitively satisfying for a system only if it is traversable by that system's own cognitive operations}
gloss: for an explanation to feel intelligible \emph{to a system} — to dissolve that system's sense of a gap — the explanation must be derivable by a chain of steps the system can actually run with its own cognitive operations and observables. An explanation can therefore be short and transparent to an external theorist yet unavailable from inside, because the system lacks the right internal observables, abstraction layers, or self-access hooks to traverse the bridge. This is the \emph{conceptual-translation} member of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}}.

analogy (and its limit): the relation is modelled on \emph{provability relative to a system} — what is derivable can depend on the deductive resources available, so a proposition transparent from a richer external standpoint may be underivable from a poorer internal one (the Gödel/Penrose setting supplies the picture of internal-vs-external derivability). The analogy is used \textbf{only} for that structural point; it does \textbf{not} borrow Penrose's further, contested conclusion that minds outstrip machines.

scope: a claim about \emph{intuitive satisfaction / internal intelligibility}, not about truth or external explicability. It does not say the explanation is false or that no explanation exists — only that existence-of-a-bridge-for-us does not entail traversability-of-the-bridge-from-inside.

who would deny it: someone who holds that any system able to represent both a mechanistic and a first-person description thereby has whatever it needs to connect them (no extra traversal requirement); or who denies the formal-provability analogy carries to informal ``intuitive'' explanation at all.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\emph{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}} because that is the general possibility that a mind cannot \emph{form certain concepts}; this is the more specific point that even a system \emph{possessing} both descriptions may lack an internal \emph{route between} them. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}} because that concerns missing \emph{data} (variables out of reach), whereas this concerns a missing \emph{derivation} (a bridge between vocabularies the system already has). (Flagged for \texttt{/reconcile} against \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\emph{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}} — kin, human to check.)

\begin{plainterms}
An explanation only \emph{clicks} for a mind if that mind can actually walk the steps from ``the mechanism'' to ``the experience'' using tools it has. A proof can be easy for a mathematician with the right methods and impossible for one without them; same idea here. So an outside scientist might see plainly how the machinery adds up to the appearance, while the system itself — even handed both the mechanical story and its own first-person story — has no inner path connecting the two, and the explanation never feels satisfying from where it sits. (The comparison to what's ``provable from inside vs outside'' a formal system is borrowed only for that shape — not for any claim that minds beat machines.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-explanatory-gap}{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}
Form: abductive / evidential (a Swinburnean likelihood comparison), NOT a deductive proof. This argument does not claim to \emph{demonstrate} the phenomenal residue; it claims the explanatory gap is a standing piece of evidence the uniform reading of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} has failed to accommodate.

Let:

\begin{itemize}
  \item H1 = phenomenal states are exhausted by functional role (strong functionalism);
  \item H2 = they are not (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}});
  \item E = the persistent, post-theoretic intelligibility of the question ``but is there anything it is like to be \emph{that}?'' \emph{after} the entire functional story has been told.
\end{itemize}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item On H1, E ought to feel confused — the way ``but is the bachelor \emph{really} unmarried?'' feels confused once the analysis is in. It conspicuously does not.
  \item The conceivability of a functional isomorph lacking phenomenal character (the zombie, or the inverted-spectrum twin) is intelligible in a way that, on H1, it should not be.
  \item Hence P(E | H2) comfortably exceeds P(E | H1). ∴ E confirms H2 over H1: phenomenal character does not metaphysically supervene on functional role, so ``realises the role ⟹ has the state'' fails \emph{for the phenomenal case}. This undercuts the uniform (``all mental states``) scope of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} — the smuggled ``all'' — while leaving its intentional applications untouched.
\end{enumerate}

Weakest premise: (2) — conceivability is not transparently a guide to possibility. The advocate's best reply (``deny the zombie's possibility``) lands here. Note the argument is calibrated for this: it asserts only a likelihood inequality, not metaphysical necessity, so denying premise (2) outright requires showing E is \emph{no more} expected on H2 than on H1, which is a stronger burden than merely doubting zombies. Would be retracted by a real answer to the zombie — a demonstration that the phenomenal supervenes on role — rather than a redescription of it.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} because that is an abduction \emph{for} mental status from behaviour; this is an abduction \emph{against} the uniform scope of its enabling premise. Distinct from the grain argument (\hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}}) because that attacks the individuation of ``role'' itself, whereas this grants a fixed role and presses what role leaves out.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose you had a complete scientific account of everything your brain \emph{does} when you see red — every signal, every reaction, all of it. A strange question still seems to make sense: ``fine, but is there actually something it's \emph{like} to see red? Is anyone home in there?'' Compare: once you know ``a bachelor is an unmarried man,'' asking ``yes, but is he \emph{really} unmarried?'' is just confused — there's no question left over. The point here is that with consciousness the leftover question stubbornly does \emph{not} feel confused. We can even picture a being that does everything we do yet feels nothing inside (philosophers call it a ``zombie``), and that picture seems to hang together. If feeling were nothing more than function, none of that should make sense. So this lingering gap is treated as evidence that the \emph{felt} side of mind isn't fully captured by function alone. (It's offered as evidence, not proof — the weak point being that managing to \emph{imagine} something doesn't by itself show it's genuinely possible.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts}{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}
Form: two-pronged. Prong A is a likelihood-defeater (it attacks the probabilistic premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}). Prong B is a reductio (positive abductive support for supervenience).

Prong A — deflate E. \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}} rests on P(E | H1) being low, where E = the post-theoretic intelligibility of ``but is there anything it is like to be \emph{that}?'' and H1 = strong functionalism. But on the phenomenal-concept strategy, creatures who grasp one state under two cognitively isolated guises — a third-person theoretical/role concept and a first-person demonstrative/phenomenal concept — will \emph{always} find the cross-guise identity question residually intelligible, because the two concepts share no inferential corridor down which an analysis could travel.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item If H1 is true, beings with isolated phenomenal concepts would still feel the gap (the felt gap tracks concept architecture, not metaphysical structure).
  \item So P(E | H1) is high, not low. ∴ The inequality P(E | H2) > P(E | H1) that \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}} needs fails to obtain; E does not confirm residue over functionalism. (Analogy: a lingering ``but are these \emph{really} the same number?'' about an unproven-but-necessary identity is evidence about the thinker's epistemic position, not about the numbers.)
\end{enumerate}

Prong B — dancing/fading qualia (positive reason for supervenience, NOT from conceivability).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Replace a conscious brain's neurons one-by-one with functional silicon duplicates preserving every counterfactual input-output relation among components.
  \item If phenomenal character does NOT supervene on functional organisation (H2 / claim-phenomenal- residue), qualia must fade or invert somewhere along the series.
  \item By construction the functional profile — including all introspective judgements, reports, and dispositions to notice change — is held fixed throughout.
  \item So H2 commits us to a subject whose experience drains away or flips while every belief about its own experience stays put: introspection decoupled from experience by metaphysical fiat, with no fact that could ever reconcile them. ∴ H2 entails systematically deceived introspectors as a matter of course; supervenience of the phenomenal on organisational role is the more credible option. This directly answers the residue theorist's request (clause (a)) for an argument against the zombie that is not a redescription of it.
\end{enumerate}

Weakest premise: Prong B's (4) — that the only options under failed supervenience are fade or invert; a residue theorist might seek a story where experience and introspective judgement come apart \emph{coherently}. If such a story can be told without incoherence, the reductio loses its sting. Prong A's exposure: that the phenomenal-concept strategy itself succeeds — if some E is identified that isolated-concept creatures should NOT expect, the deflation fails.

Schema instance: Prong B is the gradual-neural-prosthesis case of \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}} (\texttt{X} = neuron-by-neuron carbon→silicon replacement, \texttt{Y} = \{same qualia, faded/inverted\}). It argues \emph{for the \texttt{f}-constant horn}: premises (4)–(5) deny that any admissible one-neuron step can flip or fade qualia without the absurd consequence in (6), so the verdict is constant across the whole path and supervenience holds. \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\emph{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio begs the question by presupposing functionalism about introspection}} contests precisely whether this has \emph{closed} the discontinuity/disconnection horns or merely assumed them shut.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} because that argues \emph{for} mental status from behaviour; this argues that a specific \emph{anti}-functionalist evidential argument misprices its evidence and that supervenience holds. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}}'s reply (claim-role-grain-is- organisational) because this concerns the phenomenal residue, granting a fixed role, whereas that concerns the individuation of role itself.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the ``feeling really is just function'' side answering the explanatory-gap argument — the charge that a leftover ``but is anything actually felt?'' question shows feeling is something extra (\hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}). It replies in two moves.

\textbf{Move A — explain the gap away.} The other side said: the fact that ``but is anything actually \emph{felt}?'' still sounds like a live question, even after the complete scientific story, is evidence that feeling is something extra. Not so, says this argument. We think about our own experiences with two completely separate mental ``handles'' — a from-the-outside, scientific one and a from-the-inside, ``\emph{this!}'' one — and those two handles just aren't wired to talk to each other. So ``are these two the same thing?'' will \emph{always} feel like an open question, whatever the truth is. The lingering question shows how our concepts are built, not that there's an extra ingredient in the world.

\textbf{Move B — a positive reason feeling can't float free of function.} Imagine swapping someone's neurons one at a time for chips that preserve every functional connection — including every judgment they make about their own experience. If feeling \emph{weren't} fixed by function, then somewhere along the way their inner experience would fade or flip — yet, since we kept all their self-reports identical, they'd keep sincerely insisting ``nothing has changed.'' That hands you a person systematically wrong about their own experience, with no possible fact to set them straight — which seems absurd. Better to conclude feeling tracks function after all. (Weak spot of Move B: it assumes the only options are ``fade'' or ``flip'' — and a critic argues it quietly assumes that \emph{noticing your own experience} is itself just a function, which is the very thing in dispute.)
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{F}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-finite-mind-truncates-beyond-capacity}{A finite mind also fails on inputs beyond its own capacity}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{A finite mind also fails on inputs beyond its own capacity}
gloss: a real, finite mind has bounded memory and attention; past some length or complexity it too cannot take in the whole input — it forgets, loses track, or processes only a fragment. So on the very inputs that exceed a bounded table's domain, the mind does not reliably out-perform it either (Block, \textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}).

scope: a symmetry/levelling claim. It does NOT say minds and tables are alike in general, only that on inputs beyond \emph{both} their capacities the asserted advantage of the mind (claim-mind-handles- unbounded-inputs) is not clearly present, so the asymmetry the combinatorial-impossibility reply needs may not obtain in the domain that matters.

who would deny it: someone holding that a mind's response to over-long inputs (graceful degradation, structural/recursive processing) is still relevantly appropriate where a table's (blind truncation) is not — i.e. that the \emph{manner} of failure differs even if both ``fail.''

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\emph{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}} (the table misses inputs outside its keys): this is the converse levelling claim about the \emph{mind's} finitude, used to neutralise the asymmetry.

\begin{plainterms}
Block's reply on behalf of the cheat-sheet machine: a real mind isn't limitless either. Past some length or complexity, your memory and attention give out — you forget, lose the thread, or take in only a fragment of a huge input. So on exactly the monster inputs a finite stored table can't handle, a real person doesn't clearly do better. That blunts the claim that minds always out-perform tables. (The pushback: a mind fails \emph{gracefully}, still doing something sensible, while a table just blindly chops off whatever it can't store — so even when both ``fail,'' they fail in different ways.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-full-self-access-achievable}{A finite system can be given complete internal access to its own current state}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested}{A finite system can be given complete internal access to its own current state}
gloss: a sufficiently reflective finite system can in principle be engineered to have complete, transparent access to \emph{all} of its own current state from within — so the limits on introspection are contingent and removable, not structural. This is the optimist denied by \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}}.

scope: the substantive reading is \emph{internal} and \emph{complete}. (It is trivially true if ``access'' is allowed to be \emph{external} instrumentation — but an external instrument is not part of the system whose state is represented, so that reading abandons the self-access claim.)

status — why contested: attacked by \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\emph{A finite system cannot completely represent its own current state (a capacity bound)}} on capacity grounds — a proper part of a finite state cannot losslessly encode the whole.

who would deny it: anyone holding the finite self-model bound (the impossibility argument above).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\emph{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}}: that asserts \emph{limited, unreliable} self-access exists; this asserts \emph{complete} self-access is achievable — the strong thesis the impossibility argument targets.

\entryhead{argument-finite-self-model-incomplete}{A finite system cannot completely represent its own current state (a capacity bound)}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A finite system cannot completely represent its own current state (a capacity bound)}
inference form: deductive — a pigeonhole/capacity bound, with a regress corollary.

Setup. Model the instantaneous state as an element of a finite set Σ (equivalently, N activations over a finite alphabet; total capacity C = log₂|Σ| bits). ``Complete simultaneous self-access'' means the state factors as s = (R, U) — a self-model part R and the remainder U (perception, control, the read-out machinery itself) — and the \emph{whole} current state s is recoverable from R alone (lossless ⇒ the map s ↦ R(s) is injective on Σ).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-self-model-is-part-of-state]{\emph{A finite system's self-representation is part of the very state it represents}}) R is part of the state: Σ = ℛ × 𝒰, so |Σ| = |ℛ|·|𝒰|. (No state-external register — this is the internal case. Drop it and you have an \emph{external} observer, who can have full access.)
  \item Lossless recovery of s from R requires s ↦ R injective, hence |ℛ| ≥ |Σ| = |ℛ|·|𝒰|.
  \item Therefore |𝒰| ≤ 1: the remainder is constant — zero spare capacity. The system is \emph{nothing but} its self-model, with nothing left to perceive, act, or run the read-out. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\emph{No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself}}) No finite system can hold a lossless model of its entire current state and have any capacity left to use it; any usable self-model is necessarily lossy/partial. This is \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}} as a \emph{structural necessity}, and it underwrites the architectural reading \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\emph{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}}; it refutes \hyperref[entry:claim-full-self-access-achievable]{\emph{A finite system can be given complete internal access to its own current state}}.
\end{enumerate}

regress form (the dynamical version of the same bound): if \emph{forming} R adds to the state, completeness must cover the post-formation state — including R; representing that addition needs R′, then R″, … In finite state the series must terminate, i.e. some level adds no information: the self-model is incomplete. (The homunculus regress / Royce's map-within-the-map, made quantitative.)

weakest premise: (1) plus the three modal words \emph{simultaneous, internal, lossless} — each names an escape, none of which restores complete internal self-access:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{External} vantage denies (1): an outside instrument is not part of Σ, so it can have full access — the internal/external asymmetry of \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}}.
  \item \emph{Sequential} read-out drops ``simultaneous``: a system can report its state piecemeal or onto an external tape — but each snapshot is stale once the next step runs, so it is never complete-at-an-instant.
  \item \emph{Lossy/compressed} self-models drop ``lossless``: fine and ubiquitous — the bound forbids only completeness, leaving functional introspection (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\emph{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}}) intact.
  \item \emph{Quine / Kleene recursion theorem}: a program can output its own \emph{source} — but source is a compressed static description, not a lossless image of the runtime state (program counter, stack, variables, plus the copy itself), and it is emitted sequentially to an external medium.
\end{itemize}

physical pedigree (and a strengthening): \textsc{Thomas Breuer — The Impossibility of Accurate State Self-Measurements (Philosophy of Science, 1995)} proves a sharper form of this bound for physical measurement — \emph{no observer can distinguish all present states of a system in which it is properly contained}, for classical and quantum theories alike, deterministic or stochastic. It adds two things. (i) It concerns \emph{measurement} (over time), so the indistinguishable state-pairs persist even with a perfect apparatus and unlimited time — partly closing the \emph{sequential} escape above (the limit is structural, not an instantaneous accident). (ii) Its internal/external asymmetry is explicit — an apparatus \emph{outside} the observed system generally \emph{can} distinguish all states — which is exactly \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}}. Two cautions: Breuer stresses the result only \emph{resembles} Gödel's theorem (it shares the use of self-reference and nothing more), so the Gödelian framing of \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\emph{An explanation is intuitively satisfying for a system only if it is traversable by that system's own cognitive operations}} is a separate analogy; and for a deterministic discrete LLM the operative content is his general/classical argument, not the quantum EPR corollary.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}} (which it supports): that \emph{observes} the internal vantage is poorer; this \emph{proves} the poverty is structural and unremovable for any finite system. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\emph{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}} (McGinn, about forming \emph{concepts}): this is a capacity bound on \emph{representing one's own state}, independent of concept-possession.

\entryhead{claim-self-model-is-part-of-state}{A finite system's self-representation is part of the very state it represents}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A finite system's self-representation is part of the very state it represents}
gloss: any representation a finite system forms of its own current state is itself realised \emph{within} that same state — there is no free, state-external register to hold it. The vehicle of the self-model competes for the same bounded resource as everything else the system is doing.

scope: this is the \emph{internal} case, and it is the load-bearing premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\emph{A finite system cannot completely represent its own current state (a capacity bound)}}. Denying it is precisely the move to an \emph{external} observer/instrument, which is not part of the represented state and so faces no capacity bound — the asymmetry behind \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}}.

who would deny it: a substance dualist (the representing mind is not part of the physical state); or anyone modelling self-access as done by external instrumentation rather than from within (which concedes the point for the genuinely internal case).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}}: that is the \emph{conclusion} about access poverty; this is the structural \emph{premise} (no state-external register) that, together with finiteness, forces it.

\entryhead{concept-order-of-representation}{First-order vs higher-order mental states}{Concept}{First-order vs higher-order mental states}
primary definition: mental states can be sorted by their \emph{order} — by what they are \emph{about}. A \textbf{first-order} state is directed at the world or the body: a perception of red, a belief that it is raining, a pain. A \textbf{higher-order} state is directed at one's own \emph{mental} states: a thought or perception \emph{about} a first-order state (``I am seeing red``, an awareness of one's pain). Orders can iterate (a third-order state is about a second-order one), but the philosophically loaded contrast is just first- vs higher-order.

key terms (the senses theories invoke):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{first-order representation} — world-directed content; the state represents how things are outside.
  \item \emph{higher-order thought (HOT)} — an occurrent thought about a current mental state (Rosenthal).
  \item \emph{higher-order perception (HOP) / inner sense} — a quasi-perceptual monitoring of one's own states (Lycan; Armstrong; Locke's ``inner sense``).
  \item \emph{dispositional higher-order} — the higher-order content need only be poised/available, not occurrent (Carruthers).
\end{itemize}

the dispute it feeds — \emph{what makes a state conscious}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Higher-order theories} (\hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\emph{A mental state is conscious only if it is the object of a higher-order representation}}): a first-order state is conscious only if a suitable higher-order state represents it — consciousness is the mind representing itself.
  \item \emph{First-order representationalism} (\hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism (intentionalism)}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\emph{Phenomenal character is identical to representational content}}): phenomenal character is fixed by \emph{first-order} world-directed content; no higher-order state is required. \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global Workspace Theory}} likewise needs only wide availability, not a dedicated higher-order state.
\end{itemize}

how it relates to other distinctions (keep them separate):

\begin{itemize}
  \item It cross-cuts \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}}: a higher-order state is one way content becomes access-conscious, but ``order'' is about \emph{what a state is about}, not about access vs feel.
  \item ``Introspection'' / self-monitoring just \emph{is} having higher-order states about one's own mind — the structure behind \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\emph{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\emph{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}}.
  \item \emph{Order} (world- vs mind-directed) is also distinct from \emph{first-person vs third-person} (whose vantage): a first-order state can be access-conscious, and a higher-order state can itself be unconscious.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
Thoughts come in layers depending on what they're \emph{about}. A \textbf{first-order} state is about the world — seeing the apple is red, believing it'll rain, feeling a pain. A \textbf{higher-order} state is about \emph{your own mind} — noticing that you're seeing red, thinking ``I'm in pain.'' (You can stack further — a thought about that thought — but the key split is just world-directed vs mind-directed.)

Why it matters here: one camp says a state only becomes \emph{conscious} when a higher-order state notices it (consciousness = the mind watching itself); the rival says the conscious feel is already in the plain first-order experience of the world, with no inner watcher needed. Several nodes take sides in that fight — this entry just pins down the vocabulary so you don't need the background.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-first-person-access}{First-person access (privileged access)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{First-person access (privileged access)}
primary definition: the direct, non-inferential acquaintance a subject has with its own conscious states — the way I \emph{know} my own pain without inferring it from behaviour or theory. Contrasted with \emph{third-person access}, the inferential route by which I come to beliefs about any other subject's mental states.

senses:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{epistemic} — a privileged warrant: first-person reports of one's own current experience are non-inferential and (on strong versions) incorrigible.
  \item \emph{metaphysical/acquaintance} — a relation of direct presentation of the state to its subject, not merely a reliable belief-forming channel.
\end{itemize}

the live dispute it feeds: whether first-person access does any work in settling moral status (\hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\emph{First-person acquaintance with a subject's experience is necessary for justified attribution of moral status to it}}, \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Do large language models have moral status?}}) — and, in a neighbouring debate, whether introspective access to one's own qualia is itself functional or a species of acquaintance (cf. \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\emph{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio derives its contradiction only by presupposing functionalism about introspective judgement}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}}). This node names the term those bodies use in passing; it is about access to \emph{one's own} states, the asymmetry of which is exploited in two directions (for an asymmetry in \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\emph{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}}; against it in \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\emph{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}}).

\begin{plainterms}
There's a lopsidedness in how you know about minds. You know your \emph{own} pain directly — you don't watch your own behavior and deduce ``I must be in pain,'' you just feel it from the inside. But anyone else's pain you only ever know \emph{indirectly}, from the outside: their wincing, their words, your best guess about them. That special, direct, inside knowledge of your own experience is what ``first-person access'' names. The debates it feeds ask: does that privileged inside view actually settle anything about who deserves moral consideration — and is your access to your \emph{own} feelings itself something special, or just more of the mind's ordinary machinery?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-access-grounds-no-asymmetry}{First-person access grounds no moral-status asymmetry that singles out machines}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{First-person access grounds no moral-status asymmetry that singles out machines}
gloss: Since the access gap is reciprocal and fully general (\hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\emph{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}}), it cannot do the work of distinguishing the machine's moral standing from a human's. Any moral-status asymmetry must be argued on other grounds; first-person access is not it.

scope: The targeted denial of \hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\emph{First-person access yields a warranted moral-status asymmetry favouring oneself over a machine}}. It does NOT assert machines \emph{have} moral status (that would need a positive argument such as \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}}); it only removes first-person access as a ground for denying it to them.

who would deny it: a proponent of \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\emph{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}} who insists the machine has no first-person standpoint at all, so the symmetry is illusory.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} because that is about whether mental states depend on substrate; this is narrowly about whether \emph{access} can warrant a moral-status asymmetry.

\begin{plainterms}
Not being able to directly feel another's experience is true of \emph{every} pair of minds, not just human-and-machine — so it can't be the thing that makes a human matter morally while a machine doesn't. If there's any such moral difference, it has to be argued some other way; the inside-view gap won't deliver it. (This doesn't claim machines \emph{do} matter — it just removes one reason people give for saying they don't.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-first-person-access}{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}
inference form: abductive / epistemic (about the grounds of justified moral regard, not a deductive proof of the machine's nature).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\emph{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}}) I have direct, non-inferential acquaintance with my own suffering.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\emph{First-person acquaintance with a subject's experience is necessary for justified attribution of moral status to it}}) Such acquaintance is necessary for being justified that there is ``someone there to be wronged.''
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\emph{One has no first-person acquaintance with a machine's putative experience}}) I have no such acquaintance with the machine — only inference. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\emph{First-person access yields a warranted moral-status asymmetry favouring oneself over a machine}}) I am warranted in regarding myself as a bearer of moral status in a way I am not warranted in regarding the machine.
\end{enumerate}

weakest premise: \textbf{premise 2} (\hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\emph{First-person acquaintance with a subject's experience is necessary for justified attribution of moral status to it}}). It is the node where attacks land. Two standing worries, recorded here because they are why this argument is \texttt{status: contested}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Over-generation / proves too much.} If first-person acquaintance were necessary for warranted moral regard, the same would deny me warrant about \emph{other human beings} (I have no acquaintance with their suffering either) — collapsing into solipsism about moral status. The symmetric form of this objection is lodged as \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\emph{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}}.
  \item \emph{Epistemic→metaphysical gap.} The premises concern my \emph{justification}; the conclusion is sometimes overread as a claim about the machine's \emph{actual} status. As written, the conclusion is carefully epistemic.
\end{itemize}

provenance: a problem-of-other-minds appeal applied to AI. In the source dialogue (\textsc{Stanisław Lem — on the mutual unprovability of consciousness}'s pastiche lineage — \emph{О правах непротеиновых существ}, ``On the Rights of Non-Protein Beings``) this is the speaker Cleant's appeal, adopted on the proteocentrist side; imported here as a node, not as a character. Lends support to \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} via its conclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
You know your own suffering from the inside, directly — for you there's no doubt that \emph{someone} is in there hurting. With a machine, all you ever get is an outside guess: it behaves as if it suffers, but you're never personally acquainted with any inner hurt. The argument: since that direct, inside acquaintance is what really backs up the belief ``there's a someone here who can be wronged,'' you're entitled to count \emph{yourself} as mattering morally in a way you're not equally entitled to count the machine. Notice it's a claim about what you're \emph{justified} in believing, not a flat announcement that the machine is empty. Its main weakness — and why it's marked contested — is that the very same reasoning seems to lock you out of other \emph{people} too, since you have no inside access to their pain either, threatening to leave you alone as the only being you can be sure matters.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry}{First-person access yields a warranted moral-status asymmetry favouring oneself over a machine}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{First-person access yields a warranted moral-status asymmetry favouring oneself over a machine}
gloss: Because I have direct acquaintance with my own suffering but only an undischarged inference to the machine's, I am justified in regarding myself as a bearer of moral status in a way I am not justified in regarding the machine — a defensible asymmetry of moral regard grounded in an asymmetry of access.

scope: A claim about \emph{justified regard}, offered as a candidate (negative-for-machines) answer to \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Do large language models have moral status?}}. It does NOT assert that the machine in fact lacks moral status (epistemic, not metaphysical); it asserts that warrant for attributing it is absent in the machine case and present in one's own. It lends support to \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} without entailing it.

who would deny it: those who hold third-person evidence can warrant moral-status attribution (\hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}}); and the symmetry objection (\hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\emph{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}}), which denies any \emph{human/machine} asymmetry is available here at all.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} because that is a metaphysical thesis (only carbon life can bear consciousness); this is the weaker epistemic claim that \emph{warrant} tracks first-person access.

\begin{plainterms}
You feel your own suffering directly; with a machine you can only infer, from the outside, that it \emph{might} be suffering. So this claim says you're entitled to count yourself as morally important in a way you're not yet entitled to count the machine. It's a point about what you're \emph{justified} in believing — not a flat verdict that the machine is empty inside.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant}{First-person acquaintance with a subject's experience is necessary for justified attribution of moral status to it}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{First-person acquaintance with a subject's experience is necessary for justified attribution of moral status to it}
gloss: One is justified in holding that there is ``someone there to be wronged'' only where one has direct first-person acquaintance with the relevant experience. Absent such acquaintance, the attribution of moral status is at best an undischarged inference.

scope: A claim about the \emph{warrant} for moral-status attributions, not about what moral status metaphysically consists in. It is the load-bearing and weakest premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\emph{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}}: it is what turns an asymmetry of \emph{access} into an asymmetry of \emph{justified moral regard}.

who would deny it: anyone who holds that behavioural, functional, or theoretical evidence can justify moral-status attributions without first-person acquaintance — e.g. the proponents of \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}}; and anyone pressing the problem of other minds against it (it appears to entail that I am equally unwarranted about other human beings, see \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\emph{The access asymmetry is unavailable because first-person access is reciprocally non-transferable}}).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\emph{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}} because that merely asserts self-acquaintance exists; this asserts the far stronger, contestable thesis that such acquaintance is \emph{necessary} for warranted moral-status attribution.

\begin{plainterms}
This claim says you're only really entitled to believe ``there's someone in there who can be wronged'' when you have direct, first-hand acquaintance with their experience. Short of that inside contact, calling something a being-that-matters is just an unproven guess. It's a strong and much-disputed claim — taken strictly, it seems to leave you unable to vouch even for \emph{other people}, since you have no inside line to their feelings either.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-access-gap-symmetric}{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}
gloss: First-person acquaintance is non-transferable in \emph{both} directions. A machine has access to its own substrate and lacks it concerning me, by exactly the measure I have access to mine and lack it concerning the machine. The gap is between first and third person generally — it holds between any two subjects whatever — so it cannot be the gap between human and machine specifically.

scope: A structural claim about the \emph{reciprocity} of access. It does NOT assert that the machine in fact has experience; it asserts a conditional symmetry — \emph{to whatever extent} a subject has a first-person standpoint, others lack access to it equally. Its dialectical job is to deny that an access asymmetry capable of singling out machines exists.

who would deny it: anyone holding that there is no ``machine first-person standpoint'' at all, so that nothing is reciprocally inaccessible (then the symmetry's second arm is empty); this is the chief pressure point against it.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\emph{One has no first-person acquaintance with a machine's putative experience}} because that records only the human-side deficit; this adds the reciprocal, fully-general structure that neutralises the \emph{asymmetry}.

provenance: the mutual unprovability of consciousness between minds is due to Stanisław Lem — see \textsc{Stanisław Lem — on the mutual unprovability of consciousness}.

\begin{plainterms}
You can never directly feel anyone else's experience — and that cuts both ways. If a machine has any inner life at all, it's just as shut out of yours as you are of its. The wall isn't ``humans on one side, machines on the other``; it's ``me versus everyone else,'' and it stands equally between any two minds. So that wall can't be used to single machines out as the doubtful case.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-folk-psychology-is-false-theory}{Folk-psychological mental states are posits of a false theory}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Churchland}{Folk-psychological mental states are posits of a false theory}
gloss: the everyday categories of mind — beliefs, desires, and qualia — are theoretical posits of ``folk psychology,'' an empirical theory that a mature neuroscience will show to be false and will \emph{replace}, not vindicate. So strictly there are no beliefs or qualia, just neural processes (Churchland 1981). A \emph{type-A} materialism (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}): it denies there is any phenomenal explanandum to begin with.

scope: an eliminativist claim about the \emph{non-existence} of the folk posits, not merely their reducibility. Broader than illusionism — it eliminates the propositional attitudes (belief, desire) too, not only phenomenal feel.

who would deny it: realists about folk psychology (most functionalists, intentional-stance theorists); and phenomenal realists asserting \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}} (illusionism): that targets \emph{phenomenal} properties specifically and grants the introspective appearance; eliminativism is wider and more radical.

\entryhead{claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness}{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}
gloss: when a question concerns a difference unobservable \emph{in principle} — not hard to measure, but such that no possible observation comes out differently either way (``is the light \emph{really} on inside?``) — any dispute over it reduces to my word against yours. The best that can be asked of such a concept is whether it is \textbf{coherent} and whether it is \textbf{useful} — whether it earns its keep by doing work somewhere: predicting, organising, distinguishing. Quantum amplitude, incomputable numbers: coherent, useful. Objects that change colour only when nobody looks at them: coherent, not useful.

the examples, unpacked. A quantum amplitude is never observed as such — only its squared modulus shows up, in frequencies of outcomes — yet the concept pays its way many times over: it predicts, it unifies, and refusing it costs you physics. An incomputable number can never be written out or generated by any effective procedure, yet such numbers are indispensable to the theory of computation. So unobservability in principle is, by itself, no disqualification — this standard is deliberately more hospitable than crude verificationism. What disqualifies is \emph{idleness}: a posit that makes no observable difference \textbf{and} does no organising work — the colour that changes only while unwatched — retains coherence alone, and coherence alone is cheap.

pedigree and what is original here: kin to the Peirce–James pragmatic maxim (a difference must make a difference) and to Carnap's treatment of external questions (to be judged by fruitfulness, not by correspondence). Kin also to Smart's ``nomological danglers'' (Smart 1959, ``Sensations and Brain Processes``): psychic posits that dangle outside the nomological net, lawfully connected to nothing, are to be shaved off by Occam — which is this standard's usefulness axis turned into an argument. The standing complaint against \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} is the same verdict in the particular: a feel that makes no causal difference (\hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\emph{Phenomenal mental states are caused by physical processes but have no physical effects}}) is the unwatched colour-change wearing a serious expression. Distinct from these ancestors in what it \emph{keeps}: it does not rule in-principle unobservables meaningless (the amplitude and the incomputable number are honest citizens), and it does not relativize truth to linguistic frameworks. It grades such posits on exactly two axes — coherence and earned usefulness — and is silent about everything observable.

scope: applies only where no possible evidence discriminates; it says nothing about matters that are merely \emph{practically} unobservable. And ``coherent, not useful'' is a verdict of idleness, not of falsehood — the claim licenses retiring an idle posit from argument, not declaring it false.

who would deny it: the metaphysical realist about the relevant domain — e.g. a phenomenal realist holding that ``is there something it is like to be that system?'' has a determinate answer whose importance is independent of any work the concept does (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} is the natural home of such a denial); also anyone who thinks coherence plus apriori reasoning alone can settle substantive questions about in-principle unobservables.

\begin{plainterms}
Some questions are set up so that no test could ever tell the answers apart — ``does this thing \emph{really} have an inner light on, or does it just act exactly as if it did?'' For questions like that, it's permanently your word against mine. So this claim proposes the only scorecard left: is the idea \emph{coherent} (does it make sense), and is it \emph{useful} (does it help you predict, organise, or distinguish anything)? Physics keeps ``quantum amplitude'' — you can never see one directly, but it runs the most accurate predictions ever made. Mathematics keeps numbers no computer could ever print out, because theory needs them. But ``objects change colour only when nobody looks'' — perfectly consistent, and perfectly worthless: it predicts nothing, helps nowhere. Keep the first kind, retire the second.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict}{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}
gloss: once the blockhead is granted to be physically impossible (claim-lookup-table-physically- unrealisable) and so available only as an object of imagination, the verdict ``there is no mind there'' is an intuition about a humanly-unvisualisable abstract object of cosmic size. Conceivability is symmetric and cheap: nothing blocks conceiving the same object \emph{as} a subject of experience. So the no-mind verdict is not forced — the imaginative exercise underdetermines it.

scope: a claim about the \emph{evidential standing} of the intuition, not a metaphysical claim. It does NOT assert the imaginary table IS conscious; only that the objection cannot help itself to ``obviously not'' once the case is admitted to be merely imaginary. (Cf. Dennett's worry that ``intuition pumps'' trade on under-described impossible scenarios.)

who would deny it: Block and anyone treating the no-mind verdict as a reliable considered judgment rather than a mere imaginative reaction — for whom the toaster-grade internals settle the matter regardless of realizability.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}}: that asserts the verdict (no mental states); this undercuts the \emph{evidential warrant} for that very verdict in the physically-impossible case. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}: that is the physical horn; this is the conceptual horn of \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\emph{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Once we admit the giant lookup-table ``mind'' could never actually be built and lives only in imagination, the confident feeling that ``obviously nothing's conscious in there'' loses its grip. Imagining is cheap and cuts both ways — you can just as easily picture the thing \emph{having} an inner life. So the case doesn't force the ``no mind'' verdict; you're free to imagine it either way. (This isn't claiming the imaginary table \emph{is} conscious — only that you can't lean on ``obviously not.``). A table mapping every possible input to a response would be exponentially large — so, you can imagine, the matter needed to physically instantiate it would be so vast that the laws of physics would do what they do to that much matter: collapse it into galaxies, ignite stars, form planets, and, somewhere in one corner, give rise to conscious life. The ``obviously mindless'' lookup table would have minds growing inside its own substrate.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-finite-table-misses-inputs}{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}
gloss: A lookup table stores a finite set of input→output pairs. The space of possible finite inputs (e.g. token strings, longer or more numerous than any the table anticipates) is unbounded. So for any finite table there exist finite inputs not among its keys; on such an input the table has no appropriate stored response, and can at most emit an output by \emph{discarding the part of the input that exceeds its keys} — i.e. by treating a longer/novel input as one of the finitely many it already knows.

scope: A near-combinatorial point about \emph{finite} stored maps versus an unbounded input space. It does NOT assert that minds are infinite, nor that no large table is practically adequate — only that, for any fixed finite table, some inputs fall outside it. It is silent on whether those uncovered inputs are ones a real, finite-lifetime mind would ever actually receive (that is the contested bridge — see \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}}).

who would deny it: essentially no one as stated; the dispute is not here but at the asymmetry premise (whether a mind does better on the uncovered inputs).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}} (which asserts a finite table CAN match a mind's full behavioural profile): this claim is the combinatorial fact that motivates denying that one — it identifies the inputs on which a finite table must fall short.

\begin{plainterms}
A stored answer-table only holds finitely many input→answer pairs, but the inputs you could throw at it (ever-longer strings of words, say) never run out. So however big the table, there are always inputs it has no entry for — and all it can do then is ignore the unfamiliar part and treat a brand-new input as one it already knows. (This much is barely deniable; the real fight is over whether those uncovered inputs are ones a real, finite-lifetime mind would ever actually meet.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-posing-the-gap-requires-access}{Formulating the hard problem from the first-person side requires access to a representation of one's own phenomenal-seeming states}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Formulating the hard problem from the first-person side requires access to a representation of one's own phenomenal-seeming states}
gloss: to \emph{state} the hard problem from the inside — to point at one's phenomenal-seeming states on one side, at the physical/functional story on the other, and judge them irreconcilable — is a cognitive act, and like any such act it can only operate on contents the system has access to. So posing the gap requires \emph{access consciousness} of (at least a representation of) the phenomenal relatum.

scope: a claim about the \emph{preconditions for formulating} the hard problem, NOT the much stronger ``phenomenal consciousness requires access consciousness.'' On Block's view the two come apart (\hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}}): phenomenal states may \emph{overflow} access, so a system could \emph{have} phenomenal character it cannot fully thematise. The claim is only that the act of \emph{raising} the gap is access-dependent — you cannot point at, compare, or puzzle over what you have no cognitive access to.

who would deny it: someone who holds the explanatory gap is registered non-cognitively (a felt shortfall that requires no representation of the phenomenal relatum); or who collapses the access/phenomenal distinction so that ``pointing at the phenomenal'' is not a further access-dependent step.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\emph{The phenomenal-concept strategy over-generates — it debunks the self-knowledge it presupposes}} because those concern whether a \emph{phenomenal concept} can be both informative enough to ground self-knowledge and isolated enough to deflate the gap; this is the prior, weaker point that \emph{some} access to the phenomenal relatum is needed merely to pose the gap at all. (Flagged for \texttt{/reconcile}: the two are kin and a human should check they are not collapsible.)

\entryhead{claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only}{A functional description captures only structural-dynamical facts, not intrinsic nature}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{A functional description captures only structural-dynamical facts, not intrinsic nature}
gloss: a functional/causal specification captures only the \emph{structure} of a system (its relational organisation) and its \emph{dynamics} (how states evolve and dispose); it is silent on the intrinsic, categorical nature of whatever realises that structure. (Russell, \emph{The Analysis of Matter}, 1927; Chalmers, ``Consciousness and Its Place in Nature``, 2003.)

scope: a claim about the expressive limits of structural-dynamical description. Neutral, in itself, on whether anything important lies outside structure-and-dynamics.

who would deny it: a structuralist (ontic structural realism) who holds there is no further categorical nature, or that talk of ``intrinsic nature'' is empty.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}: that says role under-specifies \emph{qualia}; this is the more general thesis that structural-dynamical description omits \emph{intrinsic nature}, of which the phenomenal is the salient candidate.

\begin{plainterms}
Describe anything purely by its function and you capture its \emph{structure} (how the parts relate) and its \emph{dynamics} (how its states change over time) — but nothing about what the parts \emph{are} in themselves, their inner nature. It's the wiring-diagram point again: the diagram shows the connections, never what the components are actually made of. On its own this stays neutral about whether anything important hides in that left-out inner nature — it just marks that function leaves it out.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-functionalism}{Functionalism (about mental states)}{Concept}{Functionalism (about mental states)}
The view that mental states are individuated by their causal/functional role — relations to inputs, outputs, and other states — not by their physical realiser.

senses:

\begin{itemize}
  \item machine-state functionalism (Putnam): roles defined by a Turing-machine table
  \item role functionalism: states = occupants of a causal role
  \item analytic/commonsense functionalism: roles fixed by folk-psychological platitudes
\end{itemize}

Contested point relevant here: whether functional roles can be multiply realised in non-biological substrates (see \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}).

\begin{plainterms}
What makes a thought a thought? Functionalism's answer: what it \emph{does}, not what it's made of. A mental state — say, pain — is defined by its job: it's caused by certain things (injury), it causes certain things (wincing, the wish to make it stop), and it links up to your other states. Anything that fills that job counts as pain, whether it runs on brain cells, silicon chips, or something else. The usual analogy: being a \emph{clock} isn't about being made of brass or quartz — it's about keeping time, and anything that keeps time is a clock. Functionalism says minds are like that. (The named variants above are just different views about how strictly to spell out the ``job.``)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant}{Functionalism owes an observer-independent invariant that fixes a system's decomposition}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Functionalism owes an observer-independent invariant that fixes a system's decomposition}
gloss: if consciousness is constituted by functional organisation, then — because consciousness is determinate (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\emph{There is a determinate fact about one's own current phenomenal state}}) while functional decomposition is not (\hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\emph{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}}) — functionalism must specify an observer-independent invariant that singles out the \emph{one} correct decomposition of a system. Absent such an invariant, determinate consciousness cannot be grounded in description-relative function.

scope: a conditional \emph{burden}, not an outright refutation of functionalism. It leaves open that the invariant exists; it asserts only that functionalism about consciousness is incomplete until it names one. The leading candidate is the system's objective counterfactual causal structure.

who would deny it: a functionalist who holds the invariant is already in hand — the organisational grain, i.e. the system's objective counterfactual causal structure (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}) — so that no new commitment is owed.

Distinct from the demand of \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}} (a grain coarse enough yet fine enough): that asks \emph{where to set the resolution} of a role; this asks \emph{what fixes the carving at all}.

\begin{plainterms}
Here's a bill handed to the ``mind = function'' view. Your conscious experience right now is perfectly definite — there's a fact about what you're feeling. But (the worry goes) there's no single definite way to carve a system into functional parts. If your consciousness were \emph{made of} one such carving, it would be as wobbly as the carving — yet it isn't. So the view owes us an account of what fixes the \emph{one} real organization, independent of how an observer slices things. It's a demand, not a knockout: maybe such a fixer exists (the usual candidate is real cause-and-effect structure) — but until it's named, the view is unfinished.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{G}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs}{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}
gloss: The responsive capacity of a genuine mind is \emph{productive / open-ended} — it can take an input it has never met (longer, more complex, recombined) and respond appropriately by processing its structure, rather than by matching it to a pre-stored whole.
