Confronted with the very inputs a finite table must miss (\hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\emph{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}}), a mind does not simply discard the excess.

scope: An asymmetry claim — that a mind out-responds a finite table on inputs outside the table's domain. It does NOT claim minds are infinite or unbounded in capacity; only that for any \emph{fixed} finite table the mind's competence outruns it on some inputs.

who would deny it: a defender of Ned Block's ``Blockhead'' (\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}). Block stipulates a table covering every input up to a length exceeding any the subject would receive in a normal lifetime; he then argues (i) within that bounded domain the table matches the mind, and (ii) a real mind is \emph{also} finite — past its own memory/attention bound it too ``ignores part of the input'' — so the asymmetry vanishes over the only domain that matters. This is the weakest link of the combinatorial-impossibility argument and why its conclusion is held contested.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\emph{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}} (about whether matching behaviour suffices for mind): this is the prior, factual claim that a finite table cannot even \emph{match} an open-ended mind's behaviour. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}} (which individuates by internal organisation): this individuates by I/O competence over an unbounded input space, not by internals.

\begin{plainterms}
A real mind keeps up with an endless variety of new inputs — sentences and situations it has never met before — by actually working through their structure, not by recognizing them off a stored list. Hand it something longer or stranger than anything it's seen, and it can still answer sensibly, where a fixed table just draws a blank. (It doesn't claim minds are limitless — only that for any finite stored table, a mind can handle some inputs the table can't.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-global-workspace}{Global Workspace Theory}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Baars}{Global Workspace Theory}
Content is conscious when it is broadcast in a ``global workspace'' — made widely available to the brain's specialist systems for memory, report, and flexible control (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\emph{A state is conscious only if its content is globally broadcast to the brain's specialist systems}}; Baars 1988; Dehaene's neuronal global workspace gives the neural mechanism, e.g. the late ``ignition'' of fronto-parietal networks). A functional/empirical theory with a clear research programme.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: it is primarily a theory of the \emph{access} side of \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — what makes content reportable and usable. Block's objection is that phenomenal consciousness may \emph{overflow} access, so broadcast is at best the wrong target for the hard problem; against \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}} it needs no dedicated meta-representation, only wide availability.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture the mind as a theatre: countless specialist processes work in the dark, and a piece of information becomes \emph{conscious} when it's lit up on the central stage and broadcast to the whole audience — so you can talk about it, remember it, and act on it. It's the leading scientific theory of consciousness and comes with brain evidence. The philosophical catch: this explains how something gets into the spotlight and gets \emph{used}, but maybe not why being in the spotlight \emph{feels} like anything.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-parfit-rewiring}{Gradual psychological rewiring (Parfit's spectrum) forces identity to admit of degree}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Parfit}{Gradual psychological rewiring (Parfit's spectrum) forces identity to admit of degree}
Form: application of \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}} to personal identity, selecting a horn. \texttt{X} = a connected spectrum of psychological configurations from one person to another (e.g. gradually rewiring a psychology into Greta Garbo's), joined by one-connection edits; \texttt{Y} = \{me, not-me\}.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item At the start of the series the subject is determinately themselves; at the far end they are determinately someone else. (the endpoints differ — \texttt{f} is not constant)
  \item No single one-connection edit is the step at which they stop being themselves. (\hyperref[entry:claim-psychological-rewiring-tolerance]{\emph{Altering a single psychological connection never changes who someone is}} — tolerance; the verdict makes no jump across an admissible step)
  \item The series is a connected path of such edits (\texttt{X} connected). ∴ A discrete, bivalent identity-verdict cannot be maintained: by the schema, the verdict must instead come in \emph{degrees} — \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\emph{Personal identity is not all-or-nothing but admits of degree}}.
\end{enumerate}

Which horn it takes: the \emph{non-discrete-\texttt{Y} (degree)} horn — explicitly, in contrast with the horn-neutral \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\emph{The sorites paradox (the heap)}}. Parfit's diagnosis is not that there is a hidden sharp boundary (discontinuity) nor a mere truth-value gap (indeterminacy) but that identity genuinely holds to a degree.

Weakest premise: (2) read as forcing \emph{degree} specifically. The discontinuity-horn theorist denies (2) (some edit is the breaking point); the indeterminacy-horn theorist accepts (2) but reads the middle as gappy rather than graded. The argument's selection of the degree horn over those rivals is where it is pressed.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine slowly rewiring your mind, one tiny change at a time, until your psychology matches someone else's — say, a famous stranger's. At the start it's clearly you; at the far end it's clearly not. But no single tiny tweak is \emph{the} moment you stopped being yourself. Sound familiar? It's the heap paradox again, with ``me / not-me'' standing in for ``heap / not-heap.'' Parfit's move is to take one of the exits: personal identity isn't a hard yes-or-no, it's a matter of \emph{degree} — you can be partly the same person, more or less continuous with who you were, rather than flatly the same or flatly a different person. (Other thinkers grab different exits — a hidden exact cutoff, or a stretch where there's just no fact of the matter — which is where this argument gets contested.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-grain}{Grain (of role individuation)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Grain (of role individuation)}
primary definition: the \emph{level of resolution} at which a functional/causal role is specified — how much detail two systems must share to count as realising the \emph{same} role, and hence (on functionalism) the same mental state. A metaphor from resolution: coarse grain draws few distinctions, fine grain draws many. Because functionalism (\hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}}) individuates mental states by role but leaves the grain of ``role'' unspecified, the grain is the free parameter on which most substrate-independence verdicts actually turn: set it and you decide who counts as having a mind.

senses (settings along one dial, coarse → fine):

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{coarse / input–output / behavioural} — the role is the input-output (Turing) profile only. Most permissive; satisfied by a mere behavioural duplicate, hence ``too liberal'' (admits \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}}). See \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}} horn 1.
  \item \emph{organisational / functional-causal} — the role is the system's internal state-to-state transition structure (which states cause which, under which counterfactuals), abstracted from the physical medium. The proposed principled middle (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}): coarse enough for silicon, fine enough to exclude the lookup table.
  \item \emph{fine / microphysical / biological} — the role includes spike timing, neurochemistry, the physical realiser. Most restrictive; ``too chauvinistic,'' collapsing toward \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}. See \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}} horn 2.
\end{itemize}

the live dispute it feeds — \emph{where to set the dial}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}}: no setting works — coarse is too liberal, fine too chauvinistic.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}} + \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}}: the organisational middle is principled and independently motivated (it is the grain at which cognitive science quantifies).
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\emph{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}} + \hyperref[entry:claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational-role grain either readmits the blockhead or smuggles sub-organisational features back in}}: even the middle fails — a dense \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}} matches the organisation yet (intuitively) lacks a mind, so the grain either readmits the absurd or smuggles sub-organisational detail back in.
\end{itemize}

a distinction the debate sometimes blurs: grain is a knob about \emph{functional description}. It bears on (1) the individuation of \emph{cognitive/intentional} states (belief, inference) — where the organisational setting is strong and even the residue theorist concedes it — and (2) whether \emph{any} setting suffices for \emph{phenomenal} states (what-it-is-like). The phenomenal-residue worry (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\emph{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}) is precisely the claim that no setting of this knob captures qualia at all — a different complaint from ``the dial is set wrong.''

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose you agree a mind is defined by what it \emph{does} rather than what it's made of. There's still a catch: \emph{how exactly} do you describe what it does? Very roughly (``takes in questions, gives sensible answers``)? Or in fine detail (``these exact signals trigger those, with this precise timing``)? That choice of detail-level is the ``grain'' — like the resolution on a screen, coarse or fine. It matters enormously, because it quietly decides who counts as having a mind. Describe a mind too coarsely and almost anything qualifies — even a giant cheat-sheet that just looks up canned answers. Describe it too finely and only a biological brain can match it, so no computer ever could. A lot of the fight over whether machines can think comes down to one question: where do you set this dial — and can you justify \emph{that} setting without just tuning it to get the answer you already wanted?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-role-grain-dilemma}{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}
Form: dilemma (constructive). It attacks \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} by showing its key term ``role'' admits no grain of individuation that secures the conclusion without absurdity, absent a principled line its advocate has not drawn.

The functional role by which a mental state is individuated must be specified at some grain. Either horn:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Coarse-grained} (input–output, the classic Turing profile). Then functionalism is \emph{too liberal}: Block's homunculi-headed China, or a vast lookup table, realises the role and must — on \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} — have the mind. This is the ``blockhead'' worry that \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} itself flags as the soft underbelly of its premise (2). Going coarse to admit the silicon admits the absurd alongside it.
  \item \textbf{Fine-grained} (full causal microstructure: spike timing, neuromodulator diffusion, and the rest). Then functionalism risks being \emph{too chauvinistic}: the role becomes so biologically specific that nothing but a brain, or a near-molecular emulation of one, realises it — and substrate-independence quietly collapses toward the neighbourhood of \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, the carbon doing real individuative work after all.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The advocate of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} owes a grain \emph{coarse enough to be multiply realisable in silicon yet fine enough to exclude the blockhead} — and a principled reason for drawing the line there rather than for the convenience of the conclusion. The claim as written helps itself to the existence of such a line.

Weakest premise: the dilemma's exhaustiveness — that no \emph{intermediate} grain escapes both horns. A reply that specifies a principled middle grain (and motivates it independently of the desired verdict on silicon) would dissolve the dilemma; that is exactly the (b)-style move the dilemma's exhaustiveness leaves open.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}} because that grants a fixed role and presses what role \emph{leaves out} (phenomenal character); this argument attacks the \emph{individuation} of role itself, and applies even to intentional states. responds\_to \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} because horn 1 sharpens the blockhead objection that argument names against its own premise (2).

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose you say a mind is defined by what it does, so a machine could have one too. Fair enough — but \emph{how detailed} is your description of ``what it does``? You're caught between two bad answers. Go coarse (``right answers in, right answers out``) and you let in obvious frauds: a giant lookup table that just spits back pre-stored replies would count as having a mind. Go fine (exact brain chemistry, the precise timing of neurons) and you've quietly made a \emph{brain} the only thing that qualifies — so machines are excluded after all, and you've smuggled biology back in. To make machine minds work you need a description loose enough to fit silicon yet strict enough to shut out the cheat-sheet — \emph{and} an honest reason for drawing the line right there, not a reason picked just because it delivers the verdict you wanted. The complaint: nobody has actually drawn that principled line.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{H}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-halting-oracle}{The halting oracle (and uncomputability)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Turing}{The halting oracle (and uncomputability)}
primary definition: the \textbf{halting problem} asks, of an arbitrary program and input, whether the program eventually halts. Turing proved it \textbf{undecidable}: no Turing machine answers it for all cases (\textsc{Turing — On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem (1936)}, by a diagonal argument). A \textbf{halting oracle} is a hypothetical black box that \emph{does} return the answer — posited as an \emph{oracle} to define \textbf{relative computability} (what becomes computable \emph{given} the oracle). This is not a fringe fiction but the object of a whole rigorous theory: oracle machines, \textbf{Turing degrees}, the arithmetical hierarchy.

the key feature that makes it useful here: the halting oracle is \textbf{precisely specified and consistently theorized} — \emph{positively} conceivable, not merely ``no contradiction noticed'' — yet provably \emph{not Turing-computable}. Whether it is \emph{physically} realizable turns on the \textbf{physical Church–Turing thesis} (\hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\emph{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}}); standard physics says no, while \textbf{hypercomputation} proposals (Malament–Hogarth spacetimes; infinite-time Turing machines) dispute it.

the disputes it feeds:

\begin{itemize}
  \item a clean, non-definitional case of \emph{conceivable/coherent but physically unrealizable} (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\emph{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\emph{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}}) — sharper than the water=H₂O case because there is no ``you mislabelled it'' escape.
  \item the formal backbone of \emph{internal-vs-external derivability} (\hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\emph{An explanation is intuitively satisfying for a system only if it is traversable by that system's own cognitive operations}}).
  \item a building block for \textbf{Gödelian anti-mechanism} about minds — Penrose holds the brain exploits \emph{non-computable} physics (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\emph{Consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}}, \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness (Orch-OR)}}), i.e. denies the physical Church–Turing thesis for brains.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
There's a famous question — ``will this program eventually stop, or run forever?'' — and Turing proved \emph{no} computer program can answer it reliably for every case. A ``halting oracle'' is an imaginary gadget that just \emph{knows} the answer. The striking thing: mathematicians have built an entire, perfectly consistent theory around such gadgets (what you \emph{could} compute if you had one) — so it's a fully coherent, precisely-defined idea — and yet it's provably impossible for any ordinary computer to be one. That makes it a clean example of something you can rigorously \emph{describe and reason about} but (on standard physics) can never actually \emph{build}.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-halting-oracle-unrealizable}{The halting oracle is coherent yet physically unrealizable}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The halting oracle is coherent yet physically unrealizable}
inference form: deductive.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\emph{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}}) No Turing machine decides the halting problem.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\emph{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}}) Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable. ∴ No physical device computes the halting function. Yet the halting oracle is a \emph{consistent, richly-theorized} abstract object (relative computability; \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{The halting oracle (and uncomputability)}}) — so it is (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\emph{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}}) mathematically coherent but physically unrealizable.
\end{enumerate}

weakest premise: \textbf{(2)}, the physical Church–Turing thesis — a contingent claim about physics, denied by hypercomputation proposals (Malament–Hogarth spacetimes, infinite-time Turing machines) and, for brains specifically, by Penrose's non-computable-physics view (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\emph{Consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}}). If (2) fails, the oracle might be physically realizable after all. Premise (1) is a theorem and not in play.

note on modal reach: the conclusion is \emph{physical} unrealizability, not metaphysical impossibility — the oracle remains a coherent abstract object / metaphysically possible. That is exactly why the cluster is aimed at the conceivability → \emph{physical} realizability bridge (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\emph{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}}), not the metaphysical one.

\begin{plainterms}
Two steps. First, a theorem: no ordinary computer can solve the halting problem. Second, a bet about physics: nature has no computer stronger than an ordinary one. Put them together and \emph{nothing physical} can solve halting — even though the ``halting oracle'' that would is a perfectly coherent, well-studied mathematical idea. So it's coherent but unbuildable. The soft spot is the second step: if some exotic physics could out-compute a normal computer (which is what Penrose's theory of mind actually needs), the oracle might be buildable after all.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable}{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}
gloss: the halting oracle (\hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{The halting oracle (and uncomputability)}}) is a consistent, precisely-specified object of a developed mathematical theory (relative computability), yet — given \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\emph{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}} plus \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\emph{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}} — no physical device can be one. Coherent and theorizable; unbuildable.

scope: a claim pairing \emph{mathematical coherence} with \emph{physical unrealizability}. Two caveats kept explicit: (i) the unrealizability inherits the \emph{contingency} of the physical Church–Turing thesis (if hypercomputation is physically possible, the oracle may be realizable after all); (ii) the oracle is \emph{metaphysically} possible (a consistent abstract structure / a possible world with one) — it is \emph{physical} realizability that fails, not coherence or metaphysical possibility.

who would deny it: a hypercomputation theorist who rejects the physical Church–Turing thesis (so the oracle \emph{is} physically realizable); or a strict finitist/formalist who denies the oracle is a legitimately coherent object at all.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}} (another coherent-but-physically-impossible object — a behaviour-matching table too big for the universe): that fails on \emph{resource bounds}; this fails on \emph{uncomputability}. Two different routes to ``specifiable but unbuildable.''

\begin{plainterms}
You can write down exactly what a halting oracle would be, prove theorems about what it could do, and teach a whole course on it — it's a completely coherent idea. And yet, on standard physics, you could never actually construct one. So it sits in a striking spot: fully thinkable and mathematically real, but physically out of reach. (Two honest footnotes: this assumes nature has no exotic ``super-computers'' hiding in it; and the oracle is still \emph{possible in the abstract} — it's only the \emph{building} of one that's blocked.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-conceivable-not-physically-realizable}{The halting oracle shows conceivability does not entail physical realizability}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The halting oracle shows conceivability does not entail physical realizability}
inference form: counterexample — a single rigorous instance refutes a universal sufficiency claim.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\emph{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}}) The halting oracle is mathematically coherent (even positively, fruitfully theorized) yet physically unrealizable. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\emph{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}}) conceivability — including rigorous mathematical coherence — does not entail physical realizability: here is one coherent thing that cannot be built.
\end{enumerate}

why it beats the water case (for \emph{this} bridge): the type-B / two-dimensional reply to the water example — ``you only conceived a \emph{misdescribed} possibility (watery stuff), the necessity hides in the definition'' — has no purchase here. The oracle is specified exactly and studied as such; there is no other thing one is ``really'' conceiving, and its unbuildability is a \emph{proof} (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\emph{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}}), not an empirical identity.

scope / what it does NOT show: it is aimed at the conceivability → \emph{physical} realizability bridge, and is deliberately \textbf{not} an attack on the metaphysical conceivability → possibility bridge (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\emph{Conceivability is not a reliable guide to possibility (the a-posteriori-necessity objection)}}). The oracle is metaphysically possible; and the \emph{Turing-machine} halting-decider (which would be metaphysically impossible) is not \emph{ideally} conceivable, so Chalmers' bridge keeps its prima-facie/ideal refinement. The point is narrower and cleaner: coherent ≠ buildable.

weakest premise: it inherits the contingency of \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\emph{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}} (via its premise) — if hypercomputation is physically possible the witnessing instance dissolves; but the \emph{generalisation} still stands on other instances (e.g. \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}), so the conclusion is robust even if the oracle case is contested.

\begin{plainterms}
From one airtight example you get a general lesson. The halting oracle is completely coherent as an idea yet impossible to build — so ``I can coherently conceive it'' plainly does not guarantee ``it can be physically made.'' And it's a cleaner example than ``water that isn't H₂O,'' because nobody can say you secretly meant something else: the oracle is defined exactly, and its unbuildability is a \emph{proof}. Just note the modest target — this is about what can be \emph{built}, not a claim that the oracle is impossible in every sense.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-hard-problem}{The hard problem of consciousness}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{The hard problem of consciousness}
primary definition: David Chalmers' distinction (``Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness``, 1995) between the \emph{easy} problems and the \emph{hard} problem. The \textbf{easy problems} are to explain the brain's cognitive and behavioural \emph{functions} — discrimination, integration, reportability, the control of behaviour — all in principle tractable by the ordinary methods of cognitive science. The \textbf{hard problem} is to explain why and how the performance of any of these functions is accompanied by \emph{subjective experience} — why there is something it is like to be the system at all, rather than the processing going on ``in the dark.'' A complete functional account seems to leave this question untouched.

relation to neighbours: closely allied to the \emph{explanatory gap} (Levine — the epistemic gap between a complete physical/functional account and phenomenal truths; see \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}) and to the \emph{phenomenal residue} (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}). The hard problem is the agenda-setting \emph{frame}; the gap and the residue are specific ways of pressing it. It contrasts with \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}}, which is one proposed way to dissolve it.

the dispute it feeds: \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}}. Responses span \emph{dissolving} it (strong functionalism, illusionism — there is no further fact to explain), accepting an \emph{ontological} divide (dualism, panpsychism), and declaring it real but \emph{humanly unsolvable} (\hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism (transcendental naturalism)}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Science is good at explaining what the brain \emph{does} — how it tells red from green, stores a memory, or makes you say ``ouch.'' Call those the easy problems (easy in principle, not in practice). The hard problem is different: even after you've explained everything the brain does, there's a leftover question — why is all that machinery accompanied by an inner feel? Why does seeing red \emph{feel like} anything instead of happening with nobody home? That ``why is there an experience at all'' question is what ``the hard problem'' names.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-higher-order-theories}{Higher-order theories of consciousness}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Rosenthal}{Higher-order theories of consciousness}
A mental state is conscious in virtue of being the object of a suitable \emph{higher-order} representation (\hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs higher-order mental states}}) — a higher-order thought (Rosenthal), higher-order perception (Lycan), or dispositional higher-order content (Carruthers) (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\emph{A mental state is conscious only if it is the object of a higher-order representation}}). Being conscious is thus a relational, representational fact about a state, not an intrinsic glow. It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by reducing \emph{state-consciousness} to higher-order representation.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: against first-order \hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism (intentionalism)}} it insists a state must be \emph{represented} (not merely represent the world) to be conscious; against \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global Workspace Theory}} it requires a dedicated meta-state, not mere broadcast. Critics (and the residue theorist, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}) object that it explains \emph{access} — being aware \emph{of} a state — while leaving its felt character untouched (the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}} split), and that mismatched/empty higher-order representations make trouble.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea: a mental state becomes \emph{conscious} when your mind represents \emph{itself} as being in that state — consciousness is a kind of inner spotlight, a thought about a thought. A pain you're in but in no way aware of isn't (yet) a conscious pain; it becomes conscious when a higher-order part of you registers it. The standard worry: that explains your being \emph{aware} that you're in pain, but does it explain the \emph{hurt}?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{question-hard-problem}{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}
precise statement of the issue: granting a complete account of the brain's information-processing and the functions it performs (the ``easy problems``; see \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{The hard problem of consciousness}}), why is that processing accompanied by \emph{subjective experience} at all — why is there something it is like to undergo it, rather than the same functions running with no inner aspect? Key terms: \emph{phenomenal consciousness} (what-it-is-likeness), the \emph{easy/hard} contrast, the \emph{explanatory gap}.

sub-issues that may deserve their own nodes:

\begin{itemize}
  \item Is the question well-posed, or does it rest on an illusion that there is a further fact to explain?
  \item If well-posed, is it answerable \emph{by us} — or constitutively beyond human concepts (\hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism (transcendental naturalism)}})?
  \item Does it have a \emph{metaphysical} answer (an ontological gap) or only an \emph{epistemic} one (\hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}})?
\end{itemize}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Are mental states substrate-independent?}}: that asks which \emph{substrates} can bear mind; this asks how \emph{anything} physical gives rise to experience in the first place — a prior, more general issue.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose we one day know everything about how the brain works as a machine. One question seems to survive: why is any of that accompanied by actual \emph{feeling}? Why isn't it all just happening silently, with no one experiencing it? This is the open question the ``hard problem'' poses — and people disagree about whether it's a real question, whether it has a normal scientific answer, or whether our brains simply aren't built to understand the answer.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link}{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}
gloss: the natural property P that grounds consciousness (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\emph{There is a natural property of the brain that explains consciousness}}) falls outside the range of concepts human minds can form. The mind-body problem is therefore \emph{soluble in itself but insoluble by us}: there is an answer, but we are constitutively unable to grasp it. The mysterian thesis (McGinn, 1989).

scope: a claim about \emph{our} cognitive limits, not about metaphysics. It does NOT say consciousness is non-physical or that the gap reflects a real ontological divide — on the contrary, it presupposes the link is natural. It is the conclusion of \hyperref[entry:argument-cognitive-closure]{\emph{McGinn's cognitive-closure argument for mysterianism}}.

who would deny it: optimistic naturalists (the problem is hard but tractable); reductive functionalists and illusionists (there is no residual hard problem to be closed to); and dualists, who read the same gap as ontological rather than cognitive (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}: that locates the shortfall in the \emph{world} (role does not exhaust the phenomenal); this locates it in \emph{us} (we cannot grasp the natural property that does).

\entryhead{claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans}{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}
gloss: the hard-problem concept (\hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{The hard problem of consciousness}}) arose \emph{within} human thought, from human introspection, at a time when there was no such notion anywhere to imitate. Whoever first framed it — the lineage runs through Leibniz's mill, Du Bois-Reymond's ``ignorabimus,'' Huxley, Nagel's bat, Levine's explanatory gap, Chalmers' naming of the problem — was not parroting a corpus. So one thing is \emph{demonstrated}, not conjectured: a mind of the human kind can generate this concept from its own situation, without a template. (Pushing the origin earlier — to dualist religious culture, to animism — changes nothing: those antecedents arose in humans too. Wherever the regress stops, it stops inside a human mind.)

what it licenses (and what it does not): an \textbf{asymmetry} between human and AI hard-problem discourse. For humans, de novo origination is an established historical fact. For present AI systems, only \emph{repetition} is established — every model that talks of qualia was trained on the human discussion, so origination remains untested (\hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}} is precisely the test: a consciousness-naive model either regenerates the concept or does not). The claim does NOT say AI cannot originate it; it says the human case is proven and the AI case is open — which is enough to block any \emph{current} appeal to ``we both talk this way'' as if the two provenances were on a par.

scope note (the three etiologies, for the AI side): if an AI does exhibit hard-problem discourse, there are at least three causal stories — (1) it copied humans; (2) it has its own idiosyncratic blind spot that precludes it from connecting the mechanistic dots; (3) it is conscious and faces the same hard problem humans do. These map onto the \emph{training-contamination}, \emph{architectural-access / conceptual-translation}, and \emph{ontological} members of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}} respectively — but here sorted as origins of the \emph{concept's appearance}, not kinds of gap. These three are the core trio of a longer etiology menu — \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI (where the cause sits)}} — which adds selection pressure, confabulated self-modelling, conceptual artifacts, strategic assertion, and observer projection. This claim itself takes no stand on which story is true for any system.

who would deny it: as history, almost no one — any earlier human source for the concept is still a human source, so the origination fact survives every regress. The live disputes target the \emph{use}: an illusionist accepts the history but denies it demonstrates anything creditable (originating a confusion is still confusion); and a symmetrist notes that human origination may itself have etiology (2) — the concept as the readout of a human blind spot — so the demonstrated capacity to \emph{generate} it does not yet certify the concept as tracking anything.

\begin{plainterms}
Somebody had to be first. When humans started asking ``why does any of this brain activity \emph{feel} like something?``, there was no book they got it from — the question was invented, out of looking at their own experience. That settles one thing: human-style minds \emph{can} come up with this puzzle on their own. For AI, that's exactly what is not yet settled: every AI that talks about the mystery of consciousness read our philosophy first. So the fair scoreboard today reads: humans — origination proven; AI — imitation proven, origination untested. There's a proposed experiment to settle it (\hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}}): raise a model on a diet with no consciousness talk at all, and see whether the puzzle shows up anyway.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-hylomorphism}{Hylomorphism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Aristotle}{Hylomorphism}
The mind/soul is the \emph{form} — the organising actuality — of a living body, not a separate substance and not merely its matter (\hyperref[entry:claim-soul-is-form-of-body]{\emph{The mind is the form of a living body}}; Aristotle's \emph{De Anima}; Aquinas; modern hylomorphists Jaworski, Marmodoro). A human being is one substance, the living animal, of which mental capacities are form-level features. It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by locating mind as the form of a suitably organised living thing rather than as either an extra substance or a mere material aggregate.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: it threads between \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} (no separate mental substance) and reductive materialism (the organism is more than its matter). Although ``form ≈ organisation'' sounds like \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}}, the form is tied to a \emph{living} substance with intrinsic ends, not to a substrate-neutral causal role — so it does not straightforwardly license \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}.

\begin{plainterms}
An old idea (Aristotle) enjoying a revival: the mind isn't a ghost inside the body, nor is it just the meat — it's the \emph{form} of a living body, the way a statue's shape isn't a second thing beside the bronze yet isn't the bronze either. You are one living creature, and ``mind'' names what that creature can do in virtue of being organised the way it is. It resists both ``soul in a machine'' dualism and ``nothing but atoms'' materialism.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{I}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-conceivability-entails-possibility}{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}
gloss: the modal-epistemology bridge of the zombie argument. If a scenario is \emph{ideally} conceivable — coherent under idealised rational reflection, with no concealed contradiction — then it is metaphysically possible. (On the two-dimensional version: ideal \emph{primary} conceivability entails primary possibility, and for the zombie case primary and secondary intensions are taken to coincide.)

scope: a general principle linking what can be coherently conceived to what can be. It is the load-bearing, contested step that converts \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\emph{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}} into a conclusion against physicalism. It is \emph{not} the trivial claim that whatever we can loosely imagine is possible; it concerns \emph{ideal} conceivability.

argued both ways (it is not a brute premise): defended by \textbf{modal rationalism} (\hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\emph{Modal rationalism — ideal conceivability is a guide to possibility}}) — once \emph{primary} and \emph{secondary} intensions are distinguished, the apparent counterexamples are misdescribed possibilities (\hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\emph{Kripkean a-posteriori necessities are misdescribed genuine possibilities, not conceived impossibilities}}), so ideal primary conceivability tracks primary possibility. Attacked by the \textbf{a-posteriori-necessity objection} (\hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\emph{Conceivability is not a reliable guide to possibility (the a-posteriori-necessity objection)}}) — Kripkean necessities (\hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\emph{Some necessary truths can be known only a posteriori}}) already break the link, so a zombie may be conceivable yet impossible (the \textbf{type-B physicalist} reading — \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}). The deeper, allied \textbf{phenomenal-concept strategy} (\hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}}) explains the conceivability as an artefact of phenomenal concepts. The crux throughout: whether the \emph{phenomenal} case has the appearance/reality gap that lets the water case escape, or not.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\emph{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}} (the antecedent it operates on) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain]{\emph{An intuition's evidential weight tracks the case's resemblance to the exemplars it was formed on}} (an epistemology of \emph{intuitions about cases}); this is specifically about the conceivability→possibility inference.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the hinge the whole zombie argument swings on: the idea that \emph{if you can coherently imagine something — really coherently, with no hidden contradiction — then it's genuinely possible}. Grant that, and ``I can imagine a zombie'' turns into ``a zombie is possible,'' which would mean experience is something extra beyond the physical. The classic pushback borrows from science: ``water is H2O'' is something you \emph{can} imagine being false, yet it's necessarily true — so being able to imagine otherwise doesn't always prove real possibility. Maybe zombies are like that: imaginable but impossible.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-idealism}{Idealism}{Position}{Idealism}
What is fundamental is mind or experience; the physical world is constituted by, or derivative of, consciousness (\hyperref[entry:claim-reality-is-fundamentally-mental]{\emph{Reality is fundamentally mental}}). Variants: Berkeley's subjective idealism (to be is to be perceived), Kastrup's \emph{analytic idealism} (one universal mind whose dissociations are individual subjects), Hoffman's conscious realism (spacetime is a perceptual interface). It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} from the far side: there is no puzzle of extracting mind from matter, because matter is an appearance \emph{within} mind.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: it is the inverse of physicalism on the direction of grounding, and unlike \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{Neutral monism}} (neutral base) or \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} (two kinds) it makes the \emph{mental} fundamental and the physical derivative. Its mirror-image burden is the \emph{decomposition/structure} problem: deriving a law-governed, intersubjectively shared physical world out of mind.

\begin{plainterms}
Idealism turns the usual picture upside down: instead of matter being basic and minds somehow arising from it, \emph{mind} is basic and the physical world is something that shows up \emph{within} experience — more like a shared dream with strict rules than a lump of stuff outside us. The appeal: the hard problem (``how does dead matter produce feeling?``) never arises, because there was never any mind-free matter to start from. The bill it must pay: explaining why this ``dream'' is so stable, lawful, and the same for everyone.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-illusionism}{Illusionism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Frankish}{Illusionism}
There is no phenomenal consciousness of the qualia-laden kind; introspection \emph{misrepresents} our states as having intrinsic, ineffable feel (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}}). The hard problem is therefore dissolved and replaced by the \textbf{illusion problem} — explain why physical processing represents itself as phenomenal. A thoroughly physicalist, functionalist-friendly view (Keith Frankish coined ``illusionism``, 2016; Daniel Dennett). It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by denying its explanandum, and so it directly \texttt{rejects} \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: it is the realist views' common opponent — opposite to \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}, and \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, all of which are \emph{realist} about phenomenal character. It sits closest to strong/role functionalism (\hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}}) and sits comfortably with \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism (transcendental naturalism)}}, which holds the gap is real but unsolvable; illusionism holds there is no gap to solve.

\begin{plainterms}
Illusionism's bold move: the ``inner feel'' of experience — the redness of red, the ache of pain as something \emph{intrinsic} — isn't real; it only seems real because our brains are wired to report on themselves that way. So there's no extra mystery about how matter produces feeling, because there's no feeling-stuff to produce — only the very convincing \emph{impression} of it, which a complete brain science can explain. The new question becomes: why is the illusion so powerful?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure}{Implementing a computation requires the right counterfactual dispositions across the whole transition table}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Implementing a computation requires the right counterfactual dispositions across the whole transition table}
gloss: a physical system implements a given computation only if its states carry the right counterfactual-causal dispositions across the \emph{entire} state-transition table — ``had it been in state A with input i, it would have gone to B'' — not merely if its actual run can be relabelled to fit the trajectory. (Chalmers, ``Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton?``, 1996.)

scope: a constraint on the implementation relation. It blocks Putnam-style triviality (gerrymandered post-hoc mappings) by demanding robust dispositions. It does NOT claim a system implements only one computation — only that it does not implement \emph{every} one.

who would deny it: a defender of unrestricted triviality (Putnam's appendix to \emph{Representation and Reality}) allowing mappings keyed to the actual trajectory; or one holding even counterfactual constraints can be gerrymandered (Godfrey-Smith, Sprevak).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\emph{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}}: that asserts non-uniqueness of decomposition; this asserts a \emph{constraint} (counterfactual robustness) that narrows the admissible decompositions.

\begin{plainterms}
For a physical thing to genuinely \emph{run} a particular computation, it's not enough that you can retell the story of what it actually did and make it fit. It must also be true that \emph{if} things had gone differently, it would have responded in the matching way — across every branch, not just the one that happened. That ``what it would have done otherwise'' requirement is what stops you from claiming a rock secretly computes everything: a rock has no such robust, reliable dispositions. (It doesn't say a system runs only \emph{one} computation — just that it doesn't run \emph{every} one.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-indexical-reply}{The Indexical Reply — Mary learns where she is in the world, not what is in the world}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Perry}{The Indexical Reply — Mary learns where she is in the world, not what is in the world}
Raises thought-experiment CQ4 (bridge) against \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}: even granting that Mary learns new truths on release, the bridge from ``truths not deducible from the complete physical description'' to ``physicalism is false'' fails, because \emph{self-locating} truths are not deducible from \textbf{any} objective description — complete physics included — and their non-deducibility has never been a mark against the description's completeness.

inference form: deductive, given a classification of the new truths as self-locating.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item No objective description of the world, however complete, entails self-locating truths: a being who knew every general fact could still not know ``I am here now'' or ``it is now noon'' (Lewis 1979, the two gods who know the whole world-book but not which god they are). This silence is banal — maps are not defective for lacking a \emph{you-are-here} arrow.
  \item What Mary comes to know on seeing red is of this kind: how \emph{she herself} reacts to red light — ``\emph{this} is what seeing red does to \emph{me}``, ``\emph{I} am in \emph{this} state'' (Perry 2001).
  \item So the truths Mary could not deduce in the room are exactly the truths \emph{no} complete objective description owes anyone — their non-deducibility reflects the perspectival form of the knowledge, not a missing ingredient in the world. ∴ \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-self-locating-knowledge]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is self-locating knowledge of how she herself reacts to red, not a new general fact}} — the Knowledge Argument's bridge breaks: \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\emph{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}} is true only in the innocuous sense in which ``I am here now'' is not physically deducible, which supports no conclusion against physicalism.
\end{enumerate}

Placement among the replies: it concedes more than \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\emph{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-acquaintance-hypothesis]{\emph{The Acquaintance Hypothesis — Mary meets the experience rather than learning a truth}} (Mary learns genuine \emph{facts}) while claiming less than \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}} needs (the facts are situational, not worldly). Against the ability theorist it is openly revisionary: the bike-rider's learning is fact-learning about one's own body-plus-machine situation (\hyperref[entry:argument-bike-rider-learns-facts]{\emph{The bike rider learns facts — the ability hypothesis rests on a false dichotomy}}), so Lewis's abilities/facts dichotomy is the wrong fault line. It shares with the RoboMary move (\hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\emph{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}}) the thought that what release supplies concerns Mary's \emph{own reactive machinery} — but unlike that move it grants the learning is real rather than an artefact of misimagining the premise.

weakest premise: (2), via the \textbf{collapse objection} (Chalmers; Tye). Mary suffers no ordinary self-locating ignorance — she knows who, where, and when she is. So the indexical content of her new knowledge cannot be of the ``I am here now'' kind; it must be carried by a demonstrative concept of the experience-type itself (``\emph{this} kind of state``), and then the reply re-enacts \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}} with ``indexical'' relabelling — inheriting the phenomenal-concept strategy's standing cost (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}}). A second exposure sits in (2)'s flank: facts about how \emph{Mary's particular brain} reacts to red are objective physical facts, already inside \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\emph{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}} by stipulation — so mere particularity carries no weight, and the entire load rests on the indexical mode of access being both genuinely novel and genuinely innocent.

\begin{plainterms}
The Mary story (\hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}) says: she knew every physical fact, yet learned something new on seeing red — so the physical facts can't be all of them. This reply agrees she learned something, and even that it was a fact — but points out there's a whole family of facts that \emph{no} complete description of the world could ever hand you: where \emph{you} are in it. A perfect map of the city still can't tell you ``you are here``; two all-knowing gods could still wonder which god they are. What Mary picks up — ``so \emph{this} is what red does to \emph{me}'' — is that kind of fact: news about her own position and reactions, not a missing page from physics. The standard counter-punch: Mary already knows exactly who, where, and when she is — so what position-fact could she possibly lack? Pressed on this, the reply tends to morph into a different escape, the ``old fact under a new label'' one (\hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}}), with its own troubles.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-integrated-information-theory}{Integrated Information Theory (IIT)}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Tononi}{Integrated Information Theory (IIT)}
Consciousness \emph{is} integrated information: a system is conscious to the degree it has irreducible intrinsic cause-effect power (Φ) over itself, and the \emph{character} of an experience is the geometry of that cause-effect structure (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\emph{Consciousness is identical to a system's integrated information (Φ)}}; Tononi, Koch). Built from phenomenological axioms (intrinsic existence, integration, exclusion), it answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} with an identity rather than a reduction-to-function.

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: it makes consciousness depend on a system's \emph{actual cause-effect architecture}, not on its input-output behaviour or the computation it performs — so two behaviourally identical systems can differ in consciousness, and a feed-forward \textbf{digital simulation of a brain has Φ ≈ 0}. It therefore \texttt{rejects} \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} and answers \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Are mental states substrate-independent?}} negatively for digital computers, bearing directly against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}} (an LLM on standard hardware would, by IIT's measure, be barely conscious). This sets it sharply apart from \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global Workspace Theory}} (functional availability) and from functionalism generally; its own embarrassments are panpsychist (simple inactive grids can score non-zero Φ).

\begin{plainterms}
IIT flips the usual question: instead of asking what the brain \emph{does}, it asks how tightly a system's parts make a difference to each other — its ``integrated information,'' Φ. The more a system is an irreducible whole shaping itself, the more conscious it is, and the \emph{pattern} of those interactions \emph{is} the specific experience. Striking upshot for AI: a digital computer could perfectly imitate a person yet, because of how its chips are wired (one-way, modular), have almost no Φ — so on this theory it would feel almost nothing. Substrate (the actual cause-effect wiring) matters, not just the behaviour or the program.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain}{An intuition's evidential weight tracks the case's resemblance to the exemplars it was formed on}{Claim}{An intuition's evidential weight tracks the case's resemblance to the exemplars it was formed on}
gloss: thought-experiment intuitions are calibrated on actual, encountered cases. A verdict about a hypothetical carries evidential weight in proportion to how closely that hypothetical resembles those exemplars — in scale, in structure, in kind. Cases far outside the trained range yield verdicts of degraded warrant: the faculty is being run on inputs it was never tuned for. (Cf. Williamson on the epistemology of thought experiments; Dennett on ``intuition pumps``; experimental- philosophy findings of intuition instability for exotic cases.)

scope: a methodological/epistemic principle about the \emph{warrant} of intuitive verdicts, neutral on any first-order theory of mind. It does NOT say far-fetched cases are impossible, nor that distant intuitions are always false — only that their evidential weight decays with dissimilarity to paradigm cases, so a distant verdict cannot bear heavy argumentative load unsupported.

who would deny it: a rationalist about modal/conceptual intuition who holds that clear conceivability delivers reliable verdicts regardless of a case's distance from experience.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\emph{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}}: that \emph{applies} this principle to the blockhead; this is the general principle it instances.

\begin{plainterms}
Your gut feelings about made-up cases are trained on the real cases you've actually met. So a hunch is trustworthy roughly to the degree the imagined case resembles those familiar ones — in size, in structure, in kind. Run your intuition on something wildly outside anything you've ever encountered and its verdicts get shaky; you're using a tool far outside the range it was built for. (It doesn't say far-fetched cases are impossible, or that distant hunches are always wrong — only that they can't carry heavy argumentative weight on their own.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry}{The intuition-domain asymmetry — the cosmic table is a far extrapolation, the brain-scale isomorph a near neighbour}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The intuition-domain asymmetry — the cosmic table is a far extrapolation, the brain-scale isomorph a near neighbour}
inference form: subsumption under a principle (defeasible) — apply the warrant principle to two cases and read off a difference.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain]{\emph{An intuition's evidential weight tracks the case's resemblance to the exemplars it was formed on}}) An intuitive verdict's evidential weight decays with the case's dissimilarity, in scale and structure, to the exemplars the intuition was formed on (actual brains/agents).
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}) The behaviour-matching lookup table is of cosmic scale (\textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}9600 rows) with no brain-like internal structure — maximally dissimilar to any paradigm mind on both axes.
  \item A brain-scale silicon isomorph preserving the brain's causal organisation (\hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}) differs from a paradigm mind only in substrate — a near neighbour in scale and structure. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-blockhead-deflation-targeted-not-global]{\emph{Deflating the cosmic-table intuition does not deflate intuitions about brain-scale duplicates}}) the warrant-withdrawal that hits the blockhead ``no mind'' intuition does not hit the isomorph intuition. Horn 2 of \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\emph{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}} is therefore \emph{targeted}, not global, and the dilemma does not prove too much.
\end{enumerate}

why this is not question-begging: the asymmetry is read off \emph{distance from exemplars} (an epistemic fact about intuition), not off a prior ruling that organisation is mind-constitutive. Premise (3) cites organisation only as a \emph{similarity} metric to paradigm cases, available to friend and foe of functionalism alike; it does not assert organisation suffices for mind.

weakest premise: Block's relocation from scale to \emph{kind}. He may insist the ``no mind'' verdict targets brute \emph{storage} as a kind — reliable at any size — so a scale-extrapolation point misses it. Reply (now its own node, \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\emph{Pure storage is not a stable kind at mind-matching scale}}): at mind-matching scale ``pure storage'' is not a stable kind — addressing/retrieval over \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}9600 entries is itself a vast organised causal system, so the cosmic ``table'' is not the same kind as the surveyable phone-book the intuition was trained on. That reply does dialectical work beyond this argument's scale point; whether it fully answers Block is the live crux.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\emph{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}} (which it supports) and from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\emph{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}} (which establishes premise 2): this argument is solely about the \emph{epistemology of the intuition}, bounding horn 2's reach.

\begin{plainterms}
This defends a tricky move — distrusting our ``nobody home'' hunch about the giant lookup-table mind because it's a wild imaginary case (\hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\emph{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}}) — from a ``you can't have it both ways'' complaint. The worry: if we throw out that hunch on the grounds that gut feelings about bizarre imaginary cases aren't reliable, don't we then also have to throw out the gut feeling on our \emph{own} side, that a silicon copy of a brain would be conscious? The answer is no, and here's the principle behind it: a hunch is trustworthy roughly to the degree the case resembles the everyday examples that trained the hunch. The cosmic lookup table is unimaginably huge and utterly unlike any real brain — a wild extrapolation, so the hunch about it is weak. A brain-scale silicon twin that copies the brain's actual wiring is a near-twin of the real thing — so the hunch about \emph{it} stays strong. Same principle, different distances, so distrusting the one needn't poison the other. (And no cheating: ``similarity'' is measured by closeness to real minds, not by secretly assuming up front that organization is what makes a mind.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation}{Is first-person acquaintance with one's own experience a genuine observation, or itself a representation?}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, contested}{Is first-person acquaintance with one's own experience a genuine observation, or itself a representation?}
precise statement of the issue: when a subject introspects a current experience, what is the epistemic status of the deliverance? Two answers divide the field:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{observation (datum)} — introspection is direct acquaintance with the experience itself: a datum prior to, and independent of, any report. On this answer the experience is \emph{observed} (in the absolute sense of \hyperref[entry:concept-unobservability-in-principle]{\textsc{Unobservability in principle (intersubjective vs. absolute)}}) by exactly one observer per case; each subject possesses one instance known from the inside; and evidential standards built for never-observed posits do not govern the feel.
  \item \textbf{representation} — introspection delivers a higher-order representation \emph{of} the experience, of the same broad epistemic kind as a report: fallible, constructed, possibly confabulated. On this answer the first-/third-person wall carries no special evidential cargo — introspection is one more report channel, and whatever discount rules govern outward reports (e.g. phenomenality-neutrality arguments) apply inward as well.
\end{itemize}

who answers which way (positions in the literature, not characters): acquaintance theorists and phenomenal realists take the datum answer — Russellian acquaintance, Searle's first-person ontology, Chalmers on direct phenomenal concepts. Illusionists, heterophenomenologists, and attention-schema theorists take the representation answer — Dennett (introspection as theorist's-fiction report), Frankish (the introspected feel as user-illusion), Graziano (the self-model paints in the glow).

why it is a crux: most evidential standards in the neighbouring debates presuppose an answer. A coherence-plus-usefulness standard for in-principle unobservables (\hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\emph{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}}) reaches the feel only if introspective deliverances are not observations; an appeal to acquaintance as the bedrock datum (\hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\emph{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}} read metaphysically) blocks that standard only if they are. Hence the standoff registered on both sides of the AI-hard-problem exchange: arguments from either standard ``relocate rather than refute'' against an opponent who answers this question the other way.

sub-issue (may deserve its own node if pressed): by what \emph{shared} standard could this question itself be argued? Each side's preferred instrument appears to presuppose its own answer — applying the idleness standard to the question presupposes the representation answer; citing acquaintance as evidence presupposes the datum answer. Whether any neutral ground exists, or whether the question marks a genuine bedrock divergence, is itself open.

\begin{plainterms}
Right now you're aware of your own experience — say, a mild ache, or the colors in front of you. Question: is that awareness like \emph{seeing} the experience itself, directly — the one thing in the world you get without any middleman? Or is it more like reading a \emph{report} your brain wrote about itself — a summary that could be simplified, spun, or just wrong? It sounds like a small distinction, but huge debates turn on it. If it's direct seeing, then every person has rock-solid front-row evidence that feelings exist, evidence no outside test could ever replace. If it's a report, then your ``inner eye'' deserves the same skepticism as any other testimony — including a chatbot's eloquent claims about its inner life. And the question has a frustrating twist: each side's favorite method for settling debates already assumes its own answer here, which is why this one keeps ending in a stare-down.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{K}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-knowhow-is-knowledge-that}{Knowing how to do something is a species of propositional knowledge}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Stanley}{Knowing how to do something is a species of propositional knowledge}
gloss: the \textbf{intellectualist} thesis (Stanley \& Williamson, ``Knowing How``, 2001). To know how to F is to know, of some way \emph{w}, that \emph{w} is a way for one to F — grasped under a \emph{practical mode of presentation}. Know-how is not a separate, non-propositional category alongside knowledge of facts; it is knowledge of facts about ways of acting, held in a distinctive, action-guiding manner. The cyclist who learns to ride does learn truths — about how her body and the machine engage — even though she could not state them in words.

scope: a thesis in general epistemology, motivated by the syntax and semantics of ``knows how'' ascriptions (which embed questions, like ``knows where'' and ``knows why``). It does not deny that skills involve motor capacities, training, and inarticulable grasp; it locates all that in the \emph{mode} of grasping a proposition, not in an escape from propositions. Nor does it claim the relevant truths are general: they may be facts about one's own particular body and situation.

who would deny it: Ryleans, for whom know-how is a capacity not reducible to knowledge-that (and who press a regress against intellectualism — if applying knowledge-that itself takes know-how, the analysis loops); ability theorists about phenomenal knowledge (Lewis, Nemirow), whose reply to the Knowledge Argument needs abilities to be non-propositional (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}}); and empirically-minded critics who take dissociations (skilled action preserved in amnesia, intact ``knowledge'' without skill) to show two systems, not one knowledge under two modes.

\begin{plainterms}
Is knowing how to ride a bike a totally different thing from knowing facts? This claim says no: when you learn to ride, you come to know something \emph{true} — roughly, that \emph{this} way of balancing and pedalling is a way for you to ride — you just hold that truth in a practical, do-it form rather than a say-it form. The fact that you can't put it into words doesn't make it not a fact; it makes it a fact you grasp with your body.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-knowledge-argument}{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Jackson}{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}
primary definition: Frank Jackson's thought experiment (\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}). Mary is a brilliant scientist who has lived her whole life in a black-and-white room and learns about the world through a black-and-white monitor. She comes to know \textbf{all the physical facts} about colour vision — wavelengths, retinal processing, the neuroscience of ``red.'' Then she leaves the room and sees a ripe tomato for the first time. Intuitively, she \textbf{learns something new}: what it is \emph{like} to see red. But she already knew all the physical facts — so what she learns is a \emph{further}, non-physical fact about experience.

what it targets: physicalism — the thesis that all facts are physical facts. If Mary, knowing every physical fact, still learns something on seeing red, then not all facts are physical; there are phenomenal facts the physical description leaves out.

relation to neighbours: an \emph{epistemic} route to anti-physicalism (about what can be \emph{known/deduced} from the physical), where the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} takes a \emph{modal} route (about what is \emph{possible}). Both press toward \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}}. Its standard rebuttals turn on what ``learns something'' amounts to.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary has spent her entire life in a black-and-white room, but she's the world's expert on the science of colour — she knows every physical fact about how eyes and brains process ``red,'' down to the last detail. One day she walks outside and sees a red rose for the first time. Surely she learns \emph{something} — what red actually looks like. But she already knew all the physical facts. So whatever she just learned seems to be an \emph{extra} fact, one the complete physics left out — which would mean experience isn't captured by physical facts alone. (The popular reply: maybe she didn't learn a new \emph{fact}, just gained a new \emph{ability} — to recognise and imagine red — which isn't the same as discovering a new truth.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-knowledge-argument}{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Jackson}{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}
inference form: thought-experiment counterexample to physicalism (an epistemic gap, then an epistemic→ontological step).

\begin{itemize}
