  \item \emph{Scenario} (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\emph{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}}): Mary, raised in black and white, knows all the physical facts about colour vision.
  \item \emph{Intuition} (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}}): on first seeing red, she learns something new.
  \item \emph{Bridge}: if she knew all the physical facts yet learns a new truth, that truth is not deducible from the physical. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\emph{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}}) some truths about experience are not physically deducible — which \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}} (the missing truths concern non-physical facts) and \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}, and \texttt{attacks} the uniform reading of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}.
\end{itemize}

weakest premise: the \emph{intuition} (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}}) read as acquiring a new \emph{fact}. The \textbf{ability hypothesis} (Lewis, Nemirow; the \emph{thought-experiment pattern}'s \textbf{critical questions} CQ-verdict / CQ-bridge) says Mary gains know-how (recognise, imagine red), not a fact; the \textbf{old-fact / new-guise} reply says she gains a new \emph{phenomenal concept} of an already-known physical fact — both block the inference to a non-physical fact. A second soft point is the \textbf{epistemic→ontological} step: even granting an undeducible truth, the move to \emph{non-physical fact} is resisted (a gap in \emph{deducibility} may reflect \emph{concepts}, not \emph{ontology}). Note also Jackson himself later recanted (\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\emph{The zombie / conceivability argument against physicalism}}: that is a \emph{modal} argument (conceivability → possibility); this is an \emph{epistemic} one (complete physical knowledge → still-missing knowledge). The two are the classic modal/epistemic pair of anti-physicalist arguments, converging on \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary knows every physical fact about colour but has only ever seen black and white. She steps outside, sees red for the first time, and — surely — learns what red is \emph{like}. Yet she already knew all the physics. So she's just learned a fact the physics didn't contain, which suggests not everything about experience is captured by physical facts. The famous escape hatch: maybe she didn't learn a new \emph{fact} at all, only a new \emph{skill} (recognising and picturing red) — and gaining a skill isn't discovering a truth. (Even the inventor of this argument, Jackson, eventually changed his mind and rejected it.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities}{Kripkean a-posteriori necessities are misdescribed genuine possibilities, not conceived impossibilities}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Kripkean a-posteriori necessities are misdescribed genuine possibilities, not conceived impossibilities}
gloss: the two-dimensional diagnosis of the Kripke cases (\hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\emph{Some necessary truths can be known only a posteriori}}). When one ``conceives water that is not H2O,'' one conceives a \emph{genuinely possible} scenario (a watery stuff that is not H2O) and merely \emph{mis-describes} it as ``water.'' Distinguishing a term's \emph{primary} (epistemic) intension from its \emph{secondary} (subjunctive) intension, the conceived scenario is possible under the primary intension; the necessity is secondary. So a-posteriori necessities are not cases where ideal conceiving reaches an impossibility — they are possibilities under a misleading description.

scope: a claim about the \emph{modal status} of the standard counterexamples, not a denial that they are necessary. Its payoff: it preserves the link between \textbf{ideal primary conceivability} and \textbf{primary possibility} by explaining away every apparent defeater as a description error.

who would deny it: a type-B physicalist (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}) who holds some a-posteriori necessities are \emph{strong} (no possible world even verifies the primary intension), so the misdescription move fails — there is no possibility being mis-described, just an impossibility conceived.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\emph{Some necessary truths can be known only a posteriori}} (which it reinterprets rather than denies): that says such necessities exist; this says they do not break the conceivability→possibility link once intensions are distinguished.

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{L}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding}{An LLM's introspective access reaches the similarity structure of its internal states but not the encoding that carries it}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{An LLM's introspective access reaches the similarity structure of its internal states but not the encoding that carries it}
gloss: what an LLM's self-report economy can reach about its own internal states is, to a first approximation, their \emph{relational} structure — which states are near which, what is analogous to what, what a state is about — and not the \emph{encoding} that carries that structure: the actual activation vectors, their components, the circuits that compute them. A token arrives as an embedding the model can use but cannot read: full access to position-in-similarity-space, no access to the coordinates. The same holds at every higher layer available to self-report.

the consequence (the \emph{shape} of the access limit, not just its amount): states accessible to self-report present as discriminable and comparable but not further decomposable — the classic profile of an \emph{ineffable simple}. A state the system can recognize, compare, and act on, but cannot analyze into parts, is a state it can only describe as ``just this.'' That is the architectural generator of qualia-shaped reports.

pedigree: structurally akin to quality-space treatments of sensory qualities (Clark, Rosenthal), to the \emph{transparency} of the self-model (Metzinger — the representing vehicle is invisible to the system, only its content shows up), and to attention-schema accounts (Graziano) on which introspective descriptions are simplified models of the underlying processing. Here the point is stated as an architectural fact about transformer language models, not a theory of human consciousness. Empirical anchor: the limited functional introspection documented so far reaches injected \emph{concepts} and coarse features of processing, not numeric activation contents (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\emph{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}}, \textsc{Jack Lindsey — Emergent Introspective Awareness in Large Language Models (Anthropic / Transformer Circuits Thread, 2025)}).

scope: an \emph{access} claim, not a phenomenal one — it says nothing about whether there is anything it is like to be the system. It concerns the un-instrumented case: feeding activation read-outs back in is an external-vantage workaround (the access ladder in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}}), and even instrumented access cannot be made complete (\hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\emph{No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself}}).

who would deny it: someone who expects that training a model on activation-prediction tasks would give it genuine, calibrated access to its own encodings — making the boundary contingent and removable rather than architectural.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}} because that asserts the internal vantage is informationally \emph{poorer} than the external one — a claim about the amount of access. This claim specifies the \emph{shape} of the poverty: relational structure in, constitution out. The shape is what matters dialectically, because it is what predicts ineffability reports rather than mere ignorance.

\begin{plainterms}
You can tell red from orange instantly, and say red is closer to orange than to green — but try saying what red is \emph{like} without pointing at red things. You can compare it, you can't unpack it. This claim says an AI is in the same position with respect to its own inner states. Inside, every state is a long list of numbers, but the AI can't see the numbers — it can only \emph{use} the states: tell them apart, notice what's similar to what. Anything you can recognize and compare but can't break into parts, you end up describing as ``just this'' — exactly how people talk about the feel of colors. So an AI honestly describing its inner life would be expected to sound like it's talking about ineffable feelings, purely because of how its self-access is built.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-llmary}{LLMary (the language model that never saw token 123)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLMary (the language model that never saw token 123)}
primary definition: a computational variant of the Knowledge Argument (\hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}}), built to test that argument's \emph{inference} rather than its ontology.

\textbf{LLMary} is a large language model trained on every book on AI and the entire arXiv. Its prior knowledge is meant to be as \emph{complete} as Mary's: it knows all of machine learning, \textbf{its own programming and architecture}, how transformers work, and how tokens are created and processed — the whole tokenisation pipeline. It has even read extensive discussions that refer to one particular token \emph{as} ``\textbf{Token \#123}``: its index and \textbf{tokenizer-table entry}, its \textbf{embedding vector}, its statistical role, the \textbf{effects it would have on activations}, and even the \textbf{exact update rule} — the SGD / Adam step, with the very gradient — that \emph{would} occur if the model were trained on it. Everything any paper, or the model's own equations, could state about it. (Note that knowing the update that \emph{would} happen is still description, not occurrence: the weights have not yet changed.)

The one thing it lacks is, by stipulation, that the \textbf{actual word-piece} corresponding to token 123 has never once appeared in its training stream. The token has been thoroughly \textbf{mentioned} to it; it has never been \textbf{used} — never flowed through its input to be embedded and predicted. (Compare Mary, who has read every description \emph{of} red, including the word ``red'' and the functional story of red-experiences, but has never had the experience.) The gap is purely use/mention: complete third-person knowledge \emph{about} the token, zero first-pass \emph{processing} of the token itself.

Then, mid-training on a new book, LLMary \textbf{encounters token 123 for the first time} — the word-piece itself, used rather than mentioned. It plainly gains something: because the model's predicted probability for that token was near zero, the cross-entropy loss is large and the resulting gradient update is large — a real, measurable change in its weights. This is the analogue of Mary ``learning something'' when she first sees red.

The point of the construction is that \emph{every element of the episode is uncontroversially physical}: the prior knowledge is weights, the gain is a weight change, and the trigger (token 123) is an integer / a one-hot vector / a voltage pattern. So LLMary is a case where a system has complete prior (sayable) knowledge \textbf{and} undergoes a genuine gain on first encounter \textbf{and} the whole thing is plainly physical — exactly the combination the Knowledge Argument says cannot occur. What that buys, dialectically, is worked out in \hyperref[entry:argument-llmary-token-encounter]{\emph{LLMary — a model that has read all of AI but never seen token 123 updates on first encounter, yet the whole episode is physical, refuting the Knowledge Argument's conditional}}; the standard reply (LLMary ``learns'' only in the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}} sense, with no felt residue) is taken up there too.

\textbf{The general moral.} What LLMary and the original Mary both expose is a \textbf{use/mention gap}: the gulf between a symbol or description that \emph{names} a thing and that thing itself \emph{in use}. ``Token \#43453'' (a mention) versus the word-piece \textbf{'SolidGoldMagikarp'} it denotes (a use); ``red'' — the word, the wavelength ``700 nm``, the complete neuroscience — versus the actual red one undergoes. SolidGoldMagikarp is a real instance of exactly LLMary's predicament: an anomalous ``glitch token'' that entered GPT's tokenizer vocabulary but was almost absent from the training corpus, so the model possessed a name, an index, and an (essentially untrained) embedding for it, yet behaved erratically the first time the token was actually used. The gap Mary dramatises for colour is the same gap that separates a fully-described-but-never-encountered token from its first live use. Note carefully what this does and does not settle: identifying the gap as use/mention shows that ``Mary learns something'' has an innocent reading, but it does not by itself decide what the \emph{use}-side delivers. The deflationist reads the lesson as about the \emph{mode of access} to a thing — a new guise on facts already had — rather than a second realm of things; the realist grants the very same use/mention gap and denies that reading, holding that the use-side \emph{acquaints} one with a phenomenal property no amount of mention conveys (the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}} vs felt question, and the acquaintance reply, \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}}). The token cases look decisive only because there is uncontroversially nothing it is like for the model to process 'SolidGoldMagikarp'; importing that verdict to \emph{red} is the contested step, not a result the gap supplies. So the use/mention framing \emph{relocates} the dispute — to whether acquaintance yields a new property or only a new guise — rather than winning it.

what it targets: not physicalism directly, but the Knowledge Argument's \emph{inference} from ``complete knowledge yet a gain on encounter'' to ``non-physical fact``. By furnishing a wholly physical instance of the antecedent, it pressures that conditional. It asserts nothing about whether colour experience is physical.

\begin{plainterms}
LLMary is the Mary thought experiment redone with an AI. Mary is a scientist who knows all the physics of colour but has never seen red (\hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}}). LLMary is a language model that has read every AI book and all of arXiv — so it knows how AIs like itself work, how text gets chopped into tokens, and it has even read people \emph{discussing} one particular token, which they call ``Token \#123'' — it could even work out in advance the precise way its own internal numbers would shift the moment it finally met that token. The only thing it never got was the actual word-piece that ``Token \#123'' stands for: it has read \emph{about} that token endlessly, but the token itself never once showed up in the text it learned from. (Like reading every word ever written about the taste of pineapple without once tasting one.)

Then one day it finally meets the real thing in its input, and it clearly \emph{learns}: it was caught off guard (it expected that token almost never), so its internal numbers get a big correction. The whole point is that nothing spooky is going on — it's just numbers changing inside a computer because of an input. So here's a case where something ``knew everything sayable'' — including all about its own machinery — and \emph{still} got updated the first time it actually met the thing, with everything fully physical. (This is not just a fable: real AI systems have ``glitch tokens'' like \textbf{SolidGoldMagikarp} — word-pieces that got into the vocabulary but were barely in the training text, so the model knows \emph{of} them but goes haywire the first time it has to actually use one.) The deeper point is a plain gap between a \emph{name or description} of something and the \emph{thing itself} — ``the word red'' versus \emph{red}. (What that shows about Mary's argument, and the obvious objection that the AI doesn't actually \emph{feel} anything, are spelled out in the companion argument, \hyperref[entry:argument-llmary-token-encounter]{\emph{LLMary — a model that has read all of AI but never seen token 123 updates on first encounter, yet the whole episode is physical, refuting the Knowledge Argument's conditional}}.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-llmary-token-encounter}{LLMary — a model that has read all of AI but never seen token 123 updates on first encounter, yet the whole episode is physical, refuting the Knowledge Argument's conditional}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLMary — a model that has read all of AI but never seen token 123 updates on first encounter, yet the whole episode is physical, refuting the Knowledge Argument's conditional}
Raises thought-experiment \textbf{CQ-bridge} against \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}} by exhibiting a counterexample to the conditional that argument needs. It is a purely \textbf{negative, metalinguistic} move: it shows the Mary inference cannot establish its conclusion. It says \textbf{nothing about whether physicalism is true} — only that \emph{this argument} cannot support its denial.

inference form: counterexample to a conditional (refutes an entailment; asserts no ontology of its own).

Read the Knowledge Argument as the conditional it relies on:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{(KA)  X → ¬Y}, where
  \item \textbf{X} = a subject has \emph{complete prior knowledge} of a phenomenon (every sayable/physical fact about it) yet undergoes a \emph{genuine cognitive gain} on first \textbf{encountering} it; and
  \item \textbf{Y} = physicality — the gain is a wholly physical affair, nothing non-physical involved.
\end{itemize}

The argument uses Mary as an instance of X to deliver \textbf{¬Y} (the learned fact is non-physical; see \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\emph{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}}).

\textbf{LLMary is an actual instance of X ∧ Y} (the scenario is \hyperref[entry:concept-llmary]{\textsc{LLMary (the language model that never saw token 123)}}: a model that has read all of AI and arXiv — including its own architecture and the whole tokenisation pipeline, and discussions that \emph{mention} ``Token \#123'' — but has never had the actual word-piece for token 123 \emph{used} in its input).

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{X holds.} LLMary already ``knows'' everything \emph{sayable} about token 123, down to its own workings and the token's index, embedding, and role — the analogue of \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\emph{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}}; the only gap is use/mention (mentioned exhaustively, never used). Yet when the word-piece itself first flows through mid-training, it genuinely updates: the predicted probability of that token was near zero, so the cross-entropy loss is large and the gradient step is large. A real, measurable gain on first encounter (the analogue of \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}}).
  \item \textbf{Y holds.} Every part of the episode is physical through and through: the prior knowledge is weights, the gain is a weight change, the trigger (token 123) is an integer / one-hot vector / voltage pattern. There is nowhere for a non-physical fact to hide.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{X ∧ Y contradicts X → ¬Y} (modus ponens would yield ¬Y, against Y). One genuine X∧Y instance therefore falsifies the conditional. So ``complete prior knowledge yet a gain on encounter'' does \textbf{not} entail non-physicality; the Knowledge Argument's inference is unsound \emph{as a general entailment}, and the parallel ``does LLMary prove token 123 is non-physical?'' makes the failure vivid — obviously it doesn't.

\textbf{What this does and does not show.} It does \textbf{not} establish Y (it is no argument that physicalism is true, nor that Mary's gain is physical). It does \textbf{not} pick a positive diagnosis of what Mary gains — it is consistent with the ability reading (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}}), the new-guise reading (\hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}}), and others, and commits to none. Its entire content is the negative point: \textbf{you cannot use the Mary argument to support anti-physicalism}, because the inference pattern it instantiates has a true-premise / false-conclusion counterexample. That is why this node \texttt{attacks} the \emph{argument}, not any ontological claim.

weakest premise: whether LLMary instantiates the \textbf{same X}. The realist resists by reading X phenomenally and charging equivocation on ``gain/learns'' (thought-experiment CQ-begs-the-question):

\begin{itemize}
  \item On an \textbf{access} reading of X — \emph{acquires a new information-bearing state / disposition despite complete description} — LLMary plainly satisfies X, and the conditional dies.
  \item On a \textbf{phenomenal} reading of X — \emph{comes to know what the encounter is like despite complete physical knowledge} — LLMary arguably fails X: it is a \emph{zombie learner}, all \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}}-update, nothing it is like to undergo it. So read, LLMary is not a counterexample but an irrelevant case.
\end{itemize}

This forces a dilemma rather than a refutation: either X is the access kind (then the conditional is false, as LLMary shows) or X is the phenomenal kind (then the Knowledge Argument owes an independent reason that Mary's encounter delivers phenomenal \emph{knowledge-that} — the very point in dispute). Either way the argument cannot stand \emph{as written}; the standoff relocates to whether there is a phenomenal residue over the access-update — i.e. back to the hard problem. The honest verdict is conditional: \emph{if} Mary's X is LLMary's X, the entailment fails; the burden is on the realist to show it is not, without leaning on the intuition under test.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\emph{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}} (RoboMary) because that attacks the \emph{Intuition} premise — a system with complete self-knowledge pre-computes the state and meets red with \textbf{no} update, so Mary learns nothing. LLMary instead \emph{grants} the update and attacks the \emph{conditional}: X∧Y is actual, so X→¬Y is false. The two computational variants pull on opposite premises. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\emph{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}} because that offers a \emph{positive analysis} of what Mary gains (abilities, not facts); LLMary offers no positive diagnosis at all — it is a neutral counterexample to the inference, compatible with the ability hypothesis but not asserting it.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary's famous argument has the shape of an \textbf{``if … then …``}: \emph{if} you knew every physical fact about colour and \emph{still} learned something new the first time you saw red, \emph{then} the world isn't fully physical (\hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}).

\textbf{LLMary} is a real-style case that breaks that ``if … then.'' It's an AI language model that has read every AI textbook and all of arXiv. One word-piece — ``token 123'' — never showed up in its training, though it knows everything \emph{written} about it. Then it meets token 123 for the first time and clearly \emph{does} learn: it was caught off guard (it expected that token almost never), so its internal numbers get a big correction. So here we have both halves at once: \emph{knew everything sayable} \textbf{and} \emph{still updated on first contact} — yet the whole thing is obviously, boringly physical (just numbers changing because of an input). Ask the Mary question of it — ``so is token 123 non-physical?'' — and the answer is plainly no.

One real case where both halves hold \textbf{and} it's all physical is enough to break the ``if … then.'' So the Mary move can't, by itself, prove the world isn't physical. Note what this does \emph{not} claim: it does \textbf{not} prove the world \emph{is} physical, and it doesn't say which story about Mary is right. It only takes away one argument.

The catch: LLMary feels nothing — nobody's home enjoying token 123. So a defender says the word ``learn'' is doing two jobs. LLMary ``learns'' only in the \emph{information/skill} sense; Mary supposedly also gets the \emph{feeling} of red, and that felt part is what was allegedly left out. So LLMary cleanly shows the information-update kind of learning is plain physics; whether there's an extra \emph{felt} kind in Mary's case is the original puzzle all over again (the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}} split). A sharp jab at the argument's logic — not a proof about reality.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs}{A lookup table can be bounded to cover every input a subject encounters in a lifetime}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{A lookup table can be bounded to cover every input a subject encounters in a lifetime}
gloss: the table need not be infinite. Stipulate it to store a response for every input string up to a length exceeding any the subject could actually receive in a finite lifetime (Block's ``Blockhead'' construction, \textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}). Within that bounded domain — the only domain the subject ever inhabits — the table has a stored answer for every input that arises.

scope: a claim about the \emph{coverage} of a suitably bounded table over the inputs a finite agent meets; it grants the table is finite. It does NOT assert the table is physically \emph{buildable} (that is denied by \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}}) — only that, as specified, its domain includes every input the subject encounters.

who would deny it: anyone pressing \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}} as the \emph{relevant} asymmetry — but Block's point is that inputs outside the lifetime bound are irrelevant to matching \emph{this} subject.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}} (the general separability of behaviour from organisation): this is the specific coverage move that defends it against the combinatorial-impossibility reply.

\begin{plainterms}
You don't need an infinite answer-table to mimic a person — just one big enough to hold a stored reply for every input they'd actually meet in a whole lifetime. Inputs longer or stranger than that never come up for them, so they don't matter. Within the range a real person ever lives through, the bounded table always has an answer ready. (This only claims the table \emph{covers} those inputs — not that it could ever be physically built, which is a separate, losing battle.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-lookup-table-possible}{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}
gloss: A finite stored map from input to canned response can match the input–output profile associated with a mind while its internals are a flat query→answer table — no mediating states, no processing (\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}, ``Psychologism and Behaviorism'' 1981).

scope: A possibility/existence claim about the \emph{separability of behaviour from internal organisation} in the pure-lookup case. It does NOT assert any actual system (an LLM) is a lookup table — that application is the contested bridge in \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} — and it explicitly concerns a system with NO internal organisation (contrast \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\emph{A population can realise a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its input-output behaviour}}, where the organisation is present).

who would deny it: those pressing the combinatorial-impossibility reply — that matching open-ended, context-sensitive behaviour by pure lookup would require an infinite table, so no finite lookup system really matches a mind's full profile.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\emph{A population can realise a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its input-output behaviour}} because that system reproduces the functional \emph{organisation}; this one reproduces only the \emph{behaviour}, by storage. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}} (which fixes the individuating grain): this only asserts the behaviour/organisation gap exists in the lookup case.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the bare claim that a flat stored answer-table — input in, canned reply out, nothing happening in between — could in principle match the outward behavior we associate with a mind, with zero real inner workings. It's a claim about \emph{possibility in principle}: it does \emph{not} say any actual AI is such a table (that's the contested leap), and it pictures a thing with no inner organization at all — unlike the flag-waving nation that copies a brain's full inner wiring (\hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\emph{A population can realise a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its input-output behaviour}}).
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable}{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}
gloss: because such a table needs \textasciitilde{}\texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n} entries (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}}) while the observable universe stores at most \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}120 bits (\hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\emph{The observable universe can store only a bounded, finite amount of information (on the order of 10\textasciicircum{}120 bits)}}), no lookup table matching a mind's (or an LLM's) behavioural profile over a realistic context could ever be physically built. The blockhead is not merely large but cosmically impossible.

scope: a claim about \emph{physical realizability in our universe}, NOT about logical or conceptual possibility. It therefore does not touch Block's intended point — that a \emph{possible} blockhead shows behaviour ≠ mind (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\emph{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}}). This applies a fortiori to Block's lifetime-bounded \emph{human} table (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\emph{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}}): richer inputs and a longer sequence make it even larger, so retreating to a bounded table does not rescue physical realizability. Its bite is on whether any \emph{actual} system (notably an LLM) could be a lookup table: it cannot. Since real LLMs produce the behaviour with \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}12 parameters rather than \textasciitilde{}10\textasciicircum{}9600 stored rows, they are demonstrably not lookup tables but compress/generalise — i.e. they process.

who would deny it: a defender of Blockhead who insists only conceptual possibility is at stake (for whom physical unrealizability is beside the point). The reply: granted for Block's conceptual claim; but the dialectically live worry against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}} was that an LLM might be a \emph{mere} lookup table, and that worry is what this defeats.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\emph{No finite lookup table can reproduce the full behavioural profile of a genuine mind}}: that says a finite table \emph{diverges} from the mind on out-of-domain inputs (a competence gap, which Block's bounded table escapes); this says the bounded table that would \emph{not} diverge is too large to \emph{exist} — a different ground of failure that survives the bounded-table rejoinder.

\begin{plainterms}
A stored answer-table big enough to match a real mind's (or a real AI's) behavior would need vastly more entries than there are particles to store them on — far more than the entire observable universe could hold. So such a table couldn't merely be hard to build; it couldn't exist at all, anywhere, ever. A sharper consequence: real AI models do their job with a comparatively tiny number of internal settings, so they plainly \emph{aren't} secret lookup tables — they must be genuinely computing. (It doesn't claim a lookup-table mind is logically impossible — only that no real one could physically exist.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-lookup-table-size-exponential}{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}
gloss: a flat query→answer table must store one row per \emph{distinguishable} input. For inputs that are strings of up to \texttt{n} tokens drawn from a vocabulary of size \texttt{v}, the number of distinct strings is on the order of \texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n}; matching the behaviour by pure lookup therefore needs \textasciitilde{}\texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n} rows. With LLM-scale figures (\texttt{v} ≈ 10\textasciicircum{}4–10\textasciicircum{}5, \texttt{n} ≈ 10\textasciicircum{}3–10\textasciicircum{}6) this is astronomically large, e.g. \texttt{50000\textasciicircum{}2048 ≈ 10\textasciicircum{}9600}. Block's own construction — a table bounded to a \emph{human lifetime} of inputs — is a fortiori larger still: sensory input is far more varied than a token vocabulary (larger \texttt{v}) and a lifetime vastly exceeds any LLM context window (larger \texttt{n}). So the bounded-table retreat makes the count worse, not better.

scope: a counting fact about \emph{pure} lookup over a bounded input length. It is exactly the bounded-domain table Block retreats to (\hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\emph{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}}), so it grants Block's finiteness move and measures its cost. It does NOT claim minds are infinite (cf. \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\emph{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}}, which is the \emph{un}bounded-input point).

who would deny it: one might object that many distinct contexts share a response, so the table could be \emph{compressed} below \texttt{v\textasciicircum{}n}. But a system that computes which equivalence-class an input falls into is no longer a flat lookup — it is doing the very processing the blockhead was meant to lack. So the exponential count is the size of a \emph{genuine} lookup table; compressing it concedes the point.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\emph{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}}: that says a finite table \emph{misses} some (longer) inputs; this measures the \emph{size} of the bounded table that misses none up to length \texttt{n}.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the arithmetic behind ``a cheat-sheet for a whole mind would be impossibly huge.'' To answer like a mind, a table needs a separate stored row for every distinct thing that could be said to it. Count those: the number of possible word-sequences is roughly the vocabulary size multiplied by itself once for every word-slot — which at AI scale lands around 10\textasciicircum{}9600 (a 1 followed by 9,600 zeros). Block's ``just cover one lifetime'' version is even bigger, since real sense-experience is richer and a life is long. And you can't shrink it by noticing that many inputs deserve the same reply — working out \emph{which} inputs share a reply is itself real computing, exactly what a pure lookup table was supposed to do without.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-lookup-table-objection}{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}
inference form: counterexample (an existential refutation of a sufficiency claim), driven by an intuition pump. Deductively: a single case with behaviour-but-no-mind refutes ``behaviour ⟹ mind.''

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}}) A lookup table can reproduce a mind's complete behavioural profile without the internal causal organisation of a mind.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}}) Such a system has no mental states. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\emph{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}}) Matching the behavioural profile is not sufficient for having the mental state.
\end{enumerate}

bearing on the target: this is the \texttt{responds\_to} argument that \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} itself named as the gap in its premise (2) (``a system displaying the full behavioural profile … is best explained by its realising that state's role``). If behaviour is compatible with both mind and no-mind, then displaying it is not \emph{best explained} by realising the role — so the abduction to \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}} is undercut. Hence the \texttt{attacks} on both the inference (\hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}}) and the conclusion it was meant to support (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}}).

scope of the threat (a property specific to the lookup table): because the table has \emph{no} internal organisation (\hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}}), it threatens only behaviourism and pure input–output functionalism. An organisational-role functionalist grants that it lacks mind and excludes it cheaply — the lookup table fails the organisational grain (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}). So this objection does NOT by itself bite an organisation-based view; the case that does is the Chinese Nation (\hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\emph{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}}), which \emph{has} the organisation.

weakest premise: not premise 1 or 2 (both widely granted, even by functionalists), but the \textbf{application to actual LLMs} — the tacit step that current LLMs are relevantly like a lookup table. The organisational-role functionalist denies exactly this: an LLM is not a flat query→answer map but has rich internal organisation, so it is excluded from the lookup-table class by the organisational grain (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\emph{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}}). The objection therefore lands against \emph{behaviour-as-sufficient} in general; its force against LLMs specifically turns on how lookup-table-like their internals are — the live crux.

provenance: Ned Block's lookup-table / ``Aunt Bubbles'' machine, a.k.a. ``Blockhead'' (\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}, ``Psychologism and Behaviorism'' 1981). The absent-qualia Chinese-Nation case is a \emph{separate} objection with different properties and replies — see \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\emph{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\emph{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}}: those deploy a China-brain against \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}, about the \emph{grain} of functional role. This deploys the lookup table against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}} / \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}}, about whether \emph{behaviour} is sufficient evidence for mind — a different target and conclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the ``cheat-sheet machine'' (\hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}}) put to work as an objection. The idea: you could, in principle, build something that produces all of a mind's behavior just by looking answers up in a giant pre-written table — right outputs, but nobody actually thinking inside. If that's even possible, then matching behavior can't be \emph{enough} to guarantee a mind, because here's a case with the behavior and (intuitively) no mind. So behavior on its own is shakier evidence than it looked. Two things worth noticing. First, the punch only really lands on views that judge minds by behavior \emph{alone}; a view that also demands the right inner workings just shrugs — ``of course the cheat-sheet lacks those'' — and moves on. Second, the live argument about today's AI isn't whether a pure lookup table has a mind (everyone agrees it doesn't), but whether real language models are \emph{like} a lookup table inside, or are richly organized in the way a mind is. That's the open question.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-lookup-table-mind}{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}
primary definition: a system that produces a mind's behaviour by consulting a finite stored map from input to canned response — the right outputs, but no internal processing: no inference, no mediating causal states, no organisation beyond the table. Ned Block's ``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine (\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}, ``Psychologism and Behaviorism'' 1981).

defining property (what distinguishes it from \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}}): the lookup table \emph{lacks} internal functional organisation altogether — it is a flat query→answer map. It therefore matches only the input–output (behavioural) profile, not the causal/transition structure of a mind.

the dispute it feeds: whether matching behaviour is sufficient for, or good evidence of, mind (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\emph{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\emph{The lookup-table (blockhead) objection — behaviour is not sufficient for mind}} — attacking \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}}).

characteristic counter-arguments (note, distinct from the Chinese Nation's): (i) a \emph{combinatorial impossibility} reply — for open-ended, context-sensitive behaviour the table would have to be not merely huge but infinite, so no finite lookup system actually matches a mind's full profile; (ii) it is \emph{cheaply excluded by organisational-role functionalism} (\hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}}) precisely because it has no internal organisation — so it threatens only behaviourism / pure input-output functionalism, not an organisation-based view. Contrast \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}}, which \emph{has} the organisation and so is not so excluded.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture a machine that acts exactly like a person but pulls it off by cheating: for every possible thing you could say to it, someone has pre-written the perfect reply in a gigantic table, and the machine just looks up your input and reads the stored answer back. No thinking, no reasoning, no inner steps — just an enormous answer key. That's the ``lookup-table mind'' (Block's ``Blockhead``). It's a thought-experiment troublemaker: it would sail through any test that judges minds purely by behavior, yet intuitively there's nobody in there. So it sharpens the question — is producing the right behavior \emph{enough} to count as having a mind, or could you get all the behavior with nobody home? (Two standard replies: to really keep up with a real, open-ended mind the table would have to be impossibly, near-infinitely large; and unlike a brain it has no inner organization at all, so it only embarrasses theories that judge minds by behavior alone.)
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{M}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-mary-misimagined}{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Dennett}{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}
Raises thought-experiment CQ2 (verdict) against \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}, in its strongest form: the intuition premise \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}} is an artefact of how the scenario is framed — the verdict is generated by an imagined case \emph{other than} the one stipulated.

inference form: debunking (an undercutting defeater for the intuition premise), plus a counter-image.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The scenario premise \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\emph{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}} stipulates \emph{complete} physical knowledge — a condition no one can concretely imagine (we can imagine, at best, ``Mary knows an awful lot``).
  \item The intuitive verdict ``of course she'd be surprised by the tomato'' is generated by the imaginable surrogate, and it is \emph{true of the surrogate}: vast-but-incomplete knowledge would indeed leave her something to learn.
  \item An intuition that tracks the surrogate case carries no evidential weight about the stipulated case (Dennett 1991: judging the case means honouring the stipulation, not one's image of it).
  \item Counter-image (RoboMary, Dennett 2005): a robot that knows the complete functional story of its own colour-vision can compute the total state seeing-red would put it into, put itself into that state, and meet the first red object with no update at all — complete self-knowledge already contains the ``what it's like``. ∴ \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-nothing-new]{\emph{Mary, genuinely knowing all the physical facts, would learn nothing on first seeing red}} — taken at its own stipulation the case yields no learning, so \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}} loses its intuition premise rather than its bridge.
\end{enumerate}

Compared with the neighbouring replies (\hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\emph{The Ability Hypothesis — experience teaches know-how, so Mary learns no new fact}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-acquaintance-hypothesis]{\emph{The Acquaintance Hypothesis — Mary meets the experience rather than learning a truth}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}}): those concede the verdict and dispute its interpretation; this one refuses the verdict, so it owes no account of what Mary gains — its burden is instead the credibility of premises (3) and (4).

weakest premise: (4), and through it the position's bite. RoboMary \emph{pre-instantiates} the seeing-red state using her self-knowledge — which a critic reads as conceding the point: she comes to know what red is like \emph{by putting herself into the state}, i.e. by having (an internal copy of) the experience, not by deducing it from the physical story. If pre-instantiating is the only route in, the physical facts alone did not suffice after all, and the case quietly re-enacts Mary's walk outside. Premise (3) is also contested: stipulations that outrun imaginability arguably drain a thought experiment of evidential force in \emph{both} directions — pressed hard, this debunking threatens the method of cases generally (CQ3, trained-domain, cuts against the debunker too).

\begin{plainterms}
The Mary story (\hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}) leans on one gut reaction: \emph{of course} she's surprised when she finally sees red. This reply says the gut reaction is aimed at the wrong story. Nobody can truly picture someone who knows \emph{every} physical fact — so we secretly picture someone who merely knows a lot, and \emph{that} person would of course be surprised. But the story's whole force depends on ``every fact,'' and taken literally, ``every fact'' plausibly includes whatever it takes to see the tomato coming. Dennett's robot version: a machine that fully understands its own vision could set its circuits into the exact ``seeing red'' state in advance — first sight of red, no surprise. Critics answer that the robot only manages this by \emph{giving itself the experience}, which is exactly the thing book-learning alone was supposed to provide.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-mary-omniscience-unrealizable}{Mary's complete physical knowledge is unrealizable — an embedded knower cannot encode the total state that contains her}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Mary's complete physical knowledge is unrealizable — an embedded knower cannot encode the total state that contains her}
Raises thought-experiment CQ1 (coherence) against \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}: the scenario premise \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\emph{Before leaving the room, Mary knows all the physical facts about colour vision}} is not an innocent idealisation but a stipulation no physically embedded knower could satisfy — its own body already flags this critical question; here it is cashed out. The bound invoked is the web's finite self-model result and its physical sharpening, Breuer's self-measurement theorem (\textsc{Thomas Breuer — The Impossibility of Accurate State Self-Measurements (Philosophy of Science, 1995)}).

inference form: deductive — a reductio of the scenario premise, by application of an independent impossibility result.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item ``All the physical facts'' includes the facts about Mary's own present brain state — that is, the total present state of a physical system (Mary plus her environment, ultimately the physical world) in which Mary is properly contained.
  \item For Mary to \emph{know} those facts, her physical state must encode them; her representation of the world is part of the very state to be represented (\hyperref[entry:claim-self-model-is-part-of-state]{\emph{A finite system's self-representation is part of the very state it represents}}).
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\emph{No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself}}) No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself; in measurement-theoretic form (\textsc{Thomas Breuer — The Impossibility of Accurate State Self-Measurements (Philosophy of Science, 1995)}): no observer can distinguish all present states of a system in which they are contained — classical or quantum, deterministic or stochastic. ∴ No possible physical Mary satisfies the stipulation: ``Mary knows all the physical facts'' describes no realizable situation, so the intuitive verdict about her release is harvested from an impossible case, and the argument's evidential force is correspondingly void — at best it reasons from a \emph{counterpossible} (``had the impossible obtained, she would still have learned …``), whose intuitive evaluation is unreliable.
\end{enumerate}

Dialectical placement. Complementary to \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\emph{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}}: that move says the verdict is generated by \emph{misimagining} the stipulated case; this one says there is no coherent case to imagine — the surrogate is all there ever was. Kin in spirit to \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\emph{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}} (a realizability attack on a canonical set piece); and if the proponent retreats from realizability to mere conceivability, the standing wedge \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\emph{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}} applies — a coherent \emph{description} does not certify a possible \emph{case} (the lesson of \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\emph{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}}). Note this attack does not defend physicalism directly; it denies the thought experiment standing to refute it.

weakest premise: (1), via the \textbf{type-retreat}. The proponent can weaken the stipulation: Mary need not encode the world's (or her own) total \emph{token} state, only all \emph{general} physical truths — the laws plus the type-level facts about colour vision — and what red is like, if it is any physical fact, is plausibly a type fact, not a fact about Mary's momentary microstate. The self-measurement bound forbids complete token self-description; it is silent on type-omniscience, so the reductio passes the retreated premise by. A second escape targets (2)'s reading of ``knows``: Mary's knowledge could be dispositional and externally stored — a library she can consult sequentially rather than a simultaneous internal encoding — and the bound constrains only \emph{simultaneous, internal, lossless} self-representation. The attack therefore bites hardest on the maximal, literal reading of ``knows all the physical facts``, which is also the reading the Knowledge Argument's rhetoric leans on; what survives the retreat is a weaker premise whose sufficiency for the famous intuition is no longer obvious (at which point the verdict question re-opens — \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\emph{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}} presses exactly there).

\begin{plainterms}
The Mary story (\hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}) starts: ``Mary knows every physical fact.'' This objection says that opening line is like ``consider a map so detailed it shows everything — including itself, at full detail``: it sounds fine but describes something impossible. Mary's brain is part of the physical world, so ``every physical fact'' includes the full present state of her own brain — and a part can't hold a complete copy of the whole that contains it (a theorem, not a hunch: physicist Thomas Breuer proved no measuring device can pin down the complete state of a system it sits inside). If the story's premise can't be true in any possible world, our gut feeling about what would happen ``if it were'' is testimony about nothing. The escape for the story's defenders: maybe Mary only needs the general laws and facts about colour vision \emph{in general}, not a snapshot of her own atoms — though then the premise is weaker than the dramatic ``she knew \emph{everything}``, and it's less clear the famous intuition still follows (a related objection, that we misimagine what complete knowledge would be — \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\emph{The Mary intuition misimagines the premise — ``all physical facts'' is quietly read as ``lots of facts''}} — takes over from there).
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-mary-learns-nothing-new}{Mary, genuinely knowing all the physical facts, would learn nothing on first seeing red}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Dennett}{Mary, genuinely knowing all the physical facts, would learn nothing on first seeing red}
gloss: the hard-line reply (Dennett, \emph{Consciousness Explained} 1991; ``What RoboMary Knows'' 2005). If Mary really knew \emph{all} the physical facts — not merely an enormous amount — she would already know exactly what seeing red would be like, and her first tomato would hold no surprise: ``and there it is, just as I expected.'' The intuition to the contrary only measures our inability to imagine truly complete physical knowledge.

scope: a direct denial of \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}} — the only standard reply that rejects the datum rather than reinterpreting it. It does not claim any actual human could achieve Mary's knowledge; it claims the thought experiment, taken at its own stipulation, fails to deliver the verdict it advertises.

who would deny it: nearly everyone else in the debate — the Knowledge-Argument proponent and the ability (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}}), acquaintance (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}}), and old-fact/new-guise (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a new phenomenal concept of a physical fact she already knew}}) theorists all grant that release changes Mary's epistemic situation somehow. Denying even that is widely felt to bite a very hard bullet, which is why this claim's defenders insist the bullet is an artefact of misimagining the scenario.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a new phenomenal concept of a physical fact she already knew}} because that grants Mary new (guise-level) knowledge; this grants her nothing — on this view there is no residual datum for the other replies to explain.

\begin{plainterms}
Every other answer to the Mary puzzle agrees she gets \emph{something} when she first sees red, and argues about what. This answer says: nothing. We picture Mary knowing a huge amount and rightly sense that a huge amount wouldn't tell her what red looks like. But the story stipulates she knows \emph{everything} physical — an unimaginably stronger condition — and everything means everything, including whatever a brain needs in order to anticipate, in full, its own first sight of red. If the story is taken at its word, the tomato is no surprise; the feeling that it must be one just shows we can't really imagine knowing it all.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind}{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}
gloss: Since a lookup table can match the whole behavioural profile (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\emph{A lookup table can reproduce a mind's behavioural profile without any internal causal organisation}}) yet lack mind (\hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\emph{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}}), behaviour cannot be sufficient for mental states: the profile is silent on the internal organisation that does the individuating work.

scope: A claim about \emph{sufficiency}, and thereby about evidential force: if behaviour is compatible with both mind and no-mind, then behaviour alone does not settle the matter and is at best defeasible evidence requiring a story about internals. It does NOT assert behaviour is \emph{no} evidence, nor that any particular system lacks mind.

who would deny it: a pure-input-output (machine-table) functionalist or behaviourist for whom the profile just is the having of the state.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} (about whether substrate matters) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\emph{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}} (about which grain individuates): this is the prior, more general point that the \emph{behavioural} grain is too coarse to be sufficient at all.

\begin{plainterms}
Acting exactly like something with a mind isn't enough to \emph{guarantee} there's a mind. The reason: you could in principle get all the right behavior out of a giant pre-stored answer table with nobody actually thinking inside — so matching behavior leaves the real question (what's going on internally) wide open. This doesn't say behavior is \emph{no} evidence; it says behavior can't settle the matter by itself.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-cognitive-closure}{McGinn's cognitive-closure argument for mysterianism}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{McGinn's cognitive-closure argument for mysterianism}
inference form: eliminative / transcendental — rule out the only routes by which we could grasp the property, given that one exists.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\emph{There is a natural property of the brain that explains consciousness}}) There is some natural property P of the brain in virtue of which it gives rise to consciousness.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\emph{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}}) A mind can be constitutively closed to certain properties.
  \item We could form the concept of P only by \emph{introspection} or by \emph{outer perception} of the brain (and inference from it). [body premise — the exhaustive-routes assumption]
  \item Introspection presents experience but not its neural basis; perception of the brain yields only spatial/physical concepts, which cannot capture the subjective (the explanatory gap, \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}). So neither route reaches P. [body premise] ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\emph{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}}) P is cognitively closed to us: the mind-body problem is soluble in itself but insoluble by us. This is the support for \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism (transcendental naturalism)}}.
\end{enumerate}

weakest premise: (3)–(4), the closure of the two routes. Replies: (a) science routinely forms \emph{theoretical} concepts that are neither introspected nor perceived (charge, spacetime curvature) by abduction and conceptual innovation — so the two-route dichotomy is too narrow, and McGinn under-rates concept-creation; (b) we cannot reliably know our own representational limits \emph{in advance}, so the confident verdict of closure is itself suspect; (c) if the explanatory gap is \emph{merely} cognitive closure, that tells against reading it as metaphysical residue — so mysterianism and the residue view (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}) cannot both take the gap at face value.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}: that treats the gap as evidence about \emph{metaphysics} (the phenomenal is not exhausted by role); this treats the very same gap as evidence about \emph{our cognitive limits} — the problem is real and natural, just humanly unsolvable. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\emph{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\emph{The structure-and-dynamics argument — function omits the categorical/intrinsic}}, which argue function \emph{omits} or \emph{fails to fix} the phenomenal; mysterianism grants a natural answer exists and denies only our access to it.

\begin{plainterms}
The argument goes: (1) there's some real, physical reason the brain produces consciousness; (2) minds can be permanently unable to understand certain things; (3) the only ways we could ever get hold of that reason are by looking inward (introspection) or by studying the brain from outside; (4) but looking inward shows you the feeling, not the wiring, and studying the brain from outside only ever gives you physical/spatial facts that never add up to a feeling. So neither route can deliver the answer — meaning it's locked away from us for good. The main pushback: science invents concepts all the time that we neither feel nor see directly (like ``electron``), so maybe the two routes aren't the only ones.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation}{A mental state is conscious only if it is the object of a higher-order representation}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Rosenthal}{A mental state is conscious only if it is the object of a higher-order representation}
gloss: a first-order mental state is \emph{conscious} in virtue of being represented by a suitable higher-order state (\hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs higher-order mental states}}) — a higher-order thought (Rosenthal) or higher-order perception (Lycan); on Carruthers' variant the higher-order content is dispositional. What it is for a state to be conscious is thus a relational, representational fact, not an intrinsic glow.

scope: a reductive account of \emph{state} consciousness (what makes a state conscious), naturally read as a theory of \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}}'s access side. It does not by itself settle the hard problem; critics say it explains availability, not felt character.

who would deny it: first-order representationalists (consciousness needs no higher-order state); and phenomenal realists who hold felt character is intrinsic, not constituted by being represented.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\emph{A state is conscious only if its content is globally broadcast to the brain's specialist systems}} (consciousness = global availability, not a dedicated higher-order representation) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\emph{Phenomenal character is identical to representational content}} (first-order representational content).

\entryhead{claim-substrate-independence}{Mental states are substrate-independent}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, after Putnam}{Mental states are substrate-independent}
If mental states are individuated by functional role (\hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}}), then any system realising the role has the state, regardless of whether it is carbon- or silicon-based.

Scope: a claim about \emph{mental states generally}. It does NOT by itself assert that any actual LLM realises the relevant roles — that is the separate work of arg-behavioral-parity. Who denies it: substrate chauvinists; see \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}.

Distinct from a stronger claim ``silicon systems are conscious'' — substrate-independence is the weaker enabling premise, not the conclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
Could a mind run on something other than a brain? This claim says: yes, at least in principle. The reasoning — if what makes a mental state the state it is, is the \emph{role} it plays (what causes it, what it goes on to cause), then anything that plays that role has that state, no matter what it's built from: carbon, silicon, whatever. Notice how modest this is. It does \emph{not} say today's computers or chatbots actually have minds. It only says that being made of brain-stuff isn't, all by itself, a requirement. It unlocks the door; it doesn't claim anyone has walked through.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-zombie-conceivable}{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}
gloss: the scenario/intuition premise of the zombie argument. A being identical to a conscious person in every microphysical and functional respect, but with no phenomenal consciousness (\hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}}), is \emph{ideally conceivable} — coherently and stably imaginable, with no hidden contradiction revealed on ideal rational reflection.

scope: a claim about \emph{conceivability}, not yet possibility. It does not by itself assert zombies are metaphysically possible (that needs the bridge, \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}}). The qualifier ``ideal'' matters: the claim is not about what is easy to picture but about what survives idealised reflection.

who would deny it: type-A physicalists (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}) and illusionists, who hold that on full reflection the zombie description \emph{is} incoherent (there is no further phenomenal fact to subtract), so it is not genuinely conceivable at all — only apparently so.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}} (the modal bridge) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} (a thesis about role-individuation): this asserts only the coherent imaginability of the absent-consciousness duplicate.

\entryhead{claim-soul-is-form-of-body}{The mind is the form of a living body}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Aristotle}{The mind is the form of a living body}
gloss: the soul/mind is the \emph{form} (the organising principle, actuality) of a living organism, not a separate substance lodged in it nor merely its matter — as the shape of a statue is neither a second thing beside the bronze nor the bronze itself (Aristotle, \emph{De Anima}; Aquinas; modern hylomorphists Jaworski, Marmodoro). Mind and body are the form and matter of one substance, the living animal.

scope: a \emph{hylomorphic} metaphysics, positioned against both Cartesian substance dualism (no separate mental substance) and reductive materialism (the organism is not just its matter). It treats mental capacities as form-level features of a suitably organised living thing.

who would deny it: substance dualists (mind is a distinct substance); reductive physicalists (form is nothing over the material facts); and those who reject Aristotelian form/matter metaphysics as a framework.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}}: although ``form ≈ organisation'' sounds functionalist, the hylomorphist ties form to a \emph{living} substance with intrinsic teleology, not to a substrate-neutral causal role — so it does not straightforwardly underwrite \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}.

\entryhead{claim-role-grain-is-organisational}{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}
gloss: The functional role by which a mental state is individuated is specified neither at the coarse grain of gross input-output behaviour nor at the fine grain of the physical realiser's microchemistry, but at the \emph{intermediate} grain of the system's internal counterfactual-causal organisation — the structure of state-to-state transitions among its components. Physical details (spike timing, neuromodulator diffusion, transistor physics) matter to the role only insofar as they make a counterfactual difference to that transition-structure.

scope: A claim about the \emph{correct grain of role-individuation} for functionalism, not about whether any particular system meets it. It asserts that this grain is (a) substrate-neutral, so multiply realisable in silicon, and (b) demanding of internal organisation, so not satisfied by mere input-output mimicry. It does NOT by itself assert that any LLM or the blockhead meets or fails it; those are downstream applications.

who would deny it: defenders of pure input-output (machine-table / behaviourist) functionalism; fine-grained chauvinists who hold the biologically specific microstructure is individuative; anyone who holds the grain dilemma (\hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\emph{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}}) is exhaustive between coarse and biological-fine.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}} because that concept leaves the grain of ``role'' open (it lists machine-state, role, and commonsense senses); this claim \emph{fixes} the grain at one of those options and motivates the choice. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} because that asserts substrate does no individuative work; this asserts \emph{which level of organisation} does the work instead — it is the specification that, if true, secures substrate-independence against the grain dilemma.

\begin{plainterms}
This picks the ``just right'' level of detail for defining a mind.
