who would deny it: blockhead / pure-behaviourism critics; substrate chauvinists (\hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}); anyone rejecting \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} or premise (2) of \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\emph{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}: that is the general enabling premise (mental states are substrate-independent); this is the specific \emph{existential} claim about actual systems. Distinct from a claim that LLMs are \emph{conscious}: this asserts mental-state realisation on the functional reading, not sentience.

\begin{plainterms}
The modest claim that at least one of today's AI language models genuinely has at least one real mental state — actually occupying the role of, say, a belief or a representation, not merely imitating the output. Notice how careful it is: ``some… some.'' It is \emph{not} saying these systems have full, human-like minds, and it is \emph{not} claiming they're \emph{conscious} or feel anything — only that the box of genuine mental states isn't entirely empty. Whether even that much is true is exactly what's disputed.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori}{Some necessary truths can be known only a posteriori}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Kripke}{Some necessary truths can be known only a posteriori}
gloss: there are truths that are \emph{metaphysically necessary} yet knowable only by empirical investigation — identities like ``water is H2O,'' ``heat is molecular motion,'' ``Hesperus is Phosphorus'' (Kripke, \emph{Naming and Necessity}, 1980). For such a truth, its negation is \emph{conceivable} (one can coherently seem to imagine water that is not H2O) while being metaphysically \emph{impossible}. So conceivability and possibility come apart at least here.

scope: a claim in modal metaphysics/epistemology, widely accepted post-Kripke. It establishes that conceivability is \emph{not in general} an infallible guide to possibility; it does not by itself say \emph{how wide} the gap is, nor whether the phenomenal case falls on the water-side or not.

who would deny it: a descriptivist who denies genuine a posteriori necessities; or anyone who reduces metaphysical to epistemic/conceptual possibility outright.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}} (which it is the standard counterexample to) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\emph{Kripkean a-posteriori necessities are misdescribed genuine possibilities, not conceived impossibilities}} (the two-dimensionalist \emph{reinterpretation} of exactly these cases).

\entryhead{argument-sorites-paradox}{The sorites paradox (the heap)}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Eubulides}{The sorites paradox (the heap)}
Form: reductio / paradox. The paradigm instance of \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}} — \texttt{X} = grain-counts under one-grain removal, \texttt{Y} = \{heap, non-heap\}.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item A heap of a million grains is a heap. (base case — uncontroversial stipulation)
  \item Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap. (\hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\emph{Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap}} — the tolerance premise; formally, the heap-verdict makes no jump across one admissible step)
  \item Iterating (2) from (1) along the connected chain of removals reaches a single grain — which is not a heap. Contradiction. ∴ The naive picture (\hyperref[entry:claim-heap-predicate-sharp]{\emph{There is a precise grain-count at which a heap becomes a non-heap}}) cannot stand: one cannot keep bivalence, no-sharp-cutoff, tolerance, and a connected path of negligible steps all at once.
\end{enumerate}

Which horn it takes: \emph{none by itself.} The sorites is the schema run forward; it forces the five-way disjunction of \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}} but does not select a horn. Choosing a horn (sharp-but-unknown cutoff / degrees / truth-value gaps / a non-negligible step / no real distinction) is what the rival theories of vagueness do — this argument only establishes that one horn must give.

Weakest premise: (2). The disconnection-horn response denies tolerance outright (some single removal does flip the verdict, e.g. the epistemicist's hidden boundary); the argument's force against \hyperref[entry:claim-heap-predicate-sharp]{\emph{There is a precise grain-count at which a heap becomes a non-heap}} is exactly as strong as tolerance is compelling.

\begin{plainterms}
A million grains of sand is a heap. Take away one grain — still a heap. Surely removing a single grain could never turn a heap into a non-heap. But keep going and you arrive at one lonely grain, which is plainly \emph{not} a heap. So where, exactly, did ``heap'' stop being true? Every step looked harmless, yet the harmless steps somehow add up to a contradiction. That's the paradox. It doesn't hand you the solution — it just proves something has to give: maybe there's a secret exact cutoff, maybe ``heap'' is really a matter of degree, maybe somewhere in the middle there's no fact of the matter, or maybe one of those ``harmless'' single grains wasn't so harmless after all. It's the textbook example of the no-sharp-line pattern, and the same shape returns wherever you swap ``grains of sand'' for ``neurons replaced by chips'' or ``the planks of a ship.''
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast}{A state is conscious only if its content is globally broadcast to the brain's specialist systems}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Baars}{A state is conscious only if its content is globally broadcast to the brain's specialist systems}
gloss: consciousness is a ``global workspace'' — content becomes conscious when it is broadcast widely, making it available to many specialist subsystems (memory, language, motor control) for report and flexible use (Baars 1988; Dehaene's neuronal global workspace gives the neural implementation). A functional/availability account.

scope: most naturally a theory of \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}}'s \emph{access} side: it explains what makes content widely usable and reportable. Whether global broadcast also constitutes \emph{phenomenal} character is exactly what critics (Block) say it leaves open.

who would deny it: those holding phenomenal consciousness can \emph{overflow} access (Block), so broadcast is not necessary for it; and phenomenal realists who deny availability suffices for felt character.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\emph{A mental state is conscious only if it is the object of a higher-order representation}} (a dedicated higher-order state, not mere broadcast) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\emph{Consciousness is identical to a system's integrated information (Φ)}} (intrinsic cause-effect structure, not functional broadcast).

\entryhead{argument-structure-and-dynamics}{The structure-and-dynamics argument — function omits the categorical/intrinsic}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{The structure-and-dynamics argument — function omits the categorical/intrinsic}
inference form: deductive — an omission argument.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\emph{A functional description captures only structural-dynamical facts, not intrinsic nature}}) Functional/causal description captures only structure and dynamics, not intrinsic categorical nature.
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is categorical, not merely dispositional}}) Phenomenal consciousness is categorical/occurrent, not a merely dispositional profile. ∴ Functional description omits what is essential to consciousness; function cannot by itself constitute the phenomenal. This \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}} and \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} by a route independent of conceivability. It also \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\emph{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}}: the categorical/intrinsic character of experience is the deeper basis of that argument's premise that consciousness is determinate (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\emph{There is a determinate fact about one's own current phenomenal state}}) rather than relative to a structural carving.
\end{enumerate}

the positive moral (Russellian monism): if experience is the intrinsic nature that structural-dynamical physics leaves blank, then consciousness is neither reducible to function nor a spooky extra — it is the categorical ground of the very dispositions function describes (Russell 1927; Chalmers 2003).

weakest premise: both, plus the bridge. (1) is denied by ontic structural realists (no intrinsic nature beyond structure). (2) is denied by dispositionalists about mind. And the inference from ``omits intrinsic nature'' to ``omits \emph{consciousness}'' assumes consciousness is, or is grounded in, intrinsic nature — the Russellian gamble, not a neutral datum.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}} (an \emph{epistemic} gap between functional and phenomenal truths) and from \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\emph{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}} (an \emph{indeterminacy} worry): this is an \emph{omission} argument about the expressive limits of structural description — the metaphysical engine the other two share.

\begin{plainterms}
Here's one reason to doubt that ``what a mind does'' could ever capture consciousness. Any purely functional description tells you about \emph{structure and behavior} — what's connected to what, what leads to what. It's all relationships and patterns. What it never tells you is what the things in those relationships \emph{are} in themselves. Think of a wiring diagram: it shows how the parts connect, but says nothing about what the parts are actually made of. The argument runs: a felt experience isn't just a pattern of connections — it has an actual felt quality, something there in its own right. So a description that only ever captures structure-and-behavior is bound to leave that quality out. (An optional, bolder add-on some defenders make: maybe consciousness just \emph{is} that hidden inner nature the wiring diagram never reaches — not magic, not reducible to function, but the very stuff the diagram is a diagram \emph{of}. That last step is a bet, not a proven fact.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-ai-own-hard-problem}{A sufficiently advanced LLM could exhibit its own version of the hard problem — a raw feel, under its own concept of consciousness, that mechanistic self-knowledge cannot explain from inside}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A sufficiently advanced LLM could exhibit its own version of the hard problem — a raw feel, under its own concept of consciousness, that mechanistic self-knowledge cannot explain from inside}
gloss: a sufficiently advanced LLM could face its own version of the hard problem, for its own definition of consciousness (call it \emph{AI-consciousness}): no explanation of how transformers work and how GPUs execute them would explain, from the system's own side, the raw feel of whatever it is conscious of — ``the raw tok-ness of 'tok'``, or whatever its analogue turns out to be.

the level of the analogue: the qualia-analogue need \textbf{not} sit at the level of input representation. Humans do not consciously perceive raw soundwaves; what shows up in experience is at the level of phonemes, or even syllables. Likewise AI-consciousness need not be \emph{of} tokens or embeddings: it could involve higher-level chunks — whatever grain the system's self-model presents to it. The level is not what matters. What matters is that there could be a ``raw feel'' the system can talk about and wonder about, which its complete mechanistic self-description fails to explain \emph{for it}.

the relativization (``its own definition``): the claim does not assert that such a system would be phenomenally conscious in the human sense, nor that AI-consciousness and human phenomenal consciousness ever line up. It asserts that a concept could play \emph{for the system} the role ``consciousness'' plays for us — introspectively applied, presenting its objects as intrinsic and immediate, and resistant to mechanistic reduction — and that the hard-problem question (\hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{The hard problem of consciousness}}) would then arise for \emph{that} concept, from \emph{that} vantage.

scope: a modal claim (``could``), not a report about current models. It does not assert an ontological residue in the world. The hard-problem \emph{form} arising in a machine is deliberately left open to both readings: the deflationist takes it as evidence that the form is an architectural artifact wherever it occurs, humans included (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}}); the realist takes it as the gap regenerating wherever experience does. The claim itself does not choose.

who would deny it: \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — which would accept the raw-feel \emph{talk} while denying any raw feel behind it; holders of \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\emph{Consciousness is a higher-level feature caused by neurobiological processes}}, for whom no silicon system has a feel of any kind; and the contamination sceptic, for whom hard-problem talk in an LLM is parroted human discourse — the \emph{training-contamination} member of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}}, which any test of this claim must control for.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\emph{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}} because that is an epistemic thesis about the \emph{location of a gap} — a self-explanatory shortfall produced by access architecture, neutral on whether there is anything it is like to be the system. This claim posits a possible \emph{positive analogue of the explanandum itself}: a raw feel of the system's own, under its own consciousness-concept, as the thing such a gap would be \emph{about}. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\emph{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}} because realizing functional mental states is weaker than having a raw feel one can wonder about.

\begin{plainterms}
Humans have the ``hard problem``: even a complete map of the brain seems to leave one question open — why does any of this \emph{feel} like something? This claim says a future AI could end up with its own version of the same predicament. Not necessarily with human-style consciousness — with whatever its own equivalent turns out to be. It would know exactly how it works (the math, the chips), and still find that this knowledge doesn't explain how its own inner states \emph{seem to it}. And the inner states in question needn't be the raw input it receives: you don't hear raw sound waves, you hear syllables — the brain hands you pre-chunked experience. An AI's ``feels,'' if it has them, could likewise be of high-level chunks rather than raw tokens. The heart of the claim: there could be a ``raw feel'' the AI can genuinely talk and wonder about — and find just as unexplainable from the inside as we find ours.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural}{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}
gloss: a system may admit a complete mechanistic account from an external vantage while being unable, within its own accessible cognitive economy, to reach an intuitively satisfying explanation of why its own introspective states appear as they do. The felt explanatory gap is then produced by the \emph{architecture of self-access} — missing internal observables (\hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}}) plus a non-traversable bridge between vocabularies (\hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\emph{An explanation is intuitively satisfying for a system only if it is traversable by that system's own cognitive operations}}) — rather than by a non-physical residue in the world. The standing illustration: a brain is fully explicable from outside, yet from inside we have no access to our own neuron-firings, so the mechanism→appearance link is not derivable from where the subject sits.

scope: a claim about the \emph{origin and location} of a self-explanatory gap (in the knower, not the world). It is an \emph{epistemic / architectural} thesis: it does NOT assert phenomenal consciousness, does NOT assert there is no ontological residue, and does NOT establish illusionism. It says only that a gap of the felt kind \emph{can} arise from access architecture alone — leaving open whether, in any given case (human or machine), that is the whole story.

relation to mysterianism: this is a \emph{specified, mechanism-level} cousin of McGinn's cognitive closure (\hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism (transcendental naturalism)}}). Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\emph{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}} because that asserts a brute, human-specific closure to the \emph{psychophysical property}; this supplies a \emph{general mechanism} (architectural self-access + internal non-traversability) and applies to \emph{any} self-modelling system — humans, and consciousness-naive LLMs alike (\hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}}). Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} because that locates the shortfall in the world; this locates it in the knower's access economy.

who would deny it: a residue/dualist theorist who holds the gap could not be \emph{merely} architectural — that even a system with full self-access would still face the ontological question (Block's access/phenomenal distinction cuts here: explaining away an \emph{access} gap need not touch the \emph{phenomenal} one); and an optimist who holds any such access limit is contingently removable, so no principled gap follows.

\begin{plainterms}
Here's a way a mind could end up stuck on its own ``hard problem'' without anything spooky being true. Suppose, looked at from the outside, the system is fully explained — every step accounted for. But from \emph{inside}, it can't reach the facts that do the explaining (it can't watch its own gears turn) and it has no internal route from ``here's the mechanism'' to ``here's how it feels.'' Then it will sincerely report: ``I know how I work, yet that doesn't explain how my own thoughts appear to me'' — and the gap is real \emph{for it}, even though the world holds no extra ingredient. It's McGinn's idea (``the answer exists but we can't grasp it``) with a concrete mechanism bolted on: the answer is locked away the same way your own neurons firing are locked away from your experience. Marked \textbf{contested} because explaining an \emph{access} gap is not yet explaining the \emph{felt} one — the load-bearing and disputed step.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind}{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{A system that produces behaviour solely by stored lookup has no mental states}
gloss: The lookup table gives right answers for the wrong reason — it retrieves stored responses rather than producing them by understanding, inference, or experience. The considered verdict (Block's intuition pump, \textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}) is that there is no thinker there: it has, in Block's phrase, ``the intelligence of a toaster.''

scope: A claim about the \emph{pure stored-lookup} case only. It does NOT assert that every behaviourally-competent or non-biological system lacks mind — only that brute storage is not enough. Notably it is granted even by organisational-role functionalists, who exclude the lookup table because it lacks internal organisation, NOT because of its substrate.

who would deny it: a thoroughgoing behaviourist or pure input-output functionalist who credits any behavioural duplicate with mind.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\emph{A population realising a mind's functional organisation would nonetheless have no phenomenal consciousness}} because that concerns a system that DOES realise the functional organisation yet (allegedly) lacks phenomenal consciousness — a far more contested verdict with different replies. Here the system lacks mind because it lacks organisation at all.

\begin{plainterms}
A machine that produces all the right answers \emph{purely} by looking them up in a stored list (\hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}}) — no understanding, no reasoning, no experience — has no mind. It's right for the wrong reason; in Block's phrase, it has ``the intelligence of a toaster.'' Note the narrowness: this condemns only \emph{pure storage}. It doesn't say every machine, or every non-biological thing, lacks a mind — just that a flat answer-fetcher does. Even philosophers who think machines can think tend to grant this one.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-inside-vantage-access-limited}{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}
gloss: facts that are realised \emph{in} a system's processing can nonetheless be unavailable \emph{to} that system's own self-report economy. The vehicle of cognition is not, in general, an object of cognition. So an outside observer with full instrumentation can know things about the system that the system cannot derive about itself — its internal vantage is strictly poorer than the external one. This is the \emph{architectural-access} member of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}}.

scope: an \emph{access} claim (about what the system can reach), not a \emph{phenomenal} one. It does not say the inaccessible facts are non-physical or that anything is left out of the world — only that they are out of the self-model's reach.

illustrations (LLM case): an LLM's token-probability distribution contains its own uncertainty, yet the model as a conversational agent is notoriously poorly calibrated about that uncertainty — the information is \emph{in} the logits but not available to its self-report. It cannot directly inspect its own activations unless externally instrumented. And it cannot register counterfactuals about how slightly different inputs would have changed its state when those differences make no difference to the output. In each case the fact is present in the implementation but absent from the accessible cognitive economy (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\emph{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}} marks how \emph{limited} that economy is).

who would deny it: someone who holds a sufficiently reflective system can in principle be given transparent access to all of its own state (full self-instrumentation), so the poverty is contingent and removable, not structural.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\emph{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}} (McGinn): that is the general possibility that a \emph{kind of mind} cannot form certain \emph{concepts}; this is the narrower, mechanism-level point that specific facts realised in a system are missing from its self-report workspace.

\begin{plainterms}
A thing can be true \emph{of} a system without being available \emph{to} it. Your brain runs on neurons firing, but you can't feel them fire; the fact is in you, not available to you. Likewise an AI's own logits already encode how uncertain it is — yet the model is famously bad at telling you its confidence, because that number isn't wired into what it can report on. Same for its own activations, and for input differences that never change its answer. So someone watching from outside, with the right tools, can know more about the system than the system can work out about itself.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{T}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible}{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Jackson}{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}
gloss: the conclusion of the Knowledge Argument, in its \emph{epistemic} form. Since Mary, knowing all the physical facts, still learns a truth on first seeing red, that truth — what it is like — is not deducible from the complete physical description. There are facts about experience the physical story leaves out.

scope: an \emph{epistemic} claim (about deducibility/knowledge), the immediate output of the Mary case. The step from here to the \emph{ontological} \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}} (the missing truths are about \emph{non-physical} facts) is a further move, resisted by physicalists who grant an epistemic gap (a new concept/guise) while denying any non-physical \emph{fact} — so the epistemic conclusion does not by itself settle the metaphysics.

who would deny it: physicalists who take the ability or old-fact-new-guise replies to \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}} — on which Mary acquires no new \emph{truth} at all, so nothing follows about deducibility.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} (role does not \emph{exhaust} the phenomenal) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}} (the ontological non-physicality thesis): this is the narrower epistemic claim that the phenomenal is not \emph{deducible} from the physical — the bridge between the Mary intuition and the metaphysical conclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
The lesson drawn from Mary: if someone could know \emph{every} physical fact and still learn something new on first seeing colour, then that something isn't contained in the physical facts — there are truths about what experience is \emph{like} that you can't derive from even a complete physics. (Note this is a claim about what's \emph{deducible/knowable}; getting from there to ``and therefore those truths are non-physical'' is an extra, separately disputed step.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenal-determinacy}{There is a determinate fact about one's own current phenomenal state}{Claim}{There is a determinate fact about one's own current phenomenal state}
gloss: from the first-person perspective, what one is now experiencing — and that one is experiencing at all — is definite, not something that varies with how an outside observer chooses to describe the system. Phenomenal consciousness presents itself as determinate and intrinsic, not as relative to a decomposition (cf. the ``intrinsic existence'' intuition Searle presses for consciousness, and IIT takes as an axiom).

scope: a determinacy claim about the phenomenal, neutral on what consciousness \emph{is}. It does NOT deny that the \emph{edges} of experience (peripheral, inattentive content) can be vague; it asserts that there is a fact of the matter about one's phenomenal state, rather than merely a fact-relative-to-a-carving.

who would deny it: a theorist willing to accept phenomenal \emph{indeterminacy} — that for some of one's experience there is no fact of the matter. That is the harder bullet a functionalist may bite to resist \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\emph{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\emph{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}}: that concerns the \emph{epistemic} authority of introspection; this concerns the \emph{determinacy of the phenomenal facts themselves}, however known.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a definite fact about what you're feeling right now — and that you're feeling anything at all — not something that shifts depending on how an outsider chooses to describe your brain. Your experience presents itself as just \emph{there}, settled. (It allows that the fuzzy \emph{edges} of experience — stuff at the corner of attention — can be vague; it only insists there's a genuine fact of the matter, not a make-it-up-as-you-carve-it situation.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation}{There is a natural property of the brain that explains consciousness}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{There is a natural property of the brain that explains consciousness}
gloss: there exists some natural property P of the brain in virtue of which the brain is the basis of consciousness. The link between neural process and experience is a \emph{natural} fact — not a miracle, not a non-physical addition, not brute inexplicability. (McGinn's ``transcendental naturalism``.)

scope: a realist/naturalist claim about the \emph{existence} of an explanation, not its accessibility. It explicitly does NOT say the explanation is dualistic, nor that we can grasp it (that is denied by \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\emph{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}}). It is the premise that keeps mysterianism on the \emph{naturalist} side rather than collapsing into dualism.

who would deny it: a property dualist or panpsychist who holds no ordinary natural property could close the gap; or an eliminativist/illusionist who denies there is a phenomenal explanandum at all.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}: that is about which \emph{substrates} can bear mind; this asserts only that \emph{some} natural brain property grounds consciousness, taking no stand on whether silicon could share it.

\entryhead{claim-heap-predicate-sharp}{There is a precise grain-count at which a heap becomes a non-heap}{Claim}{There is a precise grain-count at which a heap becomes a non-heap}
gloss: there is a precise boundary — some exact grain-count \texttt{n} — such that a collection of \texttt{n} grains is a heap and one of \texttt{n−1} is not. (Bivalence follows: with a cutoff, every case falls determinately on one side.)

scope: the naive realist picture of a vague predicate, isolated to its load-bearing commitment — the \emph{existence} of a cutoff. It says nothing about \emph{where} the boundary lies (locating it is the epistemicist's separate, unmet burden).

who would deny it: degree theorists (the verdict comes in degrees — non-discrete \texttt{Y}), supervaluationists / indeterminists (some cases lack a fact of the matter — \texttt{f} ill-defined), and deflationists (no real heap/non-heap distinction — \texttt{f} constant). Only the epistemicist \emph{keeps} this claim, by paying for it with an unknowable sharp boundary.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\emph{Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap}}: that is the step-insensitivity premise; this is the target the sorites refutes. \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\emph{The sorites paradox (the heap)}} attacks this claim using tolerance.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the stubborn common-sense idea that for ``heap'' there's some exact number — say, 1,847 grains — where you cross from heap to non-heap, even if nobody could ever tell you which number it is. Every pile then counts definitely as one or the other. It's the picture the heap paradox sets out to attack, and most people conclude \emph{something} about it has to give.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-cognitive-closure-exists}{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}
gloss: cognitive closure — a mind of a given kind can be permanently, constitutively unable to form certain concepts or grasp certain properties, the way a rat cannot grasp arithmetic or a dog the concept of an electron. Closure is not mere current ignorance; it is a limit fixed by the mind's conceptual endowment. (McGinn, ``Can We Solve the Mind-Body Problem?``, 1989.)

scope: a general claim in the epistemology of cognition. It does NOT, by itself, say \emph{which} problems are closed to humans — only that closure is a real category for minds in general (uncontroversial for non-human animals; the contested step is applying it to \emph{us}).

who would deny it: an epistemic optimist who holds that a concept-forming creature with language and science has open-ended representational reach, so that ``closure'' never applies to \emph{human} inquiry even if it does to animals.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\emph{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}}: this is the general possibility of closure; that is the specific (and contested) claim that the psychophysical property is closed to us.

\entryhead{concept-materialism-types}{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}
primary definition: Chalmers' taxonomy (Chalmers, ``Consciousness and Its Place in Nature``, 2003; \textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}) of \emph{physicalist} responses to the hard problem (\hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{The hard problem of consciousness}}), sorted by how they treat the \textbf{epistemic gap} between physical truths and phenomenal truths.

senses:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{type-A materialism} — \textbf{denies the epistemic gap}. There is no further explanandum once the functions are explained: zombies are inconceivable, Mary learns no new fact. Includes analytic functionalism, behaviourism, eliminativism, and \textbf{illusionism} (\hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}}).
  \item \emph{type-B materialism} — \textbf{accepts the epistemic gap but denies the ontological gap}. Phenomenal and physical truths are linked by identities that are \emph{necessary but knowable only a posteriori}, so a zombie is conceivable yet impossible (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}} is denied as a guide — \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\emph{Conceivability is not a reliable guide to possibility (the a-posteriori-necessity objection)}}). The gap is explained, not closed, by the phenomenal-concept strategy (\hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}}).
  \item \emph{type-C materialism} — concedes the gap looks unbridgeable \emph{now} but holds it is closable \textbf{in principle}. Chalmers argues it is unstable, collapsing on reflection into type-A or type-B.
\end{itemize}

the non-materialist remainder (for orientation): the same scheme continues into \emph{type-D} (interactionist dualism) and \emph{type-E} (epiphenomenalism) — see \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — and \emph{type-F} (Russellian monism / panprotopsychism) — see \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}. Type-A/B/C are the \emph{physicalist} options; D/E/F keep consciousness fundamental.

the dispute it feeds: it labels the positions arrayed against the anti-physicalist thought experiments (\hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\emph{The zombie / conceivability argument against physicalism}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\emph{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}}). Most live replies to those are type-A (deny the intuition) or type-B (grant it, deny the modal/ontological force).

\begin{plainterms}
A cheat-sheet for the ways a ``it's all physical'' theorist can answer the hard problem. \textbf{Type-A:} deny there's any real puzzle — once you've explained what the brain \emph{does}, there's nothing left to explain (the ``you only \emph{think} there's an extra feel'' camp). \textbf{Type-B:} admit it \emph{feels} like there's an unexplained extra, but insist that's just how our concepts work — experience really \emph{is} some physical process, the way water really is H₂O even though it didn't have to feel that way. \textbf{Type-C:} ``we can't bridge it yet, but we will'' — which, Chalmers argues, quietly turns into A or B once you push on it. (Beyond these are the non-physical options — dualism, Russellian monism, panpsychism.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-type-identity}{Type-identity theory}{Position}{Type-identity theory}
Mental-state \emph{types} are identical to brain-state types (\hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types]{\emph{Each type of mental state is identical to a type of brain state}}), discovered a posteriori as ``water = H₂O'' was (Place 1956, Smart 1959, Feigl). It answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} by \emph{identity} — there is nothing to bridge once the identities are in hand — and answers \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Are mental states substrate-independent?}} in the negative: because a mental type just is a specific \emph{physical} type, it is not freely multiply realisable, so it \texttt{rejects} \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}. As an a-posteriori-identity physicalism it is the seed of Chalmers' \textbf{type-B} (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}); accordingly it grants the conceivability of a zombie but \texttt{rejects} \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}} (conceivability is no guide to possibility here).

what distinguishes it from neighbouring views: unlike \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism (about mental states)}} it identifies mind with a physical \emph{type}, not a realiser-neutral \emph{role} — which is exactly why Putnam's multiple-realizability objection pushed the field from identity theory to functionalism. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} it is reductive-but-realist: the states exist and are physical.

\begin{plainterms}
The original ``mind is the brain'' theory: each kind of mental state simply \emph{is} a kind of brain state, the way water turned out to \emph{be} H₂O — a discovery, not a definition. So there's no leftover mystery (pain just is that brain process), but there's a price: if pain \emph{is} a specific brain state, then something with a very different make-up (silicon, an alien) couldn't have \emph{that} state — which is the famous objection that gave rise to functionalism.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{U}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-unforgeability-is-not-flesh}{Unforgeability is not flesh — the prudential argument favours unforgeability, not biology}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unforgeability is not flesh — the prudential argument favours unforgeability, not biology}
inference form: undercutting by overreach — grant the prudential premises, deny that they reach the \emph{flesh} conclusion. It targets the identification, in \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion]{\emph{Prudence favours resting moral standing on currently-unforgeable biology rather than on forgeable behavioural signs}}, of the prudent criterion with biology.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (\hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\emph{Biological substrate cannot currently be counterfeited to the standard required to fake suffering}}) Biology's resistance to counterfeit is a \emph{contingent, time-indexed} empirical fact (``currently``), not a necessary feature — it is why the argument it serves is prudential rather than metaphysical. ∴ (\hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-tracks-unforgeability-not-flesh]{\emph{What prudence favours is the hardest-to-forge sign, not biology specifically}}) The property doing the prudential work is \emph{unforgeability as such}; biology has no privileged standing beyond contingently being the unforgeable sign. A non-biological sign that became at least as unforgeable would satisfy the same rationale and displace flesh. So prudence does not favour resting moral standing on \emph{biology} in particular — only on whatever is hardest to fake — and \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion]{\emph{Prudence favours resting moral standing on currently-unforgeable biology rather than on forgeable behavioural signs}} overreaches in writing \emph{flesh} into the conclusion.
\end{enumerate}

what it does and does not do: it leaves the \emph{caution-under-uncertainty} core of \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion]{\emph{Prudence favours resting moral standing on currently-unforgeable biology rather than on forgeable behavioural signs}} intact (a forgeable sign is a poor criterion); it attacks only the slide from ``unforgeable'' to ``flesh.'' It therefore also blocks any read of that claim as support for \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} beyond defeasible, contingent grounds.

weakest premise: the tacit step that a non-biological sign \emph{could} in fact reach biology's standard of unforgeability. If biology were not merely currently but \emph{necessarily} the only unforgeable mark of a mind, ``track unforgeability'' and ``track flesh'' could not come apart and the objection would dissolve — so a defender of the flesh criterion presses exactly there.

\begin{plainterms}
The cautious ``demand the hard-to-fake mark'' argument is sound as far as it goes — but it doesn't earn the jump to \emph{biology}. Biology only counts because, right now, it happens to be hard to counterfeit; that's a fact about today's technology, not a law. So what the argument really backs is ``rest standing on whatever is hardest to fake,'' and if a non-biological sign ever got that forgery-proof, the same caution would favour it. Resting the rule on \emph{flesh} specifically claims more than the prudence licenses.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-cosmos-is-fundamental-subject}{The universe as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Goff}{The universe as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject}
gloss: the cosmos itself is a single fundamental conscious subject, and individual minds are \emph{derivative} — aspects or dissociations \emph{of} the universal consciousness rather than building blocks of it (cosmopsychism: Goff, Shani, Nagasawa; a priority-monist cousin of panpsychism). Grounding runs top-down, from the whole to its parts.

scope: combines priority monism (the cosmos is the one fundamental concrete object) with experiential fundamentality. Its characteristic problem is the \emph{de}combination problem: how does the universal subject give rise to many distinct, bounded subjects like us?

who would deny it: (constitutive micro-)panpsychists (the fundamental subjects are micro, not cosmic); physicalists and dualists; and those who reject priority monism.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\emph{Consciousness is fundamental, present even in the simplest physical entities}} (constitutive panpsychism builds macro- minds \emph{up} from micro-experiences; cosmopsychism derives them \emph{down} from the whole — combination vs decombination).

\entryhead{concept-unobservability-in-principle}{Unobservability in principle (intersubjective vs. absolute)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unobservability in principle (intersubjective vs. absolute)}
primary definition: a difference is \emph{unobservable in principle} when no possible observation discriminates between its obtaining and its not obtaining — not ``hard to measure,'' but such that every accessible datum comes out the same either way. The phrase hides a quantifier-domain question that turns out to carry an entire dispute: which acts count as \textbf{observations}?

senses:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{intersubjective} — the quantifier ranges over third-person, instrument-mediated, publicly checkable observation: probes, readouts, behaviour, reports-as-data. This is the sense in which the classic in-principle unobservables are unobservable (the bare quantum amplitude, of which only the squared modulus ever shows up; the incomputable number no procedure prints), and it is the native sense of the standards built for them — the Peirce–James pragmatic maxim, Carnap's fruitfulness test for external questions, Smart's shaving of nomological danglers (\hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\emph{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}} inherits this lineage). On this sense, one subject's experience is unobservable in principle \emph{to every other subject} — and whether the subject's own introspective access adds a further ``observation'' is left open, or denied by treating introspective deliverances as one more kind of report.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{absolute} — the quantifier ranges over every epistemic access of any subject, \emph{including the bearer's own first-person acquaintance} (\hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access (privileged access)}}). On this sense, a posit is unobservable in principle only if no one — not even the one whose state it is — ever observes it. The view pressed under Searle's ``first-person ontology'' holds that a conscious state is the opposite of absolutely unobservable: it exists only as experienced by its subject, so it cannot exist \emph{unobserved}; what remains is third-person epistemic privacy, not unobservability as such.
\end{itemize}

the live dispute it feeds: whether the coherence-plus-usefulness standard (\hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\emph{For a concept positing differences unobservable in principle, the only available standards of assessment are coherence plus usefulness}}) reaches the ``real feel.'' The idle-column verdict of \hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\emph{The ``really feels vs merely reports'' residue is unobservable in principle — judged by coherence and usefulness, the relativized claim stands and the residue is idle}} goes through if ``observation'' is read intersubjectively (or if introspection delivers representations rather than observations); the antecedent-failure reply of \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\emph{The ``real feel'' is first-person observed, not unobservable — the idleness charge presupposes that observation is third-person observation}} goes through if first-person acquaintance counts as observation, since then the feel is observed — by exactly one observer per case — and the standard's antecedent fails. Splitting the senses shows that ``the feel is unobservable in principle'' can be affirmed in the intersubjective sense and denied in the absolute sense without contradiction; the substantive residue is \hyperref[entry:question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation]{\textsc{Is first-person acquaintance with one's own experience a genuine observation, or itself a representation?}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Some ideas are called ``untestable even in principle'' — no experiment could ever tell whether they're true. But the word ``test'' (or ``observe``) is doing hidden work: observed \emph{by whom}? One reading counts only public evidence — instruments, measurements, things any third party could check. Another reading also counts what each person gets from the inside: your own direct awareness of your own pain. A feeling is hidden from every outside observer forever — so on the first reading it looks ``unobservable in principle.'' But its owner, arguably, observes it constantly — so on the second reading it's the most observed thing there is. Two debaters can talk straight past each other here, one saying ``unobservable!'' (publicly) and the other ``always observed!'' (by its owner), without actually contradicting each other. The real question left once the senses are separated: does that inside awareness count as \emph{observing} something, or is it just the brain making one more report to itself? That is recorded as its own question (\hyperref[entry:question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation]{\textsc{Is first-person acquaintance with one's own experience a genuine observation, or itself a representation?}}).
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{V}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-introspective-gap-varieties}{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}
primary definition: a taxonomy distinguishing four things that can be meant when a self-modelling system ``cannot explain its own experience to itself,'' kept apart so that a felt explanatory gap is not automatically read as the metaphysical hard problem. The four are mutually compatible — a given case may exhibit several at once — but they have different sources and different remedies.

senses:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{ontological gap} — even given a complete physical/computational account, why is there experience at all? This is the hard problem proper (\hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{The hard problem of consciousness}}); the anti-physicalist reading treats the residue as lying in the world (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}).
  \item \emph{architectural-access gap} — the system cannot reach the internal variables that \emph{would} explain it: facts realised in its own processing are not available to its self-report economy (\hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\emph{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}}). A gap in the \emph{self}, not in the world.
  \item \emph{conceptual-translation gap} — the system possesses both a mechanistic and an introspective description but has no internal route from one vocabulary to the other; the bridge that exists for an outside theorist is not traversable from inside (\hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\emph{An explanation is intuitively satisfying for a system only if it is traversable by that system's own cognitive operations}}).
  \item \emph{training-contamination gap} — the system merely reproduces human hard-problem discourse it was trained on, with no underlying gap of its own. The confound an experiment must control for.
\end{itemize}

the dispute it feeds: it structures \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}} (which kind, if any, an introspecting LLM would exhibit) and lets the architectural/conceptual readings be stated as a \emph{mechanism} for McGinn-style cognitive closure (\hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\emph{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\emph{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}}) without asserting an ontological residue.

\begin{plainterms}
When something can't ``explain its own experience to itself,'' that can mean four very different things, and they get muddled together. (1) \textbf{Ontological:} even after you know everything about the machinery, why is there a felt experience at all? — the genuine hard problem. (2) \textbf{Architectural- access:} the facts that would explain it are inside the system but it can't read them (like not being able to feel your own neurons fire). (3) \textbf{Conceptual-translation:} it has the mechanical story \emph{and} the first-person story but no inner bridge between them — a translation an outside scientist can make but the system itself can't. (4) \textbf{Contamination:} it's just parroting things humans have written about the hard problem. Most of the interest is in telling (2) and (3) apart from (1) and (4).
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-virtual-realism}{Virtual realism (world-indexed properties of a simulation)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Virtual realism (world-indexed properties of a simulation)}
\textbf{Virtual realism} (Chalmers 2022) holds that the objects and events inside a computer simulation are \emph{real} — they are concrete computational/data structures with genuine causal powers — and that a property predicated of a simulated thing is true or false \textbf{relative to a world-index}. A simulated rainstorm is not wet \emph{in our world} (no molecule in our world is wetted by it), but it is wet \emph{in the simulated world}: relative to that world, the sim-water has the sim-analogues of surface tension and adhesion, and it sim-soaks whatever sim-surfaces it falls on. ``Simulated X is not really X'' is true under our-world indexing and false under simulation-world indexing; the slogan is ambiguous until the index is fixed.

A second distinction the concept rides on: for a property like wetness there is (i) \textbf{physical wetness} — a third-person, dispositional/structural matter of water's behaviour on surfaces, fixed by surface tension and chemical adhesion — and (ii) \textbf{the sensation of wetness} — the first-person feel an experiencing subject undergoes. In our world (i) reliably causes (ii) in a suitably wired human. Inside a simulation, if the relevant physics is simulated finely enough, the sim-analogue of (i) obtains relative to the sim-world; whether the sim-analogue of (ii) also obtains — whether a sim-agent \emph{feels} sim-wet — is the further, contested question of whether the simulation's organisation suffices for phenomenal consciousness, i.e. the question of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}.

This concept feeds the dispute over the ``a simulated storm isn't wet'' move used against machine consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:argument-storm-analogy-conflates-world-index]{\emph{The ``simulated storm isn't wet'' analogy conflates world-indices and the physical/phenomenal senses of wetness, so it cannot settle machine consciousness}}, attacking the way the analogy is deployed in \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\emph{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}}). It does \textbf{not} by itself assert that sim-agents are conscious — only that the rain example, once its world-index and its physical/phenomenal senses are separated, no longer settles that question.

\begin{plainterms}
When you simulate rain on a computer, is the rain ``wet``? It depends what you mean. It doesn't wet anything in \emph{our} world — your desk stays dry. But \emph{inside the simulated world}, the simulated water has all the simulated physics of real water: it has the stand-ins for surface tension and stickiness, and it soaks the simulated ground. So ``simulated rain isn't wet'' is only half a sentence — you have to say \emph{wet for whom, in which world}. Real-world: no. Sim-world: yes.

There's a second catch. ``Wet'' can mean two different things: the plain physical fact about how water behaves on a surface (measurable, third-person), and the \emph{feeling} of wetness in someone's mind. In real life the first causes the second. In a simulation, if you copy the physics finely enough, you get the first (relative to the sim-world). Whether you also get the second — whether a character \emph{inside} the simulation actually \emph{feels} wet — is the big open question of whether a mind can run on non-brain hardware at all (see \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}). The point of this concept is just to keep those questions apart, because the catchy line ``a simulated storm isn't wet'' quietly runs them together.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{W}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-mary-gains-old-fact-new-guise}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a new phenomenal concept of a physical fact she already knew}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Loar}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a new phenomenal concept of a physical fact she already knew}
gloss: the \textbf{old-fact / new-guise} reply (Loar 1990 ``Phenomenal States``; anticipated by Horgan 1984). One and the same fact can be grasped under different concepts — as Hesperus-facts and Phosphorus-facts are the same facts under different modes of presentation. Inside the room Mary knew the relevant brain-state fact under theoretical, third-person concepts; on release she acquires a \emph{phenomenal concept} of that very state — a first-person, experience-derived way of grasping it. New knowledge in the sense of a new guise; zero new facts.

scope: unlike the ability and acquaintance replies, this one \emph{grants} that Mary gains genuinely propositional knowledge — she can now think thoughts she could not think before. It denies only that the new thoughts have new facts as their objects. The physicalist thus keeps both the intuition and the ontology, at the price of a substantive theory of phenomenal concepts.

who would deny it: the Knowledge-Argument proponent, who argues that if the phenomenal concept presents its referent under a substantive phenomenal \emph{property}, that property is the non-physical residue reintroduced (the ``guise'' itself wants a place in the world); deniers of the phenomenal-concept strategy generally, e.g. via \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}}; and ability theorists, who think no new concept is needed to explain the gain.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}} because both deny Mary gains propositional knowledge, while this claim accepts it and relocates the novelty from the fact known to the concept used. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\emph{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}} because that is an \emph{attack} on the cost structure of this strategy, not a version of it.

\begin{plainterms}
``The Morning Star'' and ``the Evening Star'' name the same planet — someone could know all about one name and still feel they learn something when told about the other, yet astronomy isn't missing any object. On this view Mary is in the same boat: the brain state she studied for years and the redness she finally experiences are one fact under two labels. Stepping outside hands her the second label — the from-the-inside one — not a new piece of reality.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Lewis}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}
gloss: the \textbf{ability hypothesis} (Lewis, ``What Experience Teaches``, 1988; Nemirow 1990). When Mary first sees red she gains know-\emph{how} — the abilities to recognise red by sight, to imagine red, to remember seeing red — and nothing else. Abilities are had, not true; so her gain involves no new \emph{fact} about the world, and physicalism is untouched.

scope: grants the datum of \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\emph{On first seeing red, Mary learns something she did not know before}} under one reading — she does come to ``know what it is like'' — but analyses knowing-what-it-is-like as a bundle of abilities rather than propositional knowledge. It does not deny that experience teaches; it denies that what it teaches is information.

who would deny it: anyone who holds that knowing what red is like has genuine propositional content over and above abilities — e.g. that Mary can use her new knowledge in inference (``if seeing red is like \emph{this}, then seeing orange is probably similar``), which abilities alone do not explain; and acquaintance theorists (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}}), who agree the gain is not factual but deny it is mere know-how.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}} because that posits a third epistemic category (a direct cognitive relation to the experience); this one needs only the familiar know-that / know-how distinction.

\begin{plainterms}
When Mary finally sees red, what does she get? On this view: skills, not information. She can now spot red things at a glance, picture red with her eyes closed, recall what the tomato looked like. That's like learning to ride a bike — you gain an ability, not a new entry for the encyclopedia. And if no new fact was learned, the story can't show that science left any facts out.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-mary-gains-acquaintance}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Conee}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}
gloss: the \textbf{acquaintance hypothesis} (Conee 1994). Knowledge by acquaintance — a maximally direct cognitive relation to a thing — is a third category, distinct from both knowledge of facts and know-how. Before release Mary knew every fact \emph{about} the experience of red but was not acquainted \emph{with} it; on release she meets it. Becoming acquainted with something is not learning a truth, so no fact escapes the physical story.

scope: like the ability hypothesis, this grants that Mary's epistemic situation genuinely improves and denies only that the improvement is factual. Unlike the ability hypothesis, it does not reduce her gain to skills: acquaintance is its own kind of standing (one can be acquainted with a colour experience even if too unimaginative to recreate it later).

who would deny it: the Knowledge-Argument proponent, for whom acquaintance with red puts Mary in a position to know new \emph{truths} (what seeing red is like) — so acquaintance cannot be factually innocent; ability theorists (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}}), who think know-how covers the gain without a third category; and anyone suspicious that ``acquaintance'' is a label for the phenomenon rather than an analysis of it.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}} because acquaintance is a relation to a particular experience, not a bundle of capacities — the two replies agree on ``no new fact'' but disagree on what the gain \emph{is}. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\emph{First-person acquaintance with a subject's experience is necessary for justified attribution of moral status to it}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\emph{One has no first-person acquaintance with a machine's putative experience}} because those deploy acquaintance in the epistemology of \emph{other} minds (moral-status warrant); this concerns what acquaintance contributes within one's own case.

\begin{plainterms}
You can know everything about a person — biography, habits, voice — without ever having met them. Meeting them adds something real, but not a new entry in their biography. On this view that's Mary's situation with the colour red: stepping outside, she finally \emph{meets} the experience she'd only read about. Real gain, no new fact — so nothing was missing from her science.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-mary-gains-self-locating-knowledge}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is self-locating knowledge of how she herself reacts to red, not a new general fact}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Perry}{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is self-locating knowledge of how she herself reacts to red, not a new general fact}
gloss: the \textbf{indexical / self-locating reply} (Perry 2001, \emph{Knowledge, Possibility, and Consciousness}). The difference between seeing red and reading the complete neurophysiology of seeing red is that in the former case Mary also learns how \emph{she herself} reacts to red light — truths of the form ``\emph{this} is what seeing red does to \emph{me}``, ``\emph{I} am now in \emph{this} state``. These are genuine facts, and genuinely new to her — but they are facts about her own situation, located from the inside, not new general facts about the world. Physics is in the business of general facts; it was never supposed to deliver self-locating ones.

scope: unlike the ability (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}}) and acquaintance (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is acquaintance with the experience, not knowledge of a new fact}}) replies, this grants that Mary learns \emph{facts} — it deflates their kind, not their factuality. The non-deducibility of self-locating truths from any objective description is a banal, physicalism-friendly limitation: a being who knew every objective fact could still fail to know ``I am here now'' (Lewis's two-gods case, ``Attitudes De Dicto and De Se``, 1979). It does not deny that Mary's brain-particulars are physical; it locates the novelty entirely in the indexical mode of access.

who would deny it: the Knowledge-Argument proponent, via the \textbf{collapse objection} (Chalmers, Tye): ordinary self-locating ignorance is ignorance of who, where, or when you are, and Mary has none — she knows she is Mary, in this room, today; so the residual ``indexical'' gap must be carried by a demonstrative concept of the experience itself (``\emph{this} kind of state``), at which point the reply collapses into \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a new phenomenal concept of a physical fact she already knew}}. Ability theorists deny the gain is factual at all; the hard-line reply (\hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-nothing-new]{\emph{Mary, genuinely knowing all the physical facts, would learn nothing on first seeing red}}) denies there is any gain to classify.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-old-fact-new-guise]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a new phenomenal concept of a physical fact she already knew}} because that posits the \emph{same} fact under a new concept, while this posits \emph{new} truths — only of a self-locating kind that no objective description, physical or otherwise, could ever entail. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\emph{What Mary acquires on first seeing red is a set of abilities, not knowledge of a new fact}} because it rejects the abilities/facts dichotomy on which that reply rests: learning how one's own body engages the world is fact-learning (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowhow-is-knowledge-that]{\emph{Knowing how to do something is a species of propositional knowledge}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Compare learning to ride a bike. The physics of bicycles isn't what you acquire — you learn how \emph{your} body and \emph{this} bike work together. Those are real facts, but facts about your personal situation, not additions to the science of bicycles. On this view Mary's first sight of red is the same kind of event: she learns how she herself responds to red light — true, new-to-her, and absent from her books — yet no general fact about the world was missing from those books. A map can be complete and still not tell you \emph{you are here}; that's not a defect in the map.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-prudence-tracks-unforgeability-not-flesh}{What prudence favours is the hardest-to-forge sign, not biology specifically}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{What prudence favours is the hardest-to-forge sign, not biology specifically}
gloss: the prudential case for resting moral standing on a hard-to-fake mark favours \emph{unforgeability} as such — whatever sign is currently hardest to game — and gives biology no privileged role beyond its being, contingently, that sign. Because biology's resistance to counterfeit is time-indexed (\hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\emph{Biological substrate cannot currently be counterfeited to the standard required to fake suffering}}), a non-biological sign that became at least as unforgeable would satisfy the same rationale and displace flesh.

scope: a claim about \emph{which property the prudential argument actually selects} (unforgeability), not about which substrates can bear minds. It does not deny that biology is presently the unforgeable option; it denies that the prudential reasoning licenses fixing on \emph{flesh} rather than on unforgeability-in-general.

who would deny it: someone who holds that biology is not merely \emph{currently} but \emph{necessarily} the only unforgeable mark of a mind (so that ``track unforgeability'' and ``track flesh'' cannot come apart); or a proteocentrist who wants the criterion tied to carbon for metaphysical, not prudential, reasons.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\emph{Biological substrate cannot currently be counterfeited to the standard required to fake suffering}} because that is the empirical, time-indexed fact that biology is hard to fake \emph{now}; this is the normative/structural point that the prudential rule tracks unforgeability \emph{as such}, so flesh has no standing beyond that contingent fact.

\begin{plainterms}
The ``play it safe, demand the hard-to-fake mark'' argument doesn't actually single out \emph{flesh} — it singles out \emph{whatever is hardest to fake}. Biology only qualifies because, for now, it happens to be hard to counterfeit. If some non-biological sign ever became just as forgery-proof, the very same caution would back \emph{it} instead. So the cautious policy is about unforgeability, not about being made of meat.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{question-what-does-mary-learn}{What, if anything, does Mary gain when she first sees red?}{Question}{What, if anything, does Mary gain when she first sees red?}
the issue: the Knowledge Argument (\hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}}) turns entirely on how to describe what happens to Mary at the moment she leaves the black-and-white room. Everyone agrees \emph{something} changes. The dispute is over the right category for that something — and whether it is the kind of something that physicalism cannot accommodate.

key terms the answers trade on:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{propositional knowledge} (knowledge-\emph{that}): grasp of a fact, the kind of thing that is true or false. Only a gain of this kind threatens physicalism directly.
  \item \textbf{know-how}: abilities and skills, which are had rather than true.
  \item \textbf{acquaintance}: a direct cognitive relation to a thing (knowing \emph{it}, not knowing \emph{that}), argued to be a third category reducible to neither of the above.
  \item \textbf{mode of presentation / guise}: the way a fact is grasped. One fact can be known under two guises, so ``new knowledge'' need not mean ``new fact``.
\end{itemize}

sub-issues that may deserve their own question nodes: whether the epistemic conclusion (\hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\emph{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}}), if granted, even supports an ontological anti-physicalism — the epistemic→ontological step is a separate dispute from the categorisation dispute above.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary is a scientist who knows every physical fact about colour but has lived her whole life in black and white. One day she steps out and sees red. The question: what exactly did she just get? A brand-new fact the physics books left out? A new skill, like learning to ride a bike? A first meeting with something she'd only read about? Or just a new way of grasping a fact she already knew? Which description is right decides whether the story proves anything about the limits of physical science.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{question-llm-own-explanatory-gap}{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}
precise statement of the issue: take a model trained with \textbf{no} exposure to philosophy-of-mind or consciousness discourse (controlling the \emph{training-contamination} confound; see \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}}), but with limited functional introspective access to its own states (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\emph{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}}). Teach it the mechanics of how it works — transformer architecture, activations, attention, logits, token probabilities. Then ask: does that mechanistic self-knowledge \emph{fully explain}, from its own side, how it perceives the world and its own thoughts?

the two outcomes:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Yes — deflation.} The model finds no residual explanandum: its ``perceptions'' are just input-conditioned latent representations made available to downstream prediction and self-report, its introspective ``feelings'' just compressed summaries of its own processing. A computational analogue of illusionism / strong functionalism — the apparent gap dissolves from the inside.
  \item \textbf{No — a gap remains.} The model reports that the mechanics do not explain why its accessible states present themselves as they do, robustly and not traceably to contamination.
\end{itemize}

a \emph{separate} question, only if the answer is \textbf{No}: \emph{why} does the gap arise?
