That is a distinct issue from whether it arises, with its own candidate answers (sorted by \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}}: an \emph{ontological} residue, an \emph{architectural-access} limit, a \emph{conceptual-translation} failure). One such candidate — proposed tentatively — is recorded as its own node, \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\emph{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}}; it is a separate conjecture layered on the ``No,'' not a third outcome of the experiment. Note that a ``No'' is not by itself evidence of phenomenal consciousness (\hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness vs. phenomenal consciousness}}).

why it bears on the governing issue: a sub-issue of \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}} — it asks whether the hard-problem \emph{form} arises in a system whose only relevant property is self-modelling under restricted access, with the human cultural inheritance stripped out.

how it could be adjudicated: vary internal-state access across models and watch whether a ``No'' appears and whether it then \emph{shrinks} — e.g. (A) a baseline with no consciousness literature; (B) given only transformer mechanics; (C) trained to report specific activation-level facts; (D) given external probes of its own activations at inference; (E) given calibrated access to its own token probabilities. If a ``No'' shrinks as access improves, that supports the architectural hypothesis for it; if it persists under rich access, that is more analogous to the human hard problem; if it appears only when philosophy-of-mind text is in training, the phenomenon is contamination.

tentative remark (not a claim or edge): a \emph{deflation} outcome might in turn \emph{inform} attempts on the human hard problem — a clean artificial case of ``I know the mechanism but cannot see how it becomes this appearance.'' Recorded as exploratory only; the cluster takes no \texttt{attacks} stance on \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}} or \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Build an AI that has never read a word about consciousness but can introspect a little on its own insides, and teach it exactly how it works as a machine. Then ask: ``Does knowing all that explain how your own thoughts seem to you?'' Two answers are possible: \textbf{yes} — ``no mystery, it's just my processing summarized'' (the gap dissolves), or \textbf{no} — ``I understand the machinery but still can't see how it becomes \emph{this}.'' Only if the answer is \emph{no} does a second, separate question open up: \emph{why} not? — and one proposed answer to that is recorded as its own claim (\hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\emph{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}}), kept apart from the yes/no question here. You could even tell the cases apart experimentally, by giving models more or less access to their own internals and seeing whether the ``no'' shrinks.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{Z}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-zombie-conceivability}{The zombie / conceivability argument against physicalism}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{The zombie / conceivability argument against physicalism}
inference form: thought-experiment / modal argument — a conceived case plus a conceivability→possibility bridge yields a metaphysical conclusion.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Scenario + Intuition} (\hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\emph{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}}): a microphysical duplicate wholly lacking phenomenal consciousness is ideally conceivable.
  \item \emph{Bridge} (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}}): ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility. ∴ A zombie is metaphysically possible; so the complete physical facts do not necessitate the facts about experience — phenomenal consciousness is over and above the physical (\hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\emph{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}}). This \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\emph{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}} (a fortiori: if not even necessitated, then not exhausted by role) and \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\emph{Mental states are substrate-independent}}'s uniform, phenomenal-covering reading.
\end{itemize}

weakest premise: \textbf{the bridge} (\hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\emph{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}}). The \textbf{type-B physicalist} reply (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}; raises the \emph{thought-experiment pattern}'s \textbf{bridge critical question}, CQ-bridge) grants conceivability but denies possibility, citing necessary a posteriori identities; the \textbf{phenomenal-concept strategy} (\hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\emph{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}}) explains the conceivability as an artefact of phenomenal concepts. A type-A reply (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}}) instead presses the \emph{thought-experiment pattern}'s \emph{coherence} critical question (\textbf{CQ-coherence}), denying \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\emph{A microphysical duplicate of a conscious being that wholly lacks phenomenal consciousness is conceivable}} outright (the description is only apparently coherent). Landing spot is also disputed: the same premises are read by some not as dualism but as \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} (the physical's intrinsic nature is experiential), avoiding the epiphenomenalism that dogs \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\emph{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}}: that runs an \emph{evidential/abductive} likelihood comparison on the persistence of the gap and only attacks the \emph{uniform scope} of substrate-independence; this is a \emph{modal} argument for the stronger conclusion that the phenomenal is non-physical. (Explanatory-gap's premise already leans on zombie conceivability; this node factors that move out.)

\begin{plainterms}
Picture an atom-for-atom copy of a conscious person that has no inner experience at all — a ``zombie.'' Step one: that seems coherently imaginable. Step two (the controversial bit): if something is truly, coherently imaginable, it's genuinely possible. Put them together and a zombie is possible — which means the physical facts alone don't force experience to exist, so consciousness must be something extra beyond the physical. The argument lives or dies on step two: opponents say you can imagine plenty of things that turn out impossible (like water \emph{not} being H2O), so imaginability isn't proof of possibility.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\end{document}
